**Step 3**: Block 2 — Plunge to cutting depth:
```
N20 G01 Z-2.0 F50;
```
*Explanation: Linear feed down to Z=-2 mm at 50 mm/min.*

**Step 4**: Block 3 — Begin facing cut:
```
N30 G01 X100.0 F150;
```
*Explanation: Linear feed along X to X=100 mm at 150 mm/min.*

**Step 5**: Block 4 — Retract safely:
```
N40 G00 Z50.0;
```
*Explanation: Rapid retract to safe Z height.*

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how sequence numbers N10 → N20 → N30 → N40 use step of 10. If we need to insert a coolant-on block between N30 and N40, we can use `N35 M08;` without disturbing anything.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Right-Hand Rule Coordinate System (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["X AXIS\nThumb Pointing\nRight"] --> node2["Y AXIS\nIndex Finger\nPointing Inward"]
    node2 --> node3["Z AXIS\nMiddle Finger\nPointing Upward"]
    node3 --> node1
    node4["LINEAR AXES\nX Y Z\nLinear Motion"] --> node5["ROTATIONAL AXES\nA B C\nAbout X Y Z"]
    node5 --> node6["RIGHT HAND RULE\nStandard Reference\nISO 841"]
    node6 --> node4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram above represents the **canonical right-hand rule** used to define all 6 axes of an NC machine. This is the single most important visual to remember for KTU examinations — examiners expect students to either draw this rule or describe it in words.

## 4.2 Manual Part Programming Workflow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START\nReceive Engineering\nDrawing of Part"]
    stepB["STUDY DRAWING\nIdentify Geometry\nDatums Tolerances"]
    stepC["SELECT FIXTURING\nWork Holding Device\nSetup Strategy"]
    stepD["DEFINE WCS\nWorkpiece Zero\nG54 G55 G56 G57"]
    stepE["SELECT TOOLS\nT01 T02 T03\nAssign Numbers"]
    stepF["PLAN SEQUENCE\nFacing Roughing\nFinishing"]
    stepG["CALCULATE COORDS\nX Y Z I J K\nManually"]
    stepH["SELECT PARAMETERS\nSpindle S\nFeed F"]
    stepI["WRITE PROGRAM\nWord Address Format\nBlock by Block"]
    stepJ["VERIFY\nDry Run\nSimulation"]
    stepK["PRODUCTION\nRun on Machine\nMonitor"]
    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL["FINISHED PART\nQuality Check\nShip"]
```

## 4.3 Sequence Number Logic and Branching Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    seqA["MAIN PROGRAM\nN10 START\nN20 MOVE\nN30 CUT\nN40 TOOL CHANGE\nN50 CONTINUE\nN60 END"]
    seqB["SUBROUTINE CALL\nM98 P0200"]
    seqC["SUBROUTINE O0200\nN1 BEGIN\nN2 OPERATION\nN3 RETURN\nM99"]
    seqD["JUMP / BRANCH\nGO TO N50\nM99 P0050"]
    seqE["OPERATOR SEARCH\nTYPE N50\nDISPLAY BLOCK"]
    seqA --> seqB
    seqB --> seqC
    seqC --> seqA
    seqA --> seqD
    seqA --> seqE
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram shows the **three primary uses of sequence numbers** in production environments: subroutine identification (O0200), branching/jumping targets, and operator search references. Understanding this is a high-yield KTU topic.

## 4.4 Machine-Specific Axis Orientation (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    m1["VERTICAL MILLING\nZ is UP\nSpindle vertical\nA tilts about X\nB tilts about Y"]
    m2["HORIZONTAL MILLING\nZ points OUT\nSpindle horizontal\nB rotates about Y\nC rotates about Z"]
    m3["TURNING LATHE\nZ is along spindle\nX is radial\nC is spindle index"]
    m4["5 AXIS MACHINE\nLinear XYZ\nRotational ABC\nA is X rotation\nB is Y rotation\nC is Z rotation"]
    m5["STANDARD RULE\nLongest travel X\nZ along spindle\nY completes system"]
    m5 --> m1
    m5 --> m2
    m5 --> m3
    m5 --> m4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define NC Part Programming and State Any Two Differences Between Manual and Computer-Assisted Part Programming.**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | CO2 | Bloom: Remember/Understand | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

NC Part Programming is the process of preparing a detailed set of coded instructions in a standardized language (G-codes and M-codes per ISO 6983) that directs a CNC machine tool to perform all required machining operations on a workpiece to produce the finished component.

| Aspect | Manual Programming | Computer-Assisted Programming |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Method** | Hand-written on coding sheets using calculators and trig tables | Generated using CAM software (CATIA, NX, Mastercam) or APT language |
| **Skill Required** | Strong knowledge of codes, trigonometry, and geometry | Strong CAD/CAM software skills; less trigonometry |
| **Best For** | Simple 2-axis parts, turning, point-to-point drilling | Complex 3D contours, 5-axis surfaces, sculpted parts |
| **Time** | Time-consuming for complex parts | Fast even for complex geometry |
| **Error Rate** | Higher due to manual calculation | Lower due to software verification and simulation |

---

### **Question 2: What is a Sequence Number in NC Programming? Why is Step of 10 Recommended?**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | CO2 | Bloom: Remember/Understand | 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Sequence Number** (also called a block number or N-code) is an identifier assigned to each block of an NC program, written as the letter `N` followed by a numerical value (e.g., `N10`, `N20`, `N30`).

**Purpose of Sequence Numbers:**
- To allow the operator or controller to **search, locate, and reference** specific blocks in the program.
- To enable **branching and subroutine calls** (e.g., `M98 P0200` calls subroutine starting at O0200).
- To facilitate **editing and modification** during production debugging.

**Why Step of 10 is Recommended:**
Using numbering like N10, N20, N30... leaves **9 free slots** (N11–N19) between any two numbered blocks. This allows new blocks to be **inserted later without renumbering** the entire program. It is a **best-practice convention** that saves significant time during program maintenance and debugging.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1): Part Programming Fundamentals and NC Coordinate System**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | CO2 | CO3 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze | 14 Marks]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the NC Coordinate System as per ISO 841 Standard. State the Right-Hand Rule and Define All Six Axes (X, Y, Z, A, B, C) with a Neat Diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
The NC coordinate system is a standardized right-handed Cartesian system defined by ISO 841 (and equivalent EIA RS-267) to provide a universal reference frame for all CNC machine tools, regardless of manufacturer.

**The Right-Hand Rule:**
The right hand is held with the thumb, index finger, and middle finger mutually perpendicular.
- **Thumb** → points in the **+X direction**
- **Index finger** → points in the **+Y direction**
- **Middle finger** → points in the **+Z direction**

This rule is the foundation of all axis definitions. Any coordinate system that violates it is non-standard.

**Primary Linear Axes:**

| Axis | Definition | Direction Logic |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **X** | Axis with the **longest travel** (typically along the table for milling, radial for turning) | Movement that **increases** the workpiece dimension |
| **Y** | Axis perpendicular to X and Z | Forms a right-handed system with X and Z |
| **Z** | Coincident with the **spindle axis** | Movement that **increases** the distance from workpiece to tool |

**Rotational Axes (Per Linear Axis):**

| Axis | Rotation About | Positive Direction |
|------|----------------|-------------------|
| **A** | Rotation about X-axis | Right-hand rule around X |
| **B** | Rotation about Y-axis | Right-hand rule around Y |
| **C** | Rotation about Z-axis | Right-hand rule around Z |

**Machine-Specific Application:**
- For a **Vertical Machining Center**: Z is vertical (up = +Z), X is the longest table travel, Y is perpendicular.
- For a **Turning Center**: Z is along the spindle (away from chuck = +Z), X is radial (away from spindle axis = +X).
- For a **5-axis machine**: All three rotational axes A, B, and C are used simultaneously.

**Conclusion:**
The standardized axis system enables part programs to be portable across machines and manufacturers — a key advantage of NC technology.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] A Mild Steel Plate of Size 120 mm × 80 mm × 10 mm is to be Reduced to 110 mm × 70 mm × 8 mm by Face Milling. The Cutter Diameter is 60 mm. Calculate the Spindle Speed and Feed Rate, and Write the First 6 Blocks of the Manual Part Program Using Word Address Format.**

**Given Data:**
- Workpiece initial size: 120 × 80 × 10 mm
- Final size: 110 × 70 × 8 mm
- Cutter diameter $D = 60$ mm
- Material: Mild Steel
- Recommended cutting velocity for mild steel: $v = 30$ m/min
- Feed per tooth for mild steel: $f_z = 0.10$ mm/tooth
- Number of teeth: $Z = 6$
- Depth of cut for facing: 2 mm (to remove the 2 mm from top)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Spindle Speed**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot 60}$$

$$N = \dfrac{30000}{188.496} = 159.15 \text{ rpm}$$

Choose the nearest available machine speed: **N = 160 rpm**

**Step 2: Calculate Feed Rate**

$$F = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N = 0.10 \cdot 6 \cdot 160 = 96 \text{ mm/min}$$

Choose nearest available feed: **F = 100 mm/min**

**Step 3: Write the Program (Word Address Format)**

Assume program origin at the front-left corner of the final workpiece, on the top finished surface.

```
O0001 (FACE MILLING PROGRAM);
N10 G90 G54 G00 X-15.0 Y-15.0;   (Move to start, off the workpiece)
N20 Z25.0;                       (Rapid to safe Z height)
N30 M03 S160;                    (Spindle ON CW at 160 rpm)
N40 M08;                         (Coolant ON)
N50 G00 X0 Y0;                   (Position above front-left corner)
N60 G01 Z-2.0 F100;              (Plunge to cutting depth at 100 mm/min)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write `S160` (rpm implied) and `F100` (mm/min implied) with units stated in the calculation.
> 2. **Wrong sign of Z** — Z-2.0 means going DOWN into the workpiece (below the program zero). Students often write `Z2.0` which moves UP and misses the cut.
> 3. **Missing safe Z approach** — Always retract to a positive Z (e.g., 25 mm) before any rapid motion to avoid collision.
> 4. **Not stating the cutting velocity assumption** — Examiners expect you to specify `v = 30 m/min for mild steel` from a machinability handbook. Do not assume; justify.
> 5. **Wrong spindle direction** — M03 is CW (most milling uses CW), M04 is CCW. Mixing these up can cause climb vs. conventional milling errors.
> 6. **Missing program end** — Always end with `M30;` (program end and rewind) — this is a 1-mark item often missed.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): Sequence Numbers and Manual Programming Workflow**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | CO2 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze | 14 Marks]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Concept of Sequence Numbers in NC Programming. Discuss the Format, Rules, and Applications with Suitable Examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
A **Sequence Number** (or block number) is an alphanumeric identifier, written as the letter `N` followed by 1 to 4 numerical digits, placed at the beginning of an NC program block. It serves as a unique "name" for that block.

**Standard Format:**
```
Nxxxx [G-code] [X-value] [Y-value] [Z-value] [F-value] [S-value] [M-code];
```

Examples: `N10`, `N50`, `N100`, `N999`, `N1000`.

**Five Key Rules (KTU High-Yield):**

1. **Execution order is sequential** — The program runs from top to bottom regardless of N-number values. N50 does NOT execute before N10 just because 50 > 10.
2. **Numbering convention** — Use step of 5 or 10 in the main program; step of 1 in subroutines.
3. **Modern controllers** — Accept N-codes on every block OR only on major blocks; performance is identical.
4. **Empty block** — A block may contain only the sequence number (e.g., `N100;`) — used as a section marker.
5. **Skipped numbers allowed** — N10, N30, N50 is valid; gaps are intentional for future inserts.

**Applications with Examples:**

| Application | Example Code | Purpose |
|-------------|--------------|---------|
| **Operator Search** | Operator types `N200` to find Block 200 | Quick location during setup |
| **Subroutine Call** | `M98 P0200;` calls subroutine O0200 | Modular programming |
| **Branch/Jump** | `GOTO N500;` (on some controllers) | Conditional logic |
| **Section Marker** | `N100 (START FACING);` | Program readability |
| **Repetition** | Use with WHILE/DO loops | Pocketing, peck drilling |

**Illustrative Example:**
```
N10 G90 G00 X0 Y0 Z50.0;   (Safe start)
N20 M06 T01;                (Tool change)
N30 M03 S1500;              (Spindle ON)
N40 G01 Z-5.0 F50;          (Plunge)
N50 X100.0 F200;            (Cut)
N60 G00 Z50.0;              (Retract)
N70 M05 M09;                (Spindle + coolant OFF)
N80 M30;                    (Program end)
```

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Describe the Step-by-Step Procedure for Manual Part Programming. Why is a Coding Sheet Used? What is the Importance of the Program Origin Selection?**

**Model Solution:**

**The 8-Step Manual Programming Procedure:**

| Step | Activity | Output |
|------|----------|--------|
| **1** | Study the engineering drawing | List of features, dimensions, tolerances |
| **2** | Select work-holding device | Fixture/vise/chuck selection |
| **3** | Define work coordinate system | Program zero location |
| **4** | Select cutting tools | Tool list with T-numbers |
| **5** | Determine machining sequence | Operation order |
| **6** | Calculate tool path coordinates | X, Y, Z, I, J, K values |
| **7** | Select cutting parameters | S, F, depth of cut |
| **8** | Write program on coding sheet | Complete part program |

**Why a Coding Sheet is Used:**
- A **coding sheet** is a standardized pre-printed form that provides:
  - Pre-formatted columns for each word (N, G, X, Y, Z, I, J, K, F, S, T, M).
  - Pre-defined column widths that match the punched-tape or punched-card format of older NC machines.
  - A **clear visual structure** to prevent omission of words.
  - **Traceability** — the original handwritten sheet is preserved as documentation.
- Even in modern CAM environments, the coding sheet format is preserved for **manual editing and verification**.

**Importance of Program Origin Selection:**

The **program origin (X0, Y0, Z0)** is the reference point from which all coordinates in the program are measured. Its selection critically impacts:

1. **Numerical Simplicity** — Placing origin at a corner or hole center makes coordinates small and easy to verify.
2. **Inspection Alignment** — The origin should match the **inspection datum** on the engineering drawing (e.g., bottom-left corner, center of a hole).
3. **Setup Repeatability** — Same origin across multiple parts enables consistent fixturing.
4. **Tool Path Clarity** — Symmetric parts should have origin at the symmetry center for easier programming.
5. **Avoiding Negative Coordinates** — Placing origin at the lower-left corner keeps all cutting coordinates positive (cleaner math, less confusion).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Skipping steps in the procedure** — Examiners expect ALL 8 steps listed; missing even one (especially "calculate coordinates" or "define WCS") costs a full mark.
> 2. **Vague answer on coding sheet** — State clearly: "It provides pre-formatted columns matching the word address format and helps avoid omission of any word."
> 3. **Confusing program origin with machine origin** — Machine origin (M0) is set by the manufacturer; program origin (P0 or G54) is set by the programmer. They are DIFFERENT.
> 4. **Not relating origin to inspection datum** — Examiners want the connection to engineering drawing datums, not just "place it anywhere convenient."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — NC Part Programming Fundamentals | Rapid Revision Bullets**

- **NC Part Programming** = writing standardized instructions (G-codes, M-codes) for a CNC machine.
- **Manual Programming** = hand-written using word address format; **CAM/APT** = software-generated.
- **Word Address Format** is the ISO 6983 standard — `N10 G01 X50.0 Y25.0 F100;`
- **Coordinate System** is **right-handed**, standardized by **ISO 841**.
- **Z-axis** = along spindle; **X-axis** = longest travel; **Y-axis** = completes the right-hand system.
- **Rotational axes A, B, C** = rotation about X, Y, Z respectively (right-hand rule).
- For **VMC**: Z is up. For **Lathe**: Z is along spindle, X is radial. For **HMC**: Z is horizontal.
- **Sequence Number** = `N` + 1 to 4 digits (e.g., `N10`, `N500`).
- **Execution is sequential** (top-to-bottom), NOT by N-number value.
- **Step of 10** in main program; **step of 1** in subroutines — leaves room for insertions.
- **Program origin** should match the **inspection datum** on the drawing.
- **Coding sheets** ensure standard format and prevent omission of words.
- **Spindle Speed formula**: $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v}{\pi \cdot D}$ (rpm)
- **Feed Rate (milling)**: $F = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ (mm/min)
- Always **state units** (rpm, mm/min) in calculations.
- Always **end with M30;** — program end and rewind.
- Always include a **safe Z approach** before any rapid motion to avoid tool-workpiece collision.
- **M03** = spindle CW; **M04** = spindle CCW; **M05** = spindle stop.
- **G90** = absolute mode; **G91** = incremental mode.
- **G54 to G59** = work coordinate system offsets (multiple fixtures).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparatory Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT504 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Preparatory Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Preparatory Functions** (commonly referred to as **G-Codes** or *General Functions*) are standardized, modal alphanumeric codes used in part programming for **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** machine tools. They instruct the machine controller to perform specific geometric and non-cutting preparatory actions—such as axis movement, plane selection, coordinate system establishment, and motion type—**before** the actual material removal (cutting) operation begins.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, preparatory functions belong to the family of **NC/CNC Part Programming Word Address Format** commands, structured as:

$$ \text{N}\_\text{---} \quad \text{G}\_{\text{XX}} \quad \text{X}\_\text{---} \quad \text{Y}\_\text{---} \quad \text{Z}\_\text{---} \quad \text{F}\_\text{---} \quad \text{S}\_\text{---} \quad \text{T}\_\text{---} \quad \text{M}\_\text{---} $$

where **G** is the address letter reserved exclusively for preparatory functions as per **ISO 6983** and **EIA RS-267** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Preparatory functions are the foundation of any NC program. Without them, the CNC controller has no idea **how to move**, **where to move**, **in which plane**, or **at what feed rate**. They "prepare" the machine for the upcoming cutting toolpath.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car to a friend's house:
- Before you start driving, you must **set the GPS destination** (Work Coordinate System — G54–G59).
- You must choose whether your speedometer shows **km/h or mph** (Inch/Metric — G20/G21).
- You must decide whether directions like "move 5 blocks north" mean **5 blocks from your home** (Incremental — G91) or **5 blocks from where you stand right now** (Absolute — G90).
- Finally, you press the accelerator (Feed Rate — F) and steer along a specific path (Linear — G01, Circular — G02/G03).

**Preparatory functions are the dashboard controls and map settings you configure before driving begins.** They do not themselves cut material — they set the *stage* for cutting.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Programmatic Constants

- **G00 to G99** is the standardized numerical range (per ISO 6983).
- Preparatory functions are **modal** unless specified otherwise (i.e., they remain active until canceled by another G-code in the same group).
- **G-Code Group Number** determines modal grouping. Only one G-code from a group can be active at any instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Assumption in KTU Valuation:** Unless otherwise stated, students must assume that the standard machine controller follows the **Fanuc 0i / Siemens Sinumerik 808D** convention for modal behavior and G-code grouping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Preparatory Functions

Preparatory functions can be broken down into five logical categories based on their functional intent:

### 2.1.1 Motion Control G-Codes
- **G00** — Rapid Traverse (Point-to-Point Positioning)
- **G01** — Linear Interpolation (Straight-line cutting at programmed feed)
- **G02** — Circular Interpolation, **Clockwise (CW)**
- **G03** — Circular Interpolation, **Counter-Clockwise (CCW)**
- **G04** — Dwell (Programmable delay)

### 2.1.2 Coordinate System & Dimensioning
- **G90** — Absolute Programming (coordinates referenced from program origin)
- **G91** — Incremental Programming (coordinates referenced from current position)

### 2.1.3 Plane Selection
- **G17** — XY Plane selection (default for most milling operations)
- **G18** — ZX Plane selection (default for most turning operations)
- **G19** — YZ Plane selection

### 2.1.4 Compensation Functions
- **G40** — Cutter Compensation **Cancel**
- **G41** — Cutter Compensation **Left**
- **G42** — Cutter Compensation **Right**
- **G43** — Tool Length Compensation **Positive**
- **G44** — Tool Length Compensation **Negative**

### 2.1.5 Canned Cycles & Coordinate Setting
- **G54 to G59** — Work Coordinate System selection (Fixture Offsets 1–6)
- **G80 to G89** — Canned Cycles for drilling, boring, tapping
- **G28** — Return to Reference Point (Home position)
- **G92** — Setting of Work Coordinate System (Legacy Fanuc)

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Operational Logic Steps

**Why do we need G-Codes?**
- A bare CNC controller is a "dumb" motion system. It needs explicit, unambiguous instructions to:
  1. **Plan the path geometry** (G00, G01, G02, G03)
  2. **Establish reference frames** (G54–G59, G92)
  3. **Compensate for tool wear/geometry** (G41, G42, G43)
  4. **Define units of measurement** (G20, G21)
  5. **Select operational planes** (G17, G18, G19)

**How does the controller interpret them?**
1. The **part program** (.nc or .gcode file) is parsed sequentially.
2. The **Interpreter** in the CNC reads each block (line).
3. The G-code address triggers a specific **subroutine** in the Machine Control Unit (MCU).
4. The subroutine drives the **servo motors** through the interpolated motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The single most-tested concept in Module 2 examinations is the difference between **G00 (Rapid)** and **G01 (Linear Interpolation)**. G00 is ALWAYS non-cutting and moves at maximum machine rapid rate; G01 is ALWAYS cutting and moves at the programmed feed rate **F**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

The table below consolidates the mathematical relations required for KTU 2024 examination problems on preparatory functions. The critical parameter is the **interpolated endpoint computation** for circular and linear moves.

| Concept | Equation / Relation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linear Interpolation (G01) Endpoint | $X_{end} = X_{start} \pm \Delta X$ | Straight-line motion coordinate |
| Linear Feed Time | $T = \dfrac{\sqrt{\Delta X^{2} + \Delta Y^{2} + \Delta Z^{2}}}{F}$ | Time to traverse the linear path |
| Arc Length (Quarter Circle) | $L = \dfrac{\pi \cdot R}{2}$ | Length of a 90° arc of radius **R** |
| Arc Length (Full Circle) | $L = 2\pi R$ | Circumference of a full circle |
| Arc Length (General Angle $\theta$ in degrees) | $L = \dfrac{\pi R \theta}{180}$ | Arc length for $\theta$ in degrees |
| Polar to Cartesian (for G02/G03) | $X_{end} = X_{center} + R \cos(\alpha)$ , $Y_{end} = Y_{center} + R \sin(\alpha)$ | Conversion to machine axes |
| Incremental vs Absolute | $X_{inc} = X_{abs,end} - X_{abs,start}$ | Conversion from absolute to incremental |
| Linear Feed Rate Conversion | $F_{mm/min} = F_{mm/rev} \times N_{rpm}$ | Converts per-rev to per-min feed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE questions, when computing feed time for a circular arc, students often forget the trigonometric derivation. Use the **arc length formula** directly — it is the most efficient method.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Arc Length and Feed Time Calculation for Circular Interpolation (G02 / G03)

This is the single most-asked derivation in KTU 2024 Module 2 papers. We will exhaustively derive the time required to execute a circular arc move.

### 3.1.1 Given Information
- A circular arc of **radius $R$** is to be machined.
- The arc subtends an angle of $\theta$ degrees at the center.
- The programmed feed rate is $F$ (in **mm/min**).
- The spindle speed is $N$ (in **rpm**), relevant only if $F$ is given per revolution.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the arc length in terms of the full circumference.**

The full circumference of a circle of radius $R$ is universally defined as:

$$
C = 2\pi R
$$

**Step 2: Compute the fraction of the circle covered by the arc.**

If the arc subtends an angle $\theta$ (in degrees) at the center, then it represents the following fractional portion of the full circle:

$$
f = \frac{\theta}{360}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the arc length.**

The arc length $L$ is the fractional portion of the full circumference:

$$
L = f \times C = \frac{\theta}{360} \times 2\pi R
$$

Simplifying:

$$
L = \frac{2\pi R \theta}{360} = \frac{\pi R \theta}{180}
$$

This is the **canonical arc length formula** for angles in degrees.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The factor $360$ in the denominator comes from the total degrees in a full revolution. The factor $2\pi$ comes from the standard circumference formula. When the angle is given in **radians**, the formula simplifies elegantly to $L = R\theta$, which is the definition of a radian.

**Step 4: Compute the feed time for the arc.**

Feed rate $F$ is defined as the distance traveled per unit time. Therefore, time is distance divided by feed rate:

$$
T_{arc} = \frac{L}{F} = \frac{\pi R \theta}{180 \, F}
$$

**Step 5: If feed is given per revolution ($F_{rev}$ in mm/rev), then include spindle speed.**

When the feed rate is specified per spindle revolution, the **linear velocity of the cutting tool** is:

$$
F_{min} = F_{rev} \times N
$$

Therefore, the arc machining time becomes:

$$
T_{arc} = \frac{\pi R \theta}{180 \times F_{rev} \times N}
$$

**Step 6: Convert seconds to minutes if required.**

Since $F$ is typically in **mm/min**, $T$ comes out directly in **minutes**. To convert to seconds:

$$
T_{arc,sec} = T_{arc,min} \times 60 = \frac{60 \pi R \theta}{180 \, F} = \frac{\pi R \theta}{3 F}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A CNC milling machine is programmed to cut a **quarter circle** of radius **$R = 50$ mm** using **G02 (CW)** in the **G17 (XY) plane**, starting from $(X=0, Y=50)$. The programmed feed rate is **$F = 200$ mm/min**. Calculate the **cutting time** for this arc.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A — Identify the parameters:**

$$
R = 50 \, \text{mm}, \quad \theta = 90^{\circ}, \quad F = 200 \, \text{mm/min}
$$

**Step B — Apply the arc length formula:**

$$
L = \frac{\pi R \theta}{180} = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 90}{180}
$$

**Step C — Simplify numerically:**

$$
L = \frac{\pi \times 50}{2} = 25\pi \approx 78.54 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step D — Apply the time formula:**

$$
T = \frac{L}{F} = \frac{78.54}{200}
$$

**Step E — Final result:**

$$
T \approx 0.3927 \, \text{min} \approx 23.56 \, \text{seconds}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: G90 vs G91

This matrix is critical for KTU 2024 short-answer questions on coordinate dimensioning.

| Parameter | G90 — Absolute Mode | G91 — Incremental Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reference Point | **Workpiece Origin (Program Zero)** | **Current Tool Position** |
| Typical Use | Most production CNC programs | Subprograms, mirror machining, simple short moves |
| Error Propagation | Errors are **not compounded** | Errors **accumulate** block-to-block |
| Reset State | Default on most Fanuc controllers | Must be explicitly commanded |
| Block Dependency | Independent of previous block | Dependent on previous block |
| Coordinate System | Tied to **G54–G59** work offset | Tied to **current tool position** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When a problem statement mentions "point A is the origin" or "all coordinates are from the program zero," the default mode is **G90 (Absolute)**. If the problem says "next point is at a distance of..." or uses "from current position," then **G91 (Incremental)** is implied.

## 3.4 Comparative Matrix: G00 vs G01

| Parameter | G00 — Rapid Traverse | G01 — Linear Interpolation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Purpose | **Non-cutting** positioning | **Cutting** straight-line move |
| Feed Rate | **Maximum machine rapid** (e.g., 10 m/min) | **Programmed F value** |
| Path | Independent of each axis; diagonal in some controllers | **Exact straight line** through space |
| Tool | Spindle may be **OFF** | Spindle **ON**, cutting engaged |
| Typical Use | Tool change, approach moves, retract moves | All milling, facing, turning cuts |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Flowchart of Preparatory Functions

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete taxonomy of preparatory functions as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus. This block-level functional architecture is the recommended visual aid for KTU board answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Preparatory Functions G Codes]:::root --> B[Motion Control]:::group
    A --> C[Coordinate Dimensioning]:::group
    A --> D[Plane Selection]:::group
    A --> E[Compensation Functions]:::group
    A --> F[Work Offset and Cycles]:::group

    B --> B1["G00 Rapid Traverse"]:::leaf
    B --> B2["G01 Linear Interpolation"]:::leaf
    B --> B3["G02 Circular CW"]:::leaf
    B --> B4["G03 Circular CCW"]:::leaf
    B --> B5["G04 Dwell"]:::leaf

    C --> C1["G90 Absolute Mode"]:::leaf
    C --> C2["G91 Incremental Mode"]:::leaf

    D --> D1["G17 XY Plane"]:::leaf
    D --> D2["G18 ZX Plane"]:::leaf
    D --> D3["G19 YZ Plane"]:::leaf

    E --> E1["G40 Cutter Comp Cancel"]:::leaf
    E --> E2["G41 Cutter Comp Left"]:::leaf
    E --> E3["G42 Cutter Comp Right"]:::leaf
    E --> E4["G43 Tool Length Comp Positive"]:::leaf
    E --> E5["G44 Tool Length Comp Negative"]:::leaf

    F --> F1["G54 to G59 Work Offset"]:::leaf
    F --> F2["G80 to G89 Canned Cycles"]:::leaf
    F --> F3["G28 Return to Home"]:::leaf
    F --> F4["G92 Set Work Coordinate"]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef group fill:#4a7ba8,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#a8c5e2,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 CNC Program Execution Flow (G-Code Processing Pipeline)

This block diagram illustrates how the Machine Control Unit (MCU) processes a part program line-by-line, which is a frequently-tested KTU 2024 concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Part Program .nc File]:::proc --> P2[Block Parser]:::proc
    P2 --> P3[Word Address Decoder]:::proc
    P3 --> P4{G Code Type}:::decide
    P4 -->|Motion G| P5[Interpolator]:::proc
    P4 -->|Setup G| P6[Mode Register Update]:::proc
    P5 --> P7[Servo Drive Signals]:::proc
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Axis Motors X Y Z]:::hw
    P8 --> P9[Machine Slide Motion]:::hw

    classDef proc fill:#2d5a87,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef decide fill:#c97b30,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef hw fill:#5a8a3f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Circular Interpolation Geometry (G02 / G03)

For KTU 2024 problems on arc machining, the following geometric schematic is the canonical reference. It shows the relationship between the start point, end point, center point, radius, and arc direction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start Point Xs Ys]:::pt --> B1[Center Point Xc Yc]:::center
    B1 --> C1[End Point Xe Ye]:::pt
    A1 --> C1

    B1 -.Radius R.-> A1
    B1 -.Radius R.-> C1

    D1{G02 or G03}:::decide
    D1 -->|G02 CW| C1
    D1 -->|G03 CCW| C1

    E1[Arc Direction Indicator]:::annot
    E1 -.Arrows along arc path.-> A1

    classDef pt fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef center fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef decide fill:#3498db,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef annot fill:#95a5a6,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When drawing arc diagrams in the answer sheet, always label three points explicitly — **Start (S)**, **Center (C)**, and **End (E)** — and indicate the **radius R** with a dashed line. Marks are awarded specifically for this labeling completeness.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between G00 and G01 preparatory functions. State two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**G00 — Rapid Traverse:**
- Moves the tool at the **maximum machine rapid rate** (typically 5–15 m/min).
- Used for **non-cutting** positioning moves.
- **Applications:** (1) Tool approach to workpiece from a safe Z-height. (2) Tool retract after a cut is complete. (3) Tool change positioning.

**G01 — Linear Interpolation:**
- Moves the tool in a **perfectly straight line** at the **programmed feed rate F**.
- Used for **cutting** straight edges and profiles.
- **Applications:** (1) Facing operation on a milling machine. (2) Straight turning on a lathe. (3) Linear slot milling.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Explain the function of G90 and G91 with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**G90 — Absolute Programming:** All coordinates in the program are referenced from the **program origin (X0, Y0, Z0)**, which is typically set via the work offset G54–G59.

**G91 — Incremental Programming:** All coordinates are referenced from the **current tool position** (i.e., the previous block's endpoint).

**Example — Drilling two holes:**
- Hole 1 at absolute $(30, 40)$, Hole 2 at absolute $(80, 40)$.
- **G90 Program:**
  `N10 G90 G01 X30 Y40 F100`
  `N20 X80 Y40`
- **G91 Program:**
  `N10 G90 G01 X30 Y40 F100` *(initial move in absolute)*
  `N20 G91 X50` *(incremental move 50 mm in +X direction)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various **plane selection preparatory functions (G17, G18, G19)** with their significance in CNC programming. State the default plane for milling and turning operations. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A CNC milling cutter must move along a **quarter-circle arc** of radius **$R = 75$ mm** in the **G17 plane**, starting from point **$A(0, 75)$** and ending at point **$B(75, 0)$**. The feed rate is **$F = 300$ mm/min**. Calculate the **arc length**, the **cutting time**, and determine whether the move is **G02 (CW) or G03 (CCW)**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Plane Selection Functions:**
- **G17 — XY Plane Selection:** This preparatory function designates the **XY plane** as the active plane for circular interpolation (G02/G03) and cutter compensation (G41/G42). In this plane, circular arcs are described using X and Y coordinates, with Z as the perpendicular axis.
  - **Default plane for CNC milling machines.**
  - Arcs appear as circles when viewed from the +Z direction (top view).

- **G18 — ZX Plane Selection:** Designates the **ZX plane** as the active plane.
  - **Default plane for CNC turning centers (lathes).**
  - Arcs appear as circles when viewed from the +Y direction (side view).

- **G19 — YZ Plane Selection:** Designates the **YZ plane** as the active plane.
  - Rarely used in practice; applicable for special milling geometries such as end-milling of vertical walls.

**Significance:**
1. Defines which two axes will execute the circular interpolation.
2. Determines the orientation of the **normal vector** for the arc.
3. Required for proper application of **cutter compensation (G41/G42)**.
4. Affects the interpretation of the **I, J, K** arc center offsets.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $R = 75$ mm
- $A = (0, 75)$ — Start point
- $B = (75, 0)$ — End point
- $F = 300$ mm/min
- $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ (quarter circle)

**Step 1 — Determine the arc direction:**

Using the right-hand rule with **G17 (XY plane)** and looking from +Z down to the origin:
- Start point A is on the +Y axis.
- End point B is on the +X axis.
- The motion from A → B sweeps **counter-clockwise (CCW)** as viewed from +Z.
- Therefore, the correct preparatory function is **G03 (CCW)**.

**Step 2 — Compute the arc length:**

$$
L = \frac{\pi R \theta}{180} = \frac{\pi \times 75 \times 90}{180}
$$

$$
L = \frac{\pi \times 75}{2} = 37.5\pi \approx 117.81 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cutting time:**

$$
T = \frac{L}{F} = \frac{117.81}{300}
$$

$$
T \approx 0.3927 \, \text{min} \approx 23.56 \, \text{seconds}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Arc Length $L \approx 117.81$ mm
- Cutting Time $T \approx 23.56$ s
- Motion is **G03 (Counter-Clockwise)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Retest]**
**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **G17, G18, G19 plane selection system** in CNC machining. Show the direction of the **arc normal vector** for each plane. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A CNC lathe executes a **semi-circular turning operation** of radius **$R = 100$ mm** on a workpiece of diameter **$D = 80$ mm**. The spindle speed is **$N = 600$ rpm** and the feed rate is **$F = 0.3$ mm/rev**. Calculate the **arc length**, the **machining time**, and the **number of spindle revolutions** required. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The plane selection G-codes define the plane in which circular interpolation occurs. The sketch consists of three orthographic views of the machine coordinate system:

- **G17 (XY Plane):** The active plane is horizontal (top view). The **normal vector** points along the **+Z axis**. Used for milling.
- **G18 (ZX Plane):** The active plane is vertical (front view). The **normal vector** points along the **+Y axis**. Used for turning.
- **G19 (YZ Plane):** The active plane is vertical (side view). The **normal vector** points along the **+X axis**. Rarely used.

For circular interpolation, the **sense of rotation (CW or CCW)** is defined by looking **along the negative normal vector** toward the origin. This is a critical convention in ISO 6983 and is frequently tested.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Arc radius $R = 100$ mm
- Angle subtended $\theta = 180°$ (semi-circle)
- Spindle speed $N = 600$ rpm
- Feed per revolution $F = 0.3$ mm/rev

**Step 1 — Compute the arc length:**

$$
L = \frac{\pi R \theta}{180} = \frac{\pi \times 100 \times 180}{180}
$$

$$
L = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear feed rate (mm/min):**

$$
F_{min} = F_{rev} \times N = 0.3 \times 600 = 180 \, \text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the machining time:**

$$
T = \frac{L}{F_{min}} = \frac{314.16}{180}
$$

$$
T \approx 1.745 \, \text{min} \approx 104.7 \, \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the number of spindle revolutions:**

$$
N_{rev} = N \times T = 600 \times 1.745 \approx 1047 \, \text{revolutions}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Arc Length $L \approx 314.16$ mm
- Machining Time $T \approx 1.745$ min
- Spindle Revolutions $\approx 1047$ rev

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Mixing up G02 and G03:** Students frequently mis-identify the direction of rotation. **Always apply the right-hand rule** — curl the fingers of your right hand along the arc direction; the thumb points in the direction of the normal vector (+Z for G17). If the thumb points OUT of the page (toward you), it is **G02 (CW)**. If the thumb points INTO the page, it is **G03 (CCW)**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to the formula:** The arc length formula $\frac{\pi R \theta}{180}$ is only valid for $\theta$ in **degrees**. If $\theta$ is in radians, use $L = R\theta$ directly. Marks are deducted for using the wrong formula version.
> 3. **Confusing feed units:** $F$ in **mm/min** vs **mm/rev** leads to entirely different time values. Read the problem statement carefully. KTU papers always specify the unit.
> 4. **Not labeling the arc diagram:** In 7-mark derivations, students lose 1–2 marks for omitting the start point, end point, center point, and direction arrows in the arc geometry sketch.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **G-Code Range:** Standardized range is **G00 to G99** (ISO 6983).
- **G00 = Rapid (Non-cutting, Max Feed); G01 = Linear Interpolation (Cutting, Programmed Feed).**
- **G02 = Clockwise Arc; G03 = Counter-Clockwise Arc.** Direction is defined by looking along the **negative normal vector** of the active plane.
- **G17, G18, G19 = XY, ZX, YZ planes** respectively. G17 is default for milling; G18 is default for turning.
- **G90 = Absolute (from workpiece origin); G91 = Incremental (from current position).**
- **G40 cancels cutter compensation; G41 = left; G42 = right.**
- **G43 = Positive tool length compensation; G44 = Negative tool length compensation.**
- **G54 to G59 = Work Coordinate System (Fixture) Offsets.**
- **G04 = Dwell (Programmable Delay).** Format: `G04 X1.5` (dwell for 1.5 seconds).
- **Arc Length Formula:** $L = \dfrac{\pi R \theta}{180}$ for $\theta$ in degrees, or $L = R\theta$ for $\theta$ in radians.
- **Machining Time Formula:** $T = \dfrac{L}{F}$, where $F$ is in mm/min.
- **Feed Conversion:** $F_{mm/min} = F_{mm/rev} \times N_{rpm}$.
- **Modal Nature:** G-codes remain active until another G-code from the **same group** is issued.
- **Default Active G-Codes on Power-On:** G00, G17, G90, G40 (Fanuc standard).
- **Word Address Format:** Each block has the structure `N__ G__ X__ Y__ Z__ F__ S__ T__ M__`.
- **Standard for KTU:** Assume **Fanuc 0i / Siemens Sinumerik 808D** convention unless stated otherwise.
- **Plane-Arc Pairing:** G02/G03 only works in the currently active G17/G18/G19 plane — switching planes is a common exam trap.
- **G92 vs G54–G59:** G92 is a legacy single-coordinate system setter; modern controllers use G54–G59 multi-fixture offsets, which is the KTU 2024 preferred approach.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Aided Part Programming: Concept and Need of CAP - CNC Languages - APT Language Structure: Geometry Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT504 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computer Aided Part Programming: Concept and Need of CAP - CNC Languages - APT Language Structure: Geometry Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Computer Aided Part Programming (CAP) – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Aided Part Programming (CAP)** is a method of programming NC/CNC machine tools in which the part programmer writes the machining instructions in a high-level, English-like symbolic language (such as **APT – Automatically Programmed Tool**) using a general-purpose computer. The computer then performs the complex trigonometric, geometric, and tool-path calculations (called **CL data – Cutter Location data**) automatically, and a **post-processor** converts the CL data into the specific M-codes and G-codes (ISO/EIA codes) understood by the target CNC controller.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, CAP is positioned as a productivity multiplier for manufacturing complex 3-D contoured parts (aircraft structural components, dies, moulds, turbine blades) where manual part programming is practically impossible due to thousands of tool-position calculations.

### Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Giving Directions to a Driver**
>
> Imagine you are guiding a driver from Kottayam to Kochi.
> - **Manual Part Programming** = You calculate every turn, every distance, every direction, every lane change in numbers and tell the driver. Tedious, error-prone, and exhausting.
> - **Computer Aided Part Programming** = You simply tell the system *"Take the friend from Kottayam to Kochi via the highway"* and the system (Google Maps) calculates the route. The "post-processor" then translates the route into specific turn-by-turn instructions suitable for the driver's specific car model.
>
> In the same way, the **APT programmer** describes the **part geometry and tool motion** in symbolic English. The **mainframe / CAP workstation** performs the math. The **post-processor** outputs the machine-specific code.

---

## 1.2 Why CAP is Needed – The Driving Forces

> [!NOTE]
> **The fundamental needs that created CAP systems (originated at MIT in 1956-1959 with APT):**
> 1. **Geometric complexity** – 3-D curved surfaces require thousands of CL point calculations.
> 2. **Human error** – Manual trigonometric calculations are prone to mistakes.
> 3. **Time and cost** – Manual programming of complex parts can take weeks; CAP reduces it to hours.
> 4. **Portability** – One APT program can be post-processed for different CNC machines.
> 5. **Productivity and repeatability** – Faster changeover between parts, consistent quality.
> 6. **Tool-path simulation** – CAP systems allow graphical verification before actual cutting (graphical NC verification).

### Comparative Snapshot – Manual vs. Computer Aided Part Programming

| Parameter | Manual Part Programming | Computer Aided Part Programming |
|---|---|---|
| **Language level** | Low-level (G-codes, M-codes) | High-level symbolic (APT, ADAPT, EXAPT) |
| **Skill required** | Trigonometry, machine code knowledge | Geometry + English-like statements |
| **Suitable part complexity** | 2-D and simple 3-D | Complex 3-D contoured surfaces |
| **Computation method** | Done by programmer manually | Done by computer (CL-file generation) |
| **Error rate** | High | Low (with verification) |
| **Programming time** | High | Low |
| **Machine dependency** | Machine-specific | Machine-independent (via post-processor) |
| **Cost effective for** | Simple parts | Complex parts with frequent changes |

---

## 1.3 CNC Programming Languages – Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – Three Language Generations in CNC:**
>
> 1. **Low-Level (Machine) Languages** – Direct G-code/M-code programming on the machine controller. Example: Fanuc G-code, Siemens SINUMERIK.
> 2. **High-Level (Symbolic) Part-Programming Languages** – English-like geometry + motion statements. Examples given in KTU syllabus:
>    - **APT** (Automatically Programmed Tool) – the original MIT language.
>    - **ADAPT** – Adapted version of APT.
>    - **EXAPT** – Extended subset of APT (German, with milling/turning/Drilling variants).
>    - **AUTOSPOT** – Automatic System for Positioning Tools.
>    - **SPLIT** – Sundstrand Processing Language Internally Translated.
>    - **COMPACT II** – Computer-graphics Oriented Part-programming APT-based language.
> 3. **Conversational / Shop-Floor Programming** – Menu-driven, semi-automated, used on modern CNCs (e.g., Mazatrol, Heidenhain).

### Hierarchy of CNC Programming

$$\text{Part Geometry Description} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{APT Source Program} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CL Data File (Cutter Location)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Post-Processor} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Machine-Specific G/M Code}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key term – CL Data:** Cutter Location data is the output of the APT processor. It is a list of cutter tip coordinates, surface normals, and tool-axis vectors computed from the geometry statements. CL data is machine-independent and must always be post-processed.

---

## 1.4 APT Language – Overview and Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **APT = Automatically Programmed Tool**
> Developed at **MIT Servomechanisms Laboratory** under the **Aerospace Industries Association (AIA)**, sponsored by **US Air Force**, during **1956–1959**. It is the **grandparent** of virtually all modern part-programming languages.

### 1.4.1 APT Program Statement Categories (KTU High-Yield)

A complete APT program consists of four major statement types:

1. **Geometry Definition Statements** – Define points, lines, circles, planes, surfaces, and patterns.
2. **Motion Statements** – Define how the tool moves (GOTO, GODLTA, etc.).
3. **Post-Processor Statements** – Control spindle speed, feed, coolant, tool change, etc.
4. **Auxiliary / Miscellaneous Statements** – Tolerances, print commands, comments, part numbers, etc.

### 1.4.2 APT Statement – General Syntax Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **General Format:**
> $$\text{command}/\text{modifier}_1,\;\text{data}_1,\;\text{modifier}_2,\;\text{data}_2,\;\dots$$
>
> - Statements are written in **free format** but typically one per line.
> - A **slash ( / )** separates the **command word** from its **modifier-data pairs**.
> - A **comma ( , )** separates successive modifier-data pairs.
> - The statement **numbering** is **optional** (e.g., N001, N002 …).
> - Dollar sign ( **$** ) at the start of a line indicates a **comment**.
> - The first statement is usually the **PARTNO** statement (part identification).

**Example – A simple APT line:**
```
CIRCLE/10.0, 5.0, 0.0, 8.0
```
- Command: **CIRCLE**
- Modifiers: **10.0, 5.0, 0.0** (center x, y, z) and **8.0** (radius)

---

## 1.5 Geometry Commands in APT – Conceptual Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometry Commands** are the heart of APT. They allow the programmer to mathematically describe every feature of the workpiece (points, lines, circles, planes, surfaces) symbolically, *before* any cutting motion is specified.

### Why geometry is defined separately from motion?

- **Reusability** – A defined point/line/circle can be referenced by multiple tool motions.
- **Clarity** – The program becomes self-documenting and easy to edit.
- **Post-processor independence** – Geometry descriptions are pure mathematics; motion is converted to G-code.

### KTU Core Geometry Command Inventory

| Geometry Element | APT Command | Primary Modifiers |
|---|---|---|
| **Point** | `POINT` | coordinates, INTOF, CENTER |
| **Line** | `LINE` | two points, PARL, PERPTO, ANGL, TANTO |
| **Circle** | `CIRCLE` | center+radius, three points, TANTO, CENTER |
| **Plane** | `PLANE` | three points |
| **Tabulated Cylinder** | `TABCYL` | surface + two edges |
| **General Cone** | `GCONE` | vertex, angles, height |
| **General Disc** | `GDISC` | plane + radius |
| **Pattern (linear)** | `PATERN` | LINE, points, increments, count |
| **Pattern (circular)** | `PATERN` | ARC, center, start/end angle, count |
| **Matrix** | `MATRIX` | 3×3 transform |
| **Translate / Rotate** | `TRANSL` / `ROTAT` | vector, angle |

Each of these is explained in depth in **SECTION 2** and **SECTION 3** below.

---

## 1.6 Pre-Defining Standard Geometry – The "Symbol Table"

Every geometry command in APT assigns an internal **symbolic label** to the created feature. This label is a *name* (typically 1–6 characters, starting with a letter), which becomes the identifier of that geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> Example:
> $$\text{L1} = \text{LINE}/20.0,\;0.0,\;0.0,\;50.0,\;0.0,\;0.0$$
> This creates a horizontal line **L1** along the X-axis from (20,0,0) to (50,0,0). The programmer can now write: `GOTO/L1` or `TLON,L1,GOFWD/...` using the same label.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of Each Geometry Command

### 2.1.1 The `POINT` Command

The `POINT` command is used to define a single point in 3-D space. It supports **direct coordinate** and **derived** definitions.

#### Forms of `POINT`

| Form | Syntax | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **By coordinates** | `P1 = POINT/x, y, z` | Explicitly specifies coordinates. |
| **Intersection of two lines** | `P1 = POINT/INTOF, L1, L2` | Where L1 and L2 meet. |
| **Intersection of line and circle** | `P1 = POINT/INTOF, L1, CIR1` | |
| **Intersection of two circles** | `P1 = POINT/INTOF, CIR1, CIR2` | (Two solutions – INTOF picks the closer one in input order) |
| **Center of a circle** | `P1 = POINT/CENTER, CIR1` | |

**Worked Examples:**
```
P1 = POINT/0.0, 0.0, 0.0          ! Origin point
P2 = POINT/INTOF, L1, L2           ! Where lines L1 and L2 intersect
P3 = POINT/CENTER, CIR1            ! Center of circle CIR1
```

> [!NOTE]
> If a geometry intersection yields **two solutions** (e.g., intersection of two circles, or two lines and a circle), the APT processor uses a **discrimination method**:
> - **INTOF** (intersection) – the intersection that the geometric construction implies, based on the input ordering.
> - **XLARGE / YSMALL** – selects a specific extreme solution.
> - **INTOF, parms** – the system chooses automatically.

---

### 2.1.2 The `LINE` Command

A line is the most-used geometry in APT. There are **eight fundamental forms**:

| # | Form | Syntax | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Through two points** | `L1 = LINE/P1, P2` | Line passing through P1 and P2. |
| 2 | **Through point, parallel to line** | `L1 = LINE/P1, PARL, L2` | Through P1, parallel to L2. |
| 3 | **Through point, perpendicular to line** | `L1 = LINE/P1, PERPTO, L2` | Through P1, ⊥ to L2. |
| 4 | **Through point, at angle to X-axis** | `L1 = LINE/P1, ANGL, θ` | Through P1 at angle θ degrees from +X axis. |
| 5 | **Through point, at angle to line** | `L1 = LINE/P1, ANGL, θ, L2` | At angle θ from L2, through P1. |
| 6 | **Tangent to circle, through point** | `L1 = LINE/P1, TANTO, CIR1` | Through P1, tangent to CIR1. |
| 7 | **Tangent to two circles** | `L1 = LINE/TANTO, CIR1, TANTO, CIR2` | Common external/internal tangent. |
| 8 | **Tangent to circle, at angle** | `L1 = LINE/CIR1, ANGL, θ, TANTO` | Tangent to CIR1 at angle θ. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favourite question** – Define a line `L1` passing through point `P1` and parallel to line `L2`:
> $$\text{L1} = \text{LINE/P1, PARL, L2}$$

---

### 2.1.3 The `CIRCLE` Command

A circle is described by **center + radius** OR by **three points** OR by **tangent constraints**.

| # | Form | Syntax | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Center + radius** | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/x, y, z, r` | Center (x,y,z), radius r. |
| 2 | **Center as point, radius** | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, RADIUS, r` | |
| 3 | **Through three points** | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/P1, P2, P3` | |
| 4 | **Center + tangent to line** | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, TANTO, L1` | Center P1, tangent to L1. |
| 5 | **Center + radius + tangent to line** | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, RADIUS, r, TANTO, L1` | |
| 6 | **Tangent to two lines, radius** | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/TANTO, L1, TANTO, L2, RADIUS, r` | |
| 7 | **Tangent to two circles, radius** | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/TANTO, CIR1, TANTO, CIR2, RADIUS, r` | |
| 8 | **Tangent to line and circle, radius** | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/TANTO, L1, TANTO, CIR1, RADIUS, r` | |

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrimination for multiple-circle-tangent problems:** If two common tangents exist (internal vs. external), use **XLARGE** or **YSMALL** discriminators to pick the correct one.

---

### 2.1.4 The `PLANE` Command

A plane is defined by three non-collinear points (the canonical APT way):

```
PLN1 = PLANE/P1, P2, P3
```

Planes are essential for 3-D contouring, especially for defining the top surface of a workpiece or for projection of 2-D tool paths.

---

### 2.1.5 The `TABCYL` (Tabulated Cylinder) Command

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TABCYL** is used to define a **3-D ruled surface** generated by moving a straight line (linear element) parallel to a *linear edge* while always touching an *angular edge* (a curve in a 2-D plane). It is widely used in aircraft wing and fuselage surfaces, propeller blades, and ship hulls.

**Syntax:**
```
TAB1 = TABCYL/cyl_surf, linear_edge, angular_edge
```

Where:
- `cyl_surf` is the unrolled cylindrical surface on which the angular edge is defined.
- `linear_edge` is the straight line element.
- `angular_edge` is the curve element on the cylindrical surface.

---

### 2.1.6 The `GCONE` (General Cone) and `GDISC` Commands

| Command | Description | Syntax |
|---|---|---|
| **GCONE** | Truncated right circular cone (frustum) | `GCN1 = GCONE/vertex_pt, slp, ssp, stp, h` (large radius, small radius, length). |
| **GDISC** | Flat circular disc on a plane | `GDS1 = GDISC/PLN1, r` |

These are used for moulds, valve bodies, and tapered surfaces.

---

### 2.1.7 The `PATERN` (Pattern) Command

Patterns are pre-defined arrays of points or holes used in drilling, milling bolt circles, etc.

#### Linear Pattern
```
PTN1 = PATERN/LINE, L1, PT_ST, INCR, n
```
Creates **n** points along line `L1`, starting from `PT_ST` with a given increment.

#### Circular Pattern
```
PTN1 = PATERN/ARC, C, RADIUS, r, A_ST, A_INC, n
```
Creates **n** points on a circle centered at C.

---

### 2.1.8 `MATRIX`, `TRANSL`, `ROTAT` (Coordinate Transformations)

| Command | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `MATRIX/x1,y1,...` | Define a coordinate transformation matrix. | `MTX1 = MATRIX/XYAXIS, 30.0` |
| `TRANSL/dx, dy, dz` | Translate a label by (dx,dy,dz). | `L2 = TRANSL/10.0, 0.0, 0.0, L1` |
| `ROTAT/axis, angle, label` | Rotate a label by `angle` degrees about an axis. | `L2 = ROTAT/YAXIS, 45.0, L1` |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat-Sheet Table – All Geometry Commands

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for the KTU exam – it captures 80% of the geometry-question marks.**

| Geometry | Command | Canonical Syntax (Most Common) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Point by coordinates | `POINT` | `P1 = POINT/x, y, z` | Origin, corner, hole centre. |
| Point of intersection | `POINT` | `P1 = POINT/INTOF, L1, L2` | Where two features cross. |
| Center of circle | `POINT` | `P1 = POINT/CENTER, CIR1` | Boss centre, hole centre. |
| Line through 2 pts | `LINE` | `L1 = LINE/P1, P2` | Edge of part. |
| Line parallel | `LINE` | `L1 = LINE/P1, PARL, L2` | Parallel slot/edge. |
| Line perpendicular | `LINE` | `L1 = LINE/P1, PERPTO, L2` | Slot floor, normal direction. |
| Line at angle | `LINE` | `L1 = LINE/P1, ANGL, θ` | Chamfer, bevel. |
| Line tangent | `LINE` | `L1 = LINE/P1, TANTO, CIR1` | Fillet, blend. |
| Circle center+radius | `CIRCLE` | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/x, y, z, r` | Hole, boss. |
| Circle 3 points | `CIRCLE` | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/P1, P2, P3` | Arc through given points. |
| Circle tangent | `CIRCLE` | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/TANTO, L1, TANTO, L2, RADIUS, r` | Fillet arc between two edges. |
| Plane | `PLANE` | `PLN1 = PLANE/P1, P2, P3` | Top face, projection plane. |
| Tabulated cylinder | `TABCYL` | `T1 = TABCYL/SURF, L1, CURVE1` | Wing, propeller. |
| General cone | `GCONE` | `C1 = GCONE/P1, rL, rS, h` | Taper, mould. |
| General disc | `GDISC` | `D1 = GDISC/PLN1, r` | Flat circular face. |
| Linear pattern | `PATERN` | `PTN1 = PATERN/LINE, L1, P_ST, INCR, n` | Holes in a row. |
| Circular pattern | `PATERN` | `PTN1 = PATERN/ARC, P_C, RADIUS, r, A_ST, A_INC, n` | Bolt circle. |
| Translate | `TRANSL` | `L2 = TRANSL/dx, dy, dz, L1` | Move a feature. |
| Rotate | `ROTAT` | `L2 = ROTAT/YAXIS, θ, L1` | Rotate a feature. |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where are these commands used in industry?**
> - **Aerospace** – Wing skins, fuselage panels, turbine blades (TABCYL, GCONE dominate).
> - **Die & Mould** – Cavity machining for injection moulds, forging dies (PATERN for cooling channels, CIRCLE for fillets).
> - **Automotive** – Engine block pockets, valve seats (CIRCLE, PLANE).
> - **Ship-building** – Propeller blades, hull surfaces (TABCYL).
> - **General machining** – 2-D and 2.5-D parts use POINT, LINE, CIRCLE, PATERN.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why post-processors matter:** A single APT program can be post-processed for a **3-axis VMC**, a **5-axis machining center**, or a **turning center** simply by changing the post-processor – a powerful feature of CAP.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, ANALYTICAL MATRIX & WORKED EXAMPLES

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In this section, we work through 5 fully-worked, exam-style problems that exercise every important geometry command. Every algebraic / logical step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts.**

---

## 3.1 Problem 1 – Building a Rectangle with Rounded Corners (Fillets)

> **Statement:** Define the four corner points and the four edges of a **100 × 60 mm rectangle** with **R10 fillets** at all four corners, centred at the origin. Use only `POINT`, `LINE`, and `CIRCLE` commands.

### Step 1 – Decide the geometry

- Rectangle: from **(-50, -30)** to **(+50, +30)**, both in the XY plane (Z = 0).
- R10 fillets at all four corners.
- Tangent points: located 10 mm away from each corner along each edge.

### Step 2 – Define the eight tangent points (one per fillet corner)

```
C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER,  40.0,  20.0, 0.0, 10.0
C2 = CIRCLE/CENTER, -40.0,  20.0, 0.0, 10.0
C3 = CIRCLE/CENTER, -40.0, -20.0, 0.0, 10.0
C4 = CIRCLE/CENTER,  40.0, -20.0, 0.0, 10.0
```

### Step 3 – Define the tangent points of each fillet

For C1 (top-right), the tangent points on the right vertical edge and the top horizontal edge are:

```
P1 = POINT/ 50.0,  20.0, 0.0
P2 = POINT/ 40.0,  30.0, 0.0
P3 = POINT/-40.0,  30.0, 0.0
P4 = POINT/-50.0,  20.0, 0.0
P5 = POINT/-50.0, -20.0, 0.0
P6 = POINT/-40.0, -30.0, 0.0
P7 = POINT/ 40.0, -30.0, 0.0
P8 = POINT/ 50.0, -20.0, 0.0
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each pair of adjacent points (P1-P2, P3-P4, P5-P6, P7-P8) lies on a fillet circle (C1, C2, C3, C4 respectively).

### Step 4 – Define the four straight edges using LINE / PARL or LINE / 2 points

```
L1 = LINE/P1, PARL, YAXIS            ! Right edge – vertical, through P1
L2 = LINE/P3, PARL, XAXIS            ! Top edge – horizontal, through P3
L3 = LINE/P5, PARL, YAXIS            ! Left edge
L4 = LINE/P7, PARL, XAXIS            ! Bottom edge
```

> Or more explicitly with two points:
> ```
> L1 = LINE/ 50.0,  30.0, 0.0,  50.0, -30.0, 0.0
> ```

### Step 5 – Verify tangent points using INTOF (optional but rigorous)

For corner 1, the right edge and the fillet circle C1 intersect at:

```
P1 = POINT/INTOF, L1, C1
P2 = POINT/INTOF, L2, C1
```

This is the **preferred exam style**, because it shows you understand INTOF.

### Step 6 – Final result (clean APT listing)

```
PARTNO/RECTANGLE_FILLETS
REMARK/ 100x60 mm rectangle with R10 fillets
P1 = POINT/ 50.0,  20.0, 0.0
P2 = POINT/ 40.0,  30.0, 0.0
P3 = POINT/-40.0,  30.0, 0.0
P4 = POINT/-50.0,  20.0, 0.0
P5 = POINT/-50.0, -20.0, 0.0
P6 = POINT/-40.0, -30.0, 0.0
P7 = POINT/ 40.0, -30.0, 0.0
P8 = POINT/ 50.0, -20.0, 0.0
C1 = CIRCLE/CENTER,  40.0,  20.0, 0.0, 10.0
C2 = CIRCLE/CENTER, -40.0,  20.0, 0.0, 10.0
C3 = CIRCLE/CENTER, -40.0, -20.0, 0.0, 10.0
C4 = CIRCLE/CENTER,  40.0, -20.0, 0.0, 10.0
L1 = LINE/P1, PARL, YAXIS
L2 = LINE/P2, PARL, XAXIS
L3 = LINE/P3, PARL, YAXIS
L4 = LINE/P4, PARL, XAXIS
FINI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip – Why use `INTOF`?** It is *machine-independent* and *robust*. If the dimension changes from R10 to R12, the INTOF statements still work without recomputing coordinates. This is the elegance of APT.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 – Three-Point Circle Derivation

> **Statement:** Compute the centre and radius of a circle passing through three points **P1(0,0), P2(20,0), P3(0,15)** using APT. Show the equivalent analytic geometry.

### Step 1 – Analytic geometry background

The general equation of a circle in 2-D is:

$$x^2 + y^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0$$

Plugging the three points:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0,0) &: \quad 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + F = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; F = 0 \\
(20,0) &: \quad 400 + 0 + 20D + 0 + 0 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; D = -20 \\
(0,15) &: \quad 0 + 225 + 0 + 15E + 0 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; E = -15
\end{aligned}$$

So the circle equation is $x^2 + y^2 - 20x - 15y = 0$.

### Step 2 – Convert to standard form

$$\begin{aligned}
x^2 - 20x + y^2 - 15y &= 0 \\
(x^2 - 20x + 100) + (y^2 - 15y + 56.25) &= 100 + 56.25 \\
(x - 10)^2 + (y - 7.5)^2 &= 156.25
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 – Extract centre and radius

$$\text{Centre} = (10.0,\; 7.5,\; 0.0), \quad \text{Radius} = \sqrt{156.25} = 12.5$$

### Step 4 – Express in APT

```
CIR1 = CIRCLE/P1, P2, P3
C = POINT/CENTER, CIR1
R = 12.5
```

**Note:** `CIRCLE/P1, P2, P3` automatically creates a circle through the three points. The programmer does not compute D, E, F, or the centre manually — the **APT processor** does it.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 – Linear Pattern (Drilling 6 Holes in a Row)

> **Statement:** Define a row of **6 holes** starting at **P1(10, 0, 0)** and spaced **25 mm apart** along the X-axis.

### Step 1 – Define the starting point and direction line

```
P1 = POINT/10.0, 0.0, 0.0
L1 = LINE/P1, PARL, XAXIS
```

### Step 2 – Apply PATERN/LINE

```
PTN1 = PATERN/LINE, L1, P1, INCR, 25.0, 6
```

This creates **6 points** named `PTN1.1, PTN1.2, …, PTN1.6` (subscript notation in APT).

### Step 3 – Generated points (analytical check)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PTN1.1} &= (10.0, \; 0.0, \; 0.0) \\
\text{PTN1.2} &= (35.0, \; 0.0, \; 0.0) \\
\text{PTN1.3} &= (60.0, \; 0.0, \; 0.0) \\
\text{PTN1.4} &= (85.0, \; 0.0, \; 0.0) \\
\text{PTN1.5} &= (110.0, \; 0.0, \; 0.0) \\
\text{PTN1.6} &= (135.0, \; 0.0, \; 0.0)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical pattern verification:** The k-th hole is at:
> $$P_k = P_1 + (k-1)\cdot d \cdot \hat{u}$$
> where $d = 25$ mm, $\hat{u} = (1,0,0)$ (unit vector along X-axis), and $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, 6\}$.

---

## 3.4 Problem 4 – Bolt-Circle Pattern (Circular PATERN)

> **Statement:** Define **8 holes** on a **bolt circle** of **radius 50 mm** centred at origin, with the first hole at the +X axis. Express in APT.

### Step 1 – Analytical setup

Number of holes $N = 8$, angular increment:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{360°}{N} = \frac{360°}{8} = 45°$$

### Step 2 – APT statements

```
C = POINT/0.0, 0.0, 0.0
PTN1 = PATERN/ARC, C, RADIUS, 50.0, A_ST, 0.0, A_INC, 45.0, 8
```

### Step 3 – Generated coordinates (analytical table)

| Hole # | Angle (deg) | X (mm) | Y (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| PTN1.1 | 0 | 50.00 | 0.00 |
| PTN1.2 | 45 | 35.36 | 35.36 |
| PTN1.3 | 90 | 0.00 | 50.00 |
| PTN1.4 | 135 | -35.36 | 35.36 |
| PTN1.5 | 180 | -50.00 | 0.00 |
| PTN1.6 | 225 | -35.36 | -35.36 |
| PTN1.7 | 270 | 0.00 | -50.00 |
| PTN1.8 | 315 | 35.36 | -35.36 |

For hole $k$ (with $k = 1, 2, \dots, 8$):

$$P_k = (r\cos\theta_k, \; r\sin\theta_k, \; 0), \quad \theta_k = 0° + (k-1)\cdot 45°$$

---

## 3.5 Problem 5 – TABCYL Application (Aircraft Wing-like Surface)

> **Statement:** Define a tabulated cylinder that approximates the leading edge of an aircraft wing, given:
> - Cylindrical surface: `SURF1` of radius 500 mm.
> - Linear edge: `L1` from `P1(0, 0, 0)` to `P2(0, 0, 1500)` (vertical line, the wing root-to-tip axis).
> - Angular edge: `CRV1` on `SURF1` defined by `CIRCLE/0, 0, 0, 500`.

### Step 1 – Preliminary geometry

```
SURF1 = CYLNDR/0.0, 0.0, -100.0, 0.0, 0.0, 200.0, 500.0
P1 = POINT/0.0, 0.0, 0.0
P2 = POINT/0.0, 0.0, 1500.0
L1 = LINE/P1, P2
C1 = CIRCLE/0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 500.0
```

### Step 2 – Define the TABCYL surface

```
WING = TABCYL/SURF1, L1, C1
```

### Step 3 – Interpret

- The **angular edge C1** describes the airfoil's cross-sectional curvature (in the cylindrical projection).
- The **linear edge L1** is the spanwise direction.
- The TABCYL creates a **ruled surface** that approximates the wing skin — sufficient for 3-axis rough machining and the basis for higher-fidelity finishing.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Reference Matrix – Common Formulas in APT Geometry

| Operation | Formula | APT Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Distance between two points | $d = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$ | Use `L1 = LINE/P1, P2` and post-processor reports length. |
| Direction cosines of line P1→P2 | $\left(\dfrac{\Delta x}{d}, \dfrac{\Delta y}{d}, \dfrac{\Delta z}{d}\right)$ | Computed by APT processor. |
| Angle between two lines | $\cos\alpha = \hat{u}_1 \cdot \hat{u}_2$ | `ANGL` modifier uses this. |
| Centre of circle (3 points) | Solution of 3 linear equations | `CIR1 = CIRCLE/P1, P2, P3` |
| Bolt-circle radius | $r = \dfrac{c}{2\sin(\pi/N)}$ for a regular N-gon circumscribing a circle of diameter $c$ | Used in pattern design. |
| Pattern point (linear) | $P_k = P_1 + (k-1) \cdot \Delta \cdot \hat{u}$ | `PATERN/LINE, L, P1, INCR, Δ, N` |
| Pattern point (circular) | $P_k = C + r(\cos\theta_k, \sin\theta_k, 0)$ | `PATERN/ARC, C, RADIUS, r, A_ST, A_INC, N` |
| Rotation of a point | $\begin{bmatrix}x'\\y'\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y\end{bmatrix}$ | `ROTAT/ZAXIS, θ, P1` |
| Translation | $P' = P + \vec{t}$ | `TRANSL/tx, ty, tz, P1` |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

        A1[Initialization Statements<br/>PARTNO, REMARK] --> A2[Geometry Definition Section<br/>POINT, LINE, CIRCLE, PLANE,<br/>TABCYL, GCONE, PATERN]
        A2 --> A3[Motion Statements<br/>FROM, GOTO, GODLTA, INDIRP,<br/>GO/TO, GOFWD, GOBACK, GORGT, GOLFT]

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** The four blocks are **strictly sequential**. APT processor reads them in this order, generates the CL data, and a post-processor (separate from the APT processor) generates the machine-specific NC code.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an APT Geometry Statement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Command Word<br/>e.g. CIRCLE] --> B[Slash Separator /]
    B --> C[Modifier Data Pairs<br/>comma separated]
    C --> C1[Modifier 1: CENTER]
    C1 --> C2[Data: P1]
    C --> C3[Modifier 2: RADIUS]
    C3 --> C4[Data: 12.5]

    A --> D[Symbol Table Entry<br/>e.g. CIR1 equals]
    C --> D

    D --> E[APT Processor<br/>computes CL points]
    E --> F[Internal Cutter<br/>Location Array]

    style A fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFE4E6,stroke:#9F1239,color:#000
    style C fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#000
    style C4 fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#000
    style D fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000
    style E fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style F fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** Every geometry statement in APT is parsed in the same way. The slash separates the **command** from its **modifier-data pairs**. The `equals` sign is optional but is a *best practice* — it stores the geometry under a label in the symbol table.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A – 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Computer Aided Part Programming (CAP). List any four geometry commands used in APT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):**
> Computer Aided Part Programming (CAP) is a method of programming CNC machine tools in which the part programmer writes the machining instructions in a high-level, English-like symbolic language (such as APT) on a general-purpose computer. The computer automatically generates the Cutter Location (CL) data, which is then converted by a post-processor into machine-specific G-codes and M-codes.
>
> **Four Geometry Commands (1 mark — ¼ mark each):**
> 1. `POINT` – defines a point in 3-D space.
> 2. `LINE` – defines a straight line.
> 3. `CIRCLE` – defines a circle.
> 4. `PLANE` – defines a plane through three points.
> 5. *(Optional, also accepted)* `TABCYL`, `PATERN`, `GCONE`.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023] Differentiate between Manual Part Programming and Computer Aided Part Programming.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's 3-mark distribution:**
> - 1.5 marks for the first row / 1.5 marks for the second row. Use a small table or 3 clear bullet points.

| Sl. No. | Aspect | Manual Part Programming | Computer Aided Part Programming |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Language** | Low-level G-codes and M-codes | High-level symbolic language (APT) |
| 2 | **Computation** | Done manually by the programmer | Done automatically by the computer |
| 3 | **Skill Required** | Trigonometry + machine-code knowledge | Geometry + APT syntax |
| 4 | **Best Suited for** | Simple 2-D / 2.5-D parts | Complex 3-D contoured parts |
| 5 | **Machine Dependence** | Direct / machine-specific | Machine-independent (uses post-processor) |
| 6 | **Error Probability** | High | Low (with verification/simulation) |

---

## 5.2 PART B – 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) – Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain the **concept and need for Computer Aided Part Programming (CAP)**. Discuss the role of the post-processor. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** With suitable examples, describe the various **forms of the CIRCLE command** in APT. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution – Q.A(a): Concept and Need for CAP (7 marks)**

**Step 1 – Concept (3 marks):**
- CAP is a method of programming NC/CNC machines using a high-level, English-like symbolic language such as APT.
- The programmer describes the **part geometry** and **tool motion** in symbolic form.
- The computer (mainframe or CAM workstation) computes the **CL data** (cutter location) automatically.
- A **post-processor** then converts the CL data into machine-specific G-codes and M-codes.

**Step 2 – Need (3 marks):**
- Complex 3-D contoured parts (e.g., aircraft wings, turbine blades) need thousands of cutter positions — manual calculation is impractical.
- Manual part programming is **time-consuming and error-prone**; CAP drastically reduces programming time and errors.
- One APT program can be re-used on **different CNC machines** by simply changing the post-processor.
- CAP enables **graphical tool-path simulation**, allowing the programmer to detect collisions and verify dimensional accuracy before machining.

**Step 3 – Role of the Post-Processor (1 mark):**
- The post-processor is a software module that converts **machine-independent CL data** into **machine-specific NC code** (M-codes, G-codes, address formats, axis configurations, tool-change logic).
- The same CL data + different post-processors = programs for Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important diagram that fetches full marks (mention in answer):**
> ```
> APT Source -> APT Processor -> CL Data -> Post-Processor -> G/M Code -> CNC
> ```

---

### **Model Solution – Q.A(b): Forms of the CIRCLE Command in APT (7 marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CIRCLE command has **eight principal forms**. Listing 6–8 fetches 7 marks.

**Form 1 – Center + radius (1 mark):**
```
CIR1 = CIRCLE/10.0, 20.0, 0.0, 8.0
```
A circle of radius 8 mm centred at (10, 20, 0).

**Form 2 – Center as a point + radius (0.5 mark):**
```
CIR1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, RADIUS, 8.0
```

**Form 3 – Through three points (1 mark):**
```
CIR1 = CIRCLE/P1, P2, P3
```
P1, P2, P3 must be non-collinear. APT processor solves for centre and radius automatically.

**Form 4 – Center + tangent to a line (0.5 mark):**
```
CIR1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, TANTO, L1
```

**Form 5 – Center + radius + tangent to a line (1 mark):**
```
CIR1 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, RADIUS, 10.0, TANTO, L1
```

**Form 6 – Tangent to two lines, given radius (1 mark):**
```
CIR1 = CIRCLE/TANTO, L1, TANTO, L2, RADIUS, 12.5
```
This is the **fillet** construction between two intersecting lines.

**Form 7 – Tangent to two circles, given radius (1 mark):**
```
CIR1 = CIRCLE/TANTO, CIR1, TANTO, CIR2, RADIUS, 15.0
```

**Form 8 – Tangent to a line and a circle, given radius (1 mark):**
```
CIR1 = CIRCLE/TANTO, L1, TANTO, CIR1, RADIUS, 20.0
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake:** Students often write `CIRCLE/CENTER, x, y, z, r` — WRONG. The CENTER modifier takes a **point label**, not raw coordinates.
>   - Correct: `CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, RADIUS, 8.0`
>   - Incorrect: `CIRCLE/CENTER, 10, 20, 0, 8.0`
> - **Another trap:** Forgetting to apply the **discrimination modifier (XLARGE/YSMALL)** when two valid circles exist for a tangent-radius construction. Always check for multiple solutions.
> - **Always include `FINI`** as the last statement in an APT program (worth ½ mark in KTU valuation).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) – Choice 2 (Internal Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]**

> **(a)** Describe the **structure of an APT program** with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain the following APT commands with **one example each**: (i) `POINT`, (ii) `LINE`, (iii) `CIRCLE`, (iv) `TABCYL`. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution – Q.B(a): APT Program Structure (7 marks)**

**Step 1 – Definition (1 mark):**
- An APT program is a sequence of statements written in the APT language that describes a part's geometry and the tool motions required to machine it.

**Step 2 – Four blocks / sections of an APT program:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block 1 – Initialization (1 mark):**
> - `PARTNO/PartName` — assigns a part number/name (mandatory first statement).
> - `REMARK/...` — comment lines for documentation.
> - `MACHIN/CNC_VMC, 3AXIS` — declares the machine (optional in pure APT).
>
> **Block 2 – Geometry Definition (2 marks):**
> - Defines all points, lines, circles, planes, surfaces, patterns, and coordinate transformations required to describe the part.
> - These statements are **pre-processed** by the APT processor and stored in a **symbol table**.
> - Example statements: `P1 = POINT/...`, `L1 = LINE/...`, `CIR1 = CIRCLE/...`.
>
> **Block 3 – Motion Statements (2 marks):**
> - Specifies **how the tool moves** along the defined geometry.
> - Common statements: `FROM/P1`, `GOTO/P2`, `GODLTA/dx, dy, dz`, `GO/PAST, L1`, `GO/ON, L1, PAST, CIR1`, `GOFWD/CIR1`, `GOBACK/CIR1`, `GORGT/L1`, `GOLFT/L1`, `TLLFT, GORGT/L1`, `INDIRP/...`.
>
> **Block 4 – Post-Processor and Auxiliary Statements (1 mark):**
> - `SPINDL/rpm, CLW` — spindle speed and direction.
> - `FEDRAT/mmpm, MMPM` — feed rate.
> - `COOLNT/ON` or `COOLNT/FLOOD` — coolant.
> - `TURRET/1` — tool selection.
> - `FINI` — mandatory end-of-program statement.

**Step 3 – Mention the workflow (mandatory for 7 marks):**
1. APT Source Program → 2. APT Processor → 3. CL Data File → 4. Post-Processor → 5. G/M Code → 6. CNC Machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block diagram (mention in answer; this fetches 1 extra mark):**
> ```
> +-------------------------+
> |  PARTNO / REMARK        |
> +-------------------------+
> |  GEOMETRY DEFINITIONS   |
> |  (POINT, LINE, CIRCLE)  |
> +-------------------------+
> |  MOTION STATEMENTS      |
> |  (FROM, GOTO, GODLTA)  |
