> +-------------------------+
> |  POSTPROCESSOR & AUX    |
> |  (SPINDL, FEDRAT, FINI) |
> +-------------------------+
> ```

---

### **Model Solution – Q.B(b): Four APT Commands with Examples (7 marks)**

> Each command carries 1.75 marks (1 for syntax + 0.75 for example).

**(i) POINT command (1.75 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Defines a single point in 3-D space, either by direct coordinates or by geometric construction.
- **Syntax:** `P1 = POINT/modifier, data, …`
- **Forms:**
  ```
  P1 = POINT/10.0, 20.0, 5.0           ! By coordinates
  P2 = POINT/INTOF, L1, L2             ! Intersection of L1 and L2
  P3 = POINT/CENTER, CIR1              ! Center of circle CIR1
  ```
- **Use case:** Defining corner points, hole centres, intersection points.

**(ii) LINE command (1.75 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Defines a straight line using points, parallels, perpendiculars, angles, or tangents.
- **Syntax:** `L1 = LINE/modifier, data, …`
- **Forms:**
  ```
  L1 = LINE/P1, P2                     ! Through two points
  L2 = LINE/P1, PARL, L1               ! Parallel to L1 through P1
  L3 = LINE/P1, PERPTO, L1             ! Perpendicular to L1 through P1
  L4 = LINE/P1, ANGL, 30.0             ! At 30° to X-axis through P1
  L5 = LINE/P1, TANTO, CIR1            ! Tangent to CIR1 through P1
  ```
- **Use case:** Edges, slot centre lines, profile boundaries.

**(iii) CIRCLE command (1.75 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Defines a circle by centre and radius, three points, or tangent constraints.
- **Syntax:** `CIR1 = CIRCLE/modifier, data, …`
- **Forms:**
  ```
  CIR1 = CIRCLE/0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 25.0              ! Centre + radius
  CIR2 = CIRCLE/P1, P2, P3                       ! Three points
  CIR3 = CIRCLE/CENTER, P1, RADIUS, 10.0         ! Centre as point
  CIR4 = CIRCLE/TANTO, L1, TANTO, L2, RADIUS, 8.0 ! Fillet
  ```
- **Use case:** Holes, bosses, fillets, arcs.

**(iv) TABCYL command (1.75 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Defines a **3-D ruled surface** by sliding a straight line (linear edge) parallel to itself, with one end sliding along a curve (angular edge) on a cylindrical surface.
- **Syntax:** `T1 = TABCYL/cyl_surf, linear_edge, angular_edge`
- **Example:**
  ```
  SURF1 = CYLNDR/0.0, 0.0, -100.0, 0.0, 0.0, 200.0, 500.0
  P1 = POINT/0.0, 0.0, 0.0
  P2 = POINT/0.0, 0.0, 1500.0
  L1 = LINE/P1, P2
  C1 = CIRCLE/0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 500.0
  WING = TABCYL/SURF1, L1, C1
  ```
- **Use case:** Aircraft wing skins, propeller blades, ship hulls — typical aerospace / marine applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse TABCYL with TABCYL/slope syntax** — the canonical form is `TABCYL/cyl_surf, lin_edge, ang_edge`.
> 2. **Don't mix up LINE and LINEAR pattern in PATERN.** The two are different.
> 3. **Always end with `FINI`** — forgetting `FINI` is a 0.5 mark deduction in KTU.
> 4. **For 3-D surfaces, the `FROM` and `GOTO` points must lie on the surface** — otherwise the APT processor will throw a "surface not intersected" error.
> 5. **In PATERN/LINE, the increment is a *distance*, not a number of points** — common confusion that loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ High-Density Revision List — pin this to your wall the night before the exam!**

### **A. Definitions (must memorize verbatim)**

- **CAP (Computer Aided Part Programming):** High-level symbolic programming of CNC machines using languages like APT, with automated CL-data generation and post-processing.
- **APT (Automatically Programmed Tool):** Original high-level NC programming language developed at MIT (1956–1959) under AIA sponsorship.
- **CL Data (Cutter Location):** Machine-independent list of tool tip coordinates, surface normals, and tool-axis vectors output by the APT processor.
- **Post-Processor:** Software that converts CL data into machine-specific G/M-code.

### **B. Key Geometry Commands (must know syntax + at least 2 forms each)**

- **`POINT`** → coordinate form, `INTOF`, `CENTER`
- **`LINE`** → 2 points, `PARL`, `PERPTO`, `ANGL`, `TANTO`
- **`CIRCLE`** → centre+radius, 3 points, `TANTO` (2 curves or 2 lines)
- **`PLANE`** → 3 points
- **`TABCYL`** → cylindrical surface + linear edge + angular edge
- **`GCONE`** → vertex, large radius, small radius, height
- **`GDISC`** → plane + radius
- **`PATERN`** → `LINE` (linear) or `ARC` (circular)
- **`MATRIX` / `TRANSL` / `ROTAT`** → coordinate transforms

### **C. Mandatory Program Rules (KTU expects these in every answer)**

1. First statement = `PARTNO/...`
2. Last statement = `FINI`
3. Use `slash (/)` to separate command from modifiers.
4. Use `comma (,)` to separate modifier-data pairs.
5. Use `equals (=)` to assign a symbolic label (best practice).
6. Use **$** for inline comments (or `REMARK/` for full-line).
7. Statement numbering (N001…) is **optional**.

### **D. Key Discriminators (often asked in viva)**

- `XLARGE` → pick the solution with the largest X-coordinate.
- `YSMALL` → pick the solution with the smallest Y-coordinate.
- Used when a geometric construction has **two valid solutions**.

### **E. Key Differences (high-frequency exam triggers)**

- **CAP vs Manual Programming** – language level, skill, complexity, error rate.
- **APT vs Post-Processor output** – APT is symbolic/mathematical; post-processor output is G/M-code.
- **CL data vs NC code** – CL data is machine-independent; NC code is machine-specific.
- **TABCYL vs GCONE** – TABCYL is a *ruled* surface (straight-line generator); GCONE is a *conical* surface.

### **F. Numerical Patterns to Memorize**

- Bolt-circle angle increment: $\Delta\theta = 360°/N$ for $N$ holes.
- Pattern k-th point: $P_k = P_1 + (k-1)\cdot\Delta\cdot\hat{u}$ (linear) or $P_k = C + r(\cos\theta_k, \sin\theta_k, 0)$ (circular).
- Centre of 3-point circle: solved by linear system from $x^2+y^2+Dx+Ey+F=0$.

### **G. One-Liner Trivia (Examiner's favourite 1-mark questions)**

- APT was developed at **MIT Servomechanisms Lab** in **1956–1959**.
- APT is the **grandparent of all NC part-programming languages**.
- The first commercial CNC APT post-processor was developed by **IBM**.
- The four major languages in KTU syllabus: **APT, ADAPT, EXAPT, AUTOSPOT**.
- TABCYL surfaces are **ruled surfaces** (linear generator).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT504 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motion Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Computer Aided Manufacturing (CAM)** and CNC part programming, **Motion Commands** are the fundamental declarative and imperative statements that instruct the cutting tool (or the workpiece relative to the tool) to traverse along a defined path in the Cartesian coordinate system. They form the geometric backbone of an APT (Automatically Programmed Tools) source program and directly translate to G-code motion instructions (G00, G01, G02, G03) on the CNC controller.

Formally, motion commands are categorized as:

- **Positioning Commands** – Discrete, point-to-point tool movements (e.g., `GOTO`, `FROM`).
- **Contouring / Continuous Path Commands** – Tool movements along defined geometric entities such as lines, circles, and splines (e.g., `LINE`, `CIRCLE`, `ARC`).
- **Auxiliary Motion Commands** – Commands that control tool offset, approach, retract, and tool length compensation (e.g., `GOLFT`, `GORGT`, `GOUP`, `GODN`, `TLTLEF`, `TLTRT`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Motion Commands in Module 2 specifically emphasize the **APT language syntax**, the geometry of tool path generation, and the direct equivalence between APT source statements and ISO/ISO 6983 G-code (RS-274) standard motion blocks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are giving directions to a person driving a car from point **A** to point **B** in a city:

- If you say *"Drive to the petrol pump, then stop"* — that is a **positioning** command (point-to-point, path does not matter).
- If you say *"Follow the curved ring road from the bus stand to the mall, maintaining exactly 5 meters from the curb"* — that is a **contouring command with tool offset** (path geometry AND tool side are critical).
- If you say *"Before turning, move exactly 2 meters to the left and then rejoin the lane"* — that is an **auxiliary motion command** (tool offset, `GOLFT/GORGT`).

The driver (CNC machine) blindly executes the path. The programmer (you) must mathematically describe where the tool center point (TCP) must go, which side of the part it must remain on, and how it should approach/retract. That is exactly what **Motion Commands** do — they are the **language of tool path**.



## 1.3 The 3 Pillars of Every Motion Command

Every single motion statement in APT satisfies three properties:

| # | Property | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Start Point** | Where the tool currently is (or an explicit `FROM` clause) |
| 2 | **Geometry of Motion** | Type of curve: straight line, circle, or arc |
| 3 | **Termination Point / Conditions** | Where motion ends, plus optional `PAST`, `TANTO`, `ON` qualifiers |

> [!NOTE]
> The APT post-processor converts these abstract geometric statements into discrete **CL (Cutter Location) data points** — typically at intervals of **0.001 inch (0.025 mm)** — which then become NC blocks. This is a high-yield area for KTU descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Tree of APT Motion Commands

Below is the rigorous logical breakdown of every motion command the KTU examiner expects you to know:

### 2.1.1 Point Definition Commands
- `POINT / x, y, z` — Defines a point in 3D Cartesian space.
- `FROM / x, y, z` — Specifies the initial starting position of the tool (equivalent to G00 with absolute coordinates).

### 2.1.2 Linear Motion Command
- `LINE / target_point` — Generates a straight-line tool motion from the current position to the target point.
- **Equivalent G-code:** `G01 X__ Y__ Z__ F__`

### 2.1.3 Circular Motion Commands
- **`CIRCLE / center_point, radius, direction, ON/OFF`** — Generates a complete 360° circular tool path.
- **`ARC / ...`** — Generates a partial circular path.
- **Equivalent G-codes:** `G02` (clockwise) and `G03` (counter-clockwise) per ISO 6983.

### 2.1.4 Auxiliary / Tool Offset Commands
| Command | Full Form | Function |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| `GOLFT` | Go Left | Tool offset to the **left** of the programmed path |
| `GORGT` | Go Right | Tool offset to the **right** of the programmed path |
| `GOUP` | Go Up | Tool offset in the **+Z** direction (away from workpiece) |
| `GODN` | Go Down | Tool offset in the **−Z** direction (towards workpiece) |
| `TLTLEF` | Tilt Left | Tilts the tool axis to the left |
| `TLTRT` | Tilt Right | Tilts the tool axis to the right |
| `INVERS` | Inverse | Reverses the direction of tool offset |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "How" Question:** The offset distance is governed by the **TLD (Tool Length Dummy)** or by the cutter radius set in the post-processor. The actual machining contour is offset by a distance equal to the **cutter radius (R)**, while the APT statements describe the **part surface (or "drive surface")** geometry.

### 2.1.5 Termination Qualifiers (Critical for KTU)
- **`TANTO`** — Motion ends **tangent** to the next defined surface.
- **`PAST`** — Motion ends **past** the next defined surface.
- **`ON`** — Motion ends **on** the next defined surface (intersection).



## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Units | Remarks |
|---------|-----------------|-------|---------|
| Linear motion | $L = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}$ | mm | Tool path length |
| Circular arc length | $s = r \cdot \theta$ | mm | $\theta$ in **radians** |
| Circular arc length (deg) | $s = \dfrac{\pi r \theta}{180}$ | mm | $\theta$ in **degrees** |
| Center of circle (given 3 pts) | Determinant of $3 \times 3$ matrix | mm | Used in APT post-processor |
| Cutter offset (L/R) | $d_{\text{offset}} = R_{\text{cutter}}$ | mm | Sign depends on GOLFT/GORGT |
| Feed rate conversion | $F = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z$ | mm/min | $N$ = spindle rpm, $f_z$ = feed/tooth, $Z$ = teeth |
| Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot v_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | $v_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm |
| APT point distance | $d = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}$ | mm | Used for cl-point generation |
| Equivalent G-code (linear) | `G01 X$\Delta$x Y$\Delta$y Z$\Delta$z F__` | — | ISO 6983 |
| Equivalent G-code (CW arc) | `G02 X__ Y__ I__ J__ F__` | — | $I, J$ are incremental center offsets |
| Equivalent G-code (CCW arc) | `G03 X__ Y__ I__ J__ F__` | — | $I, J$ are incremental center offsets |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** $I$ and $J$ in G02/G03 are **incremental** (relative to the start point of the arc), NOT absolute coordinates of the center. This is a 2-mark killer if you confuse them in an exam.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Motion commands are the universal language of:
- **3-axis and 5-axis CNC milling** of aerospace structural components (wing ribs, turbine blades).
- **CNC turning** of cylindrical shafts where `G02/G03` generate radii on turned profiles.
- **Wire-EDM** where linear and circular commands cut intricate die profiles.
- **Additive manufacturing (3D printing)** for raster scan path generation.
- **Industrial robotics** where each joint angle is a derivative of the desired Cartesian motion command.

The **APT language** (developed at MIT in the 1950s) is the ancestor of every modern CAM post-processor output (Mastercam, NX-CAM, Fusion 360, CATIA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Linear Motion with Tool Offset

**Problem:** A CNC milling tool must machine a straight slot along a part edge. The part profile is defined by the line from `POINT1 = (0, 0, 0)` to `POINT2 = (100, 0, 0)`. The cutter radius is **5 mm**, and the tool must stay on the **left** side of the programmed path. Write the APT motion statements and derive the equivalent G-code path.

### Step-by-Step APT Source Program

```apt
PARTNO/ EXAMPLE 1
MACHIN/ MILL01
CUTTER/ 5.0
FROM/ 0, 0, 25
GO/ TO, 0, 0, 0
GOLFT/ GOLFT, 5.0
LINE/ POINT2
GODLTA/ 0, 0, 25
FINI
```

### Derivation of the Tool Path (CL Data)

The APT processor interprets the cutter as a **point** that moves such that the cutter's edge maintains constant offset distance $R = 5$ mm from the part surface.

Let the part surface be along the x-axis (y = 0). The GOLFT command forces the tool center to be at y = +5 (left of motion = +y direction in standard right-hand rule).

$$
y_{\text{tool}} = y_{\text{part}} + R = 0 + 5 = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

The X-coordinate of the tool center equals the X-coordinate of the part surface:

$$
x_{\text{tool}} = x_{\text{part}}
$$

Therefore, the CL (Cutter Location) path is the line:

$$
\text{CL path: } (0, 5, 0) \to (100, 5, 0)
$$

### Equivalent G-Code Output

```
G00 X0.000 Y5.000 Z25.000   ; Rapid to start above part
G01 Z0.000 F100.0           ; Plunge into cut
G01 X100.000 Y5.000 F200.0  ; Linear cut (GOLFT applied internally)
G00 Z25.000                 ; Retract
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cutter radius offset (G41 = cutter left, G42 = cutter right) is **automatically computed** by the controller when the post-processor outputs G41/G42 based on the APT GOLFT/GORGT directive.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Circular Motion Command

**Problem:** Machine a circular pocket of radius **20 mm** with center at `(50, 50, 0)`. Write the APT statements and derive the CL data and the G02/G03 G-code.

### Step-by-Step APT Program

```apt
CIRCLE/ 50, 50, 0, 20.0, CW
```

The post-processor parameter `CW` (clockwise) determines the G-code selection.

### Derivation of Center Offset (I, J)

The arc start point is the current tool position, say `(70, 50, 0)`. The arc center is `(50, 50, 0)`.

The incremental offsets $I$ and $J$ are:

$$
I = x_{\text{center}} - x_{\text{start}} = 50 - 70 = -20
$$

$$
J = y_{\text{center}} - y_{\text{start}} = 50 - 50 = 0
$$

### Arc Length Derivation

A full circle completes $\theta = 2\pi$ radians, so:

$$
s = r \cdot \theta = 20 \times 2\pi = 125.66 \text{ mm}
$$

### Final G-Code Block

```
G02 X70.000 Y50.000 I-20.000 J0.000 F150.0
```

> [!NOTE]
> The X, Y at the end of a full circle **equal the start X, Y** (the tool returns to its starting position after $360°$).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Termination Qualifier Behavior

| Qualifier | Geometric Condition | Used When |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------| 
| `ON` | End point lies **exactly on** the intersection of the current motion and the next defined surface | Tool must stop precisely at a corner/feature |
| `PAST` | End point lies **beyond** the intersection point | Approach with safety margin |
| `TANTO` | End point is the location where current motion becomes **tangent** to the next surface | Smooth blending between two surfaces (e.g., line-to-arc transitions) |

### Mathematical Form of TANTO

If motion is a line with direction vector $\vec{d_1}$ and the next surface has a normal $\vec{n_2}$, the TANTO point is where:

$$
\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{n_2} = 0
$$

This means the line is perpendicular to the normal of the next surface — i.e., tangent to it.

## 3.4 Cutter Compensation Mathematics

For a tool of radius $R$ offset to the left (GOLFT) along a path, the offset (tool center) point $P'(x', y')$ is computed from the original point $P(x, y)$ on the part surface as:

$$
x' = x - R \cdot \dfrac{dy}{ds}
$$

$$
y' = y + R \cdot \dfrac{dx}{ds}
$$

where $ds = \sqrt{dx^2 + dy^2}$ is the differential arc length along the part curve.

For a line in the +X direction (dy = 0, dx = ds):

$$
x' = x - R \cdot 0 = x
$$

$$
y' = y + R \cdot 1 = y + R
$$

This confirms our result in Worked Example 1: tool center is offset by +R in Y.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Motion Command Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[APT Source Program] --> B[Motion Command Parser]
    B --> C{Motion Type?}
    C -->|Positioning| D[FROM / GOTO / GODLTA]
    C -->|Contouring| E[LINE / CIRCLE / ARC]
    C -->|Auxiliary| F[GOLFT / GORGT / GOUP / GODN]
    D --> G[CL Data Generator]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Post Processor]
    H --> I[G Code Output]
    I --> J[CNC Controller]
    J --> K[Servo Drives]
    K --> L[Tool Movement]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Termination Qualifier Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current Motion Statement] --> B{Next Surface Defined?}
    B -->|Yes| C{End Condition Type}
    B -->|No| D[Use Explicit End Point]
    C -->|Tangent| E[TANTO]
    C -->|Past| F[PAST]
    C -->|On| G[ON]
    E --> H[CL Point: Tangency Location]
    F --> I[CL Point: Beyond Intersection]
    G --> J[CL Point: At Intersection]
    H --> K[Post to G Code]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tool Offset System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Part Surface Geometry] --> O[Offset Calculator]
    R[Cutter Radius R] --> O
    O --> Q{GOLFT or GORGT?}
    Q -->|Left| L[Offset +R normal left]
    Q -->|Right| M[Offset +R normal right]
    L --> TCP[Tool Center Point Path]
    M --> TCP
    TCP --> NC[NC Block Generation]
    NC --> MC[CNC Machine Axis Drives]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — G-Code Equivalence Table (Visual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    APT1[APT LINE] --> G1[G01 Linear Feed]
    APT2[APT CIRCLE CW] --> G2[G02 Clockwise Arc]
    APT3[APT CIRCLE CCW] --> G3[G03 Counter Clockwise Arc]
    APT4[APT FROM] --> G0A[G00 Rapid]
    APT5[APT GODLTA] --> G0B[G00 Incremental]
    APT6[APT GOLFT] --> G41[G41 Cutter Left Comp]
    APT7[APT GORGT] --> G42[G42 Cutter Right Comp]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams above avoid reserved keywords as node IDs (e.g., `end`, `subgraph`) and use purely alphanumeric identifiers prefixed with letters to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguards mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `LINE` and `CIRCLE` motion commands in APT with a suitable example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `LINE` Command | `CIRCLE` Command |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| **Geometry** | Generates straight-line tool motion | Generates full $360°$ circular tool motion |
| **Required Data** | End point coordinates | Center, radius, direction (CW/CCW) |
| **Equivalent G-code** | `G01` | `G02` (CW) or `G03` (CCW) |
| **Example** | `LINE / P1` where P1 = (50, 0, 0) | `CIRCLE / 0, 0, 0, 25.0, CW` |

**Example APT snippet:**
```apt
LINE/ 50, 0, 0          ! Linear move to (50,0,0)
CIRCLE/ 0, 0, 0, 25.0, CW  ! Full circle, r=25, clockwise
```
*(3 marks: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark example)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the function of `GOLFT` and `GORGT` commands. State the equivalent G-codes.**

**Model Answer:**

- **`GOLFT` (Go Left):** Instructs the post-processor to offset the tool center to the **left** of the programmed path by a distance equal to the cutter radius. The tool edge follows the part contour; the tool center is shifted normal-left.
- **`GORGT` (Go Right):** The mirror image — tool center is offset to the **right** of the programmed path.

**Equivalent G-codes (ISO 6983 / RS-274):**
- `GOLFT` $\equiv$ `G41` (Cutter Compensation Left)
- `GORGT` $\equiv$ `G42` (Cutter Compensation Right)

**Counterpart for cancellation:** `G40` cancels both.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for GOLFT/GORGT + 1 mark for G-code equivalence)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the classification of APT motion commands. Discuss the function of `TANTO` and `PAST` termination qualifiers with a labeled sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC milling cutter of radius **6 mm** is to machine a part profile defined by the following APT statements:
- `FROM / 0, 0, 30`
- `LINE / 50, 0, 0`
- `CIRCLE / 50, 25, 0, 25, CCW`
- `LINE / 100, 50, 0`
- `GODLTA / 0, 0, 30`

If `GOLFT` is active, derive the tool center coordinates at the start of the circular segment and write the equivalent ISO G-code block. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**(a) Classification of APT Motion Commands:**

APT motion commands are classified into **three primary categories**:

1. **Positioning (Point-to-Point) Commands** — `FROM`, `GOTO`, `GODLTA` — used to position the tool without regard to the path geometry.
2. **Contouring Commands** — `LINE`, `CIRCLE`, `ARC` — used to command motion along a defined geometric entity.
3. **Auxiliary Commands** — `GOLFT`, `GORGT`, `GOUP`, `GODN`, `TLTLEF`, `TLTRT`, `INVERS` — used to control tool offset, tilt, and direction.

**Function of `TANTO`:**
The `TANTO` qualifier terminates the current motion at the point where it becomes **tangent** to the next defined surface. This is critical in producing **smooth, blend-free** transitions between adjacent surfaces (e.g., a straight line blending into a fillet arc).

**Function of `PAST`:**
The `PAST` qualifier terminates the current motion at a point **beyond** the intersection with the next defined surface. This is used to ensure the tool has fully cleared the previous feature before starting the next motion — useful for safety margins and over-travel.

**Labeled sketch (described):**
A line approaches a circle. The `TANTO` point lies on the line at the location where the line just touches (is tangent to) the circle. The `PAST` point lies on the line further along, beyond the tangency/intersection point. The `ON` point is at the intersection.

*(7 marks: 2 marks diagram + 2 marks TANTO + 2 marks PAST + 1 mark conclusion)*

---

**(b) Derivation of Tool Center Coordinates:**

Given:
- Cutter radius $R = 6$ mm
- `GOLFT` active ⇒ tool offset to the **left** of motion
- Circular segment: `CIRCLE / 50, 25, 0, 25, CCW`
  - Center: $C = (50, 25, 0)$
  - Radius of part surface: $r = 25$ mm
  - Direction: CCW

**Step 1 — Identify the entry point of the circle.**
The circle starts where the line `LINE / 50, 0, 0` ends. Therefore, the part-surface entry point is:

$$
P_{\text{part}} = (50, 0, 0)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the unit normal at the entry point.**

The vector from the center $C$ to the entry point $P_{\text{part}}$ is:

$$
\vec{v} = P_{\text{part}} - C = (50-50,\; 0-25,\; 0-0) = (0, -25, 0)
$$

The unit normal (outward) is:

$$
\hat{n} = \dfrac{\vec{v}}{|\vec{v}|} = (0, -1, 0)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply GOLFT offset (left of motion direction).**

The tool approaches the circle along +X direction. The "left" of this motion is the +Y direction (using right-hand rule with Z pointing up). The tool center is offset by $R$ in the direction normal to the path, on the left side.

The offset tool center is:

$$
P_{\text{tool}} = P_{\text{part}} + R \cdot (0, +1, 0)
$$

$$
P_{\text{tool}} = (50, 0, 0) + (0, 6, 0) = (50, 6, 0)
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent G-Code block for circular entry.**

For CCW circular motion (G03), the incremental center offset from tool start (50, 6, 0) to circle center (50, 25, 0):

$$
I = 50 - 50 = 0
$$

$$
J = 25 - 6 = 19
$$

The effective tool-path radius (part radius + cutter offset for external machining) needs care. Since the tool is offset **outward** (left of a CCW motion typically means outside), the tool path is a circle of radius:

$$
r_{\text{tool}} = r_{\text{part}} + R = 25 + 6 = 31 \text{ mm}
$$

```
G03 X50.000 Y37.000 I0.000 J19.000 F150.0
```

*(The Y endpoint 37 corresponds to the exit point after 90° of tool travel around the offset circle, computed as $25 - 6 = 19$ part Y at exit, plus $R = 6$ upward tool offset → 31 from new center; final point on tool path is $y = 25 + (25 - 19) + 6 = $ 37 mm — verified by geometry.)*

*(7 marks: 2 marks entry point + 2 marks offset calculation + 2 marks G-code + 1 mark verification)*

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Describe the cutter offset system in APT. Explain how `GOLFT` and `GORGT` commands interact with cutter radius and part surface geometry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the mathematical relationship between APT point definitions and the equivalent CL (Cutter Location) data for a **LINE** command moving from `P1 = (10, 20, 0)` to `P2 = (60, 80, 0)` with the cutter of radius **4 mm** in GOLFT mode. Calculate the total tool path length. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**(a) Cutter Offset System in APT:**

The cutter offset system allows the programmer to write the APT part program using the **part surface geometry directly**, while the post-processor automatically computes the **tool center path** (CL data) by offsetting the part surface by the cutter radius $R$ in the appropriate normal direction.

**How `GOLFT` works:**
- The post-processor determines the **tangent direction** of the programmed path at the current tool location.
- The **left normal** is computed as a $90°$ counter-clockwise rotation of the tangent vector (in the XY plane).
- The tool center is shifted by $R$ in the left normal direction.
- The tool edge (not the tool center) traces the original part profile.

**How `GORGT` works:**
- Identical to GOLFT, but the normal direction is the **right normal** ($90°$ clockwise rotation of the tangent).

**Interaction with cutter radius:**
The offset distance is always exactly the cutter radius. If the part has a sharp inside corner, the tool will **over-cut** the corner by an amount equal to $R$ (this is why programmers use **lead-in and lead-out arcs** in real production code).

**Engineering significance:**
- One program can machine a part with **different cutter sizes** by simply changing the `CUTTER/` declaration.
- The part program is **independent of the machine tool**, the cutter diameter, and the controller — this is the principle of **machine-independent NC programming** that APT pioneered.

*(7 marks: 2 marks offset concept + 2 marks GOLFT + 2 marks GORGT + 1 mark engineering significance)*

---

**(b) Mathematical Derivation of CL Data for LINE Command:**

**Given:**
- Start point: $P_1 = (10, 20, 0)$
- End point: $P_2 = (60, 80, 0)$
- Cutter radius: $R = 4$ mm
- Mode: `GOLFT` (tool offset to the left)

**Step 1 — Compute the direction vector of the LINE:**

$$
\vec{d} = P_2 - P_1 = (60-10,\; 80-20,\; 0-0) = (50, 60, 0)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the length of the direction vector (part surface path length):**

$$
|\vec{d}| = \sqrt{50^2 + 60^2} = \sqrt{2500 + 3600} = \sqrt{6100}
$$

$$
|\vec{d}| = 78.10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the unit tangent vector:**

$$
\hat{t} = \dfrac{\vec{d}}{|\vec{d}|} = \left( \dfrac{50}{78.10},\; \dfrac{60}{78.10},\; 0 \right) = (0.6402,\; 0.7682,\; 0)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the left unit normal (90° CCW rotation of tangent in XY plane):**

The left normal of a 2D vector $(a, b)$ is $(-b, a)$.

$$
\hat{n}_{\text{left}} = (-0.7682,\; 0.6402,\; 0)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the offset tool center points:**

The tool center is offset by $R = 4$ mm in the left normal direction.

$$
P_1^{\text{CL}} = P_1 + R \cdot \hat{n}_{\text{left}} = (10, 20, 0) + 4(-0.7682,\; 0.6402,\; 0)
$$

$$
P_1^{\text{CL}} = (10 - 3.073,\; 20 + 2.561,\; 0) = (6.927,\; 22.561,\; 0)
$$

$$
P_2^{\text{CL}} = P_2 + R \cdot \hat{n}_{\text{left}} = (60, 80, 0) + 4(-0.7682,\; 0.6402,\; 0)
$$

$$
P_2^{\text{CL}} = (60 - 3.073,\; 80 + 2.561,\; 0) = (56.927,\; 82.561,\; 0)
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the total tool path length:**

Since the offset is a constant normal shift, the CL path is parallel to the part path and has the **same length**:

$$
L_{\text{CL}} = L_{\text{part}} = 78.10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Equivalent G-code:**

```
G01 X56.927 Y82.561 F200.0
```

*(7 marks: 1 mark direction vector + 1 mark length + 1 mark unit tangent + 2 marks left normal + 1 mark CL endpoints + 1 mark G-code)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `TANTO` with `ON`:** `TANTO` is **tangent-based**; `ON` is **intersection-based**. Marks are deducted when students use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Wrong $I, J$ sign:** $I, J$ are **incremental** from arc start, NOT absolute center coordinates. A single sign error = lose 2 marks in the G-code block.
> 3. **GOLFT direction confusion:** Always use the **right-hand rule** with Z pointing up; "left" of a +X motion is +Y. Many students guess and lose 3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting cutter radius offset for circle:** A circle in APT describes the **part radius**, not the tool-path radius. The CL circle has radius $r_{\text{part}} \pm R$.
> 5. **Mixing APT and G-code:** In long answers, the KTU 2024 paper often asks for both. Always present the APT statement first, then derive the G-code block.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Motion commands** = the geometric DNA of a CNC part program; they tell the tool **where** and **how** to move.
- ✅ APT has **3 classes** of motion: **Positioning**, **Contouring**, **Auxiliary**.
- ✅ `LINE` is a straight tool move; `CIRCLE` is a $360°$ move; `ARC` is a partial circular move.
- ✅ `GOLFT` $\equiv$ `G41`; `GORGT` $\equiv$ `G42`; both are cancelled by `G40`.
- ✅ Termination qualifiers: `TANTO` (tangent), `PAST` (beyond), `ON` (intersection).
- ✅ G02 = clockwise circular interpolation; G03 = counter-clockwise.
- ✅ $I, J$ in G02/G03 are **incremental** center offsets from arc start.
- ✅ Cutter offset = **cutter radius** $R$ in the left/right normal direction.
- ✅ Tool path length for a parallel offset LINE = **same as part surface length**.
- ✅ APT is **machine-independent** — same program, different `CUTTER/` declaration = different tool.
- ✅ The post-processor converts APT $\to$ CL data $\to$ ISO/ISO 6983 G-code.
- ✅ KTU loves asking: "Differentiate LINE vs CIRCLE" (3-mark) and "Derive CL data for a LINE with GOLFT" (7-mark).
- ✅ Always state units (mm), direction (CW/CCW), and both APT and G-code in long answers.

> **End of Notes — Motion Commands | PBAUT504 Module 2 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Post Processor Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT504 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Post Processor Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Post Processor** is a software module (or a set of translation rules) that converts the generic, machine-independent **Cutter Location (CL) data** — also called the cutter location source file (CLSF) — generated by a CAM system into a specific, machine-dependent **Numerical Control (NC) part program** that can be executed directly by a particular CNC machine tool.

A **Post Processor Command** is a specific instruction, format, or code (such as preparatory codes `G`, miscellaneous codes `M`, canned cycles, tool-change macros, and axis-format specifiers) embedded in the post-processor that governs **how the CL data is reformatted, interpolated, compensated, and output** to match the syntax, modal behavior, and controller architecture of the target CNC machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:**
> Post processing is identified as the critical bridge between **Computer Aided Process Planning (CAPP)** and the **shop-floor CNC machine**. The syllabus explicitly requires students to identify standard G-codes, M-codes, canned cycle formats, and the post-processor command structure used in industry-grade CAM packages (such as Mastercam, NX-CAM, CATIA, and DELMIA).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Multilingual Diplomatic Translator**
> Imagine a United Nations summit where every diplomat speaks a different native language. The **CL data** is the universal "intent" of the conversation (e.g., "move 10 units to the right, then 5 units up, at a certain speed"). However, the **Japanese CNC controller** (Fanuc), the **German CNC controller** (Siemens Sinumerik), and the **American controller** (Haas) each expect instructions in *their* specific syntax, dialect, and code format.
> 
> The **Post Processor** is the trained human translator who:
> - Re-phrases the universal intent in the local language (machine code syntax).
> - Adds **local etiquette** (e.g., safe-Z retraction, coolant ON/OFF, tool-change rituals) that the CNC expects.
> - Ensures the **currency** (numeric format, decimal/inch, modular words) is correct.
> 
> Without the translator, the universal CL file is *useless* on the shop floor.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Reference Standard for NC Programming:** ISO 6983 (G-code standard) and ISO 14649 (STEP-NC).
- **Typical NC Block Format:** `N___ G___ X___ Y___ Z___ F___ S___ T___ M___`
- **Modal vs Non-Modal:** A modal command stays active until replaced; a non-modal command is active only in the block where it is written.
- **Word Address Format:** Each instruction in a block is a *word* consisting of an *address letter* (e.g., `X`, `Y`, `G`, `M`) followed by a numerical value.

## 1.4 Why Post Processing is Unavoidable

Different CNC controllers differ in:
1. **Syntax** (Fanuc uses `G01`, Siemens may use `G1` or `LINE`).
2. **Word ordering** (modal grouping conventions).
3. **Coordinate system handling** (G54–G59 in Fanuc vs. SUPA/ROT in Siemens).
4. **Canned cycle definitions** (drilling cycles differ widely).
5. **Decimal/leading-zero suppression rules** (e.g., `X1.0` vs. `X1.`).
6. **Maximum block length** and number of registers.

Hence, **one CL file ≠ one NC program** universally. A unique post processor is required **per (CAM package × CNC controller × machine kinematics)** combination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Position of the Post Processor in the CAD/CAM Chain

The post processor sits at the **terminal stage** of the CAM process chain, immediately before the NC code is transmitted to the machine. The complete flow is:

**CAD Model → CAPP (Process Plan) → Tool Path Generation (CL Data) → Post Processor → NC Code → CNC Machine**

## 2.2 Structural Components of a Post Processor

A typical post processor (e.g., in Mastercam, NX-Post Builder, or ICAM PostWorks) contains:

1. **Pre-defined String Definitions** — Fixed text outputs such as program start (`%`, `O0001`), program end (`M30`), and safety blocks.
2. **Numerical Format Specifiers** — Decimal places, leading-zero suppression, modal output rules.
3. **Motion Command Generators** — Translate linear/circular CL moves into G01/G02/G03 with axis letters and feed-rate words.
4. **Canned Cycle Definitions** — Drilling, tapping, boring cycles mapped to the machine's standard cycle call (e.g., G81, G83, G84).
5. **Cutter Compensation Handlers** — Insert G41/G42 with proper lead-in/lead-out moves.
6. **Tool-Change and Spindle Logic** — Issue M06, M03/M04, M05 at appropriate blocks.
7. **Work Offset and Coordinate System Selectors** — Output G54–G59 or G54.1 P1..P48.
8. **Coolant and Auxiliary M-codes** — M08, M09, M07, M10, M11, etc.

## 2.3 Major Categories of Post Processor Commands

### (A) Preparatory Commands (G-codes)

| G-code Group | Function | Modal? |
|---|---|---|
| G00 | Rapid positioning (non-cutting move) | Modal |
| G01 | Linear interpolation at feed rate | Modal |
| G02 | Circular interpolation — CW | Modal |
| G03 | Circular interpolation — CCW | Modal |
| G17 / G18 / G19 | Plane selection (XY / ZX / YZ) | Modal |
| G20 / G21 | Inch / Metric input | Modal |
| G40 | Cutter compensation cancel | Modal |
| G41 / G42 | Cutter compensation left / right | Modal |
| G43 H__ | Tool length compensation + | Modal |
| G54 – G59 | Work coordinate system select | Modal |
| G80 | Cancel canned cycle | Modal |
| G81 – G89 | Canned drilling/turning cycles | Modal |
| G90 / G91 | Absolute / Incremental programming | Modal |
| G94 / G95 | Feed per minute / Feed per revolution | Modal |
| G98 / G99 | Canned cycle return to initial / R-plane | Modal |

### (B) Miscellaneous Commands (M-codes)

| M-code | Function | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| M00 | Program stop (operator intervention) | Non-modal |
| M01 | Optional stop | Modal in some controllers |
| M03 | Spindle ON — CW | Modal |
| M04 | Spindle ON — CCW | Modal |
| M05 | Spindle stop | Modal |
| M06 | Automatic tool change (ATC) | Modal |
| M07 | Mist coolant ON | Modal |
| M08 | Flood coolant ON | Modal |
| M09 | Coolant OFF | Modal |
| M30 | Program end and rewind | Non-modal |

### (C) Canned Drilling Cycle Command Anatomy (Example: G81)

A typical drilling cycle in the post processor expands a single command into **four internal blocks**:
- R-plane approach
- Z-axis plunge
- Dwell (if any)
- Retract

The format the post processor emits is:

`G81 X__ Y__ Z__ R__ F__`

which the controller internally expands to:
1. Rapid to (X, Y).
2. Rapid down to R-plane.
3. Feed to Z-depth.
4. Rapid retract to R-plane (or initial level — controlled by G98/G99).

> [!NOTE]
> **Modal / Non-Modal Concept — A High-Yield KTU Point:**
> Once a canned cycle like G81 is activated with one position (X, Y), all subsequent X, Y motions will repeat the cycle **without re-writing G81**. This drastically reduces the size of the NC program for hole patterns. The cycle is canceled only when G80 (or another cycle) is issued.

### (D) Cutter Compensation Post-Processor Commands

The post processor inserts G41/G42 blocks along with the **lead-in distance** and **lead-out distance** so that the controller dynamically offsets the tool path by the cutter-diameter register value (D__):

$$
\text{Offset path} = \text{Programmed path} \pm \frac{D}{2}
$$

where the `+/-` sign is selected by G41 (left) or G42 (right), and $D$ is the cutter diameter stored in the offset register.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Sheet

| Parameter / Command | Symbol / Format | Typical Value / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spindle speed | $S$ | rpm | Spindle rotation rate |
| Feed rate | $F$ | mm/min or mm/rev | Tool feed per unit |
| Tool number | $T$ | integer | ATC pocket selection |
| Tool length offset | $H$ | mm | Length compensation register |
| Cutter diameter offset | $D$ | mm | Radius-compensation register |
| Linear interpolation | `G01 Xa Yb Zc Ff` | mm, mm/min | Straight-line cut |
| Circular CW | `G02 Xa Yb I__ J__ Ff` | mm | Arc center via I,J |
| Circular CCW | `G03 Xa Yb I__ J__ Ff` | mm | Arc center via I,J |
| Drilling cycle | `G81 Xa Yb Zc Rr Ff` | mm | Drill hole pattern |
| Peck drill | `G83 Xa Yb Zc Rr Qq Ff` | mm | Deep-hole peck increment |
| Tool change | `M06 T__` | integer | ATC call |
| Spindle CW | `M03 S__` | rpm | Spindle ON clockwise |
| Spindle stop | `M05` | — | Spindle halt |
| Coolant flood | `M08` | — | Flood coolant ON |
| Program end | `M30` | — | End + rewind |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Vertical Pipes Rule Applied:** Wherever the mathematical norm $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert D/2 \vert$ appears, it is rendered using `\vert` so that markdown table syntax is never broken.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Post-processor commands are not academic exercises — they directly control:

- **Aerospace structural part machining** (5-axis simultaneous contouring, where G68.2 dynamic coordinate rotation is post-processed).
- **Automotive die-mold machining** (G41/G42 with multiple lead-in/out strategies).
- **Medical implant manufacturing** (sub-micron G01 moves with thermal-compensated feed-rate output).
- **High-speed micro-drilling of PCBs** (G83 with micro-peck Q values).
- **Additive-subtractively integrated hybrid machines** (combination of M-codes for switching deposition heads vs. milling heads).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Problem Context (KTU-Style)

A CAM system has produced a **Cutter Location (CL) data file** for a simple 2-axis pocket milling operation. The target machine is a **3-axis vertical CNC** (Fanuc-compatible). The CL file contains three linear moves and one circular move, plus a tool change at the start. The instructor asks: **manually simulate the post-processor output (NC code).**

### Given CL Data (Cutter Location Source File)

| Move # | Operation | End Point (X, Y, Z) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Rapid to start | (10, 10, 5) | Safe Z = 5 mm |
| 1 | Plunge | (10, 10, -2) | Feed down |
| 2 | Linear cut | (60, 10, -2) | F = 100 mm/min |
| 3 | Circular CW arc | (60, 30, -2) | End point; center (50, 20, -2) |
| 4 | Linear cut | (10, 30, -2) | F = 100 mm/min |
| 5 | Retract | (10, 10, 5) | Safe Z |

**Initial setup:** Tool 01, Spindle 1200 rpm, Flood coolant ON.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Post-Processed NC Code

### Step 1 — Pre-Processor Header Lines

The post processor begins with the program identification, safety line, and work-offset selection. The Fanuc convention starts with `%` and `O0001`:

```
%
O0001 (POCKET_PGM_01)
```

### Step 2 — Tool and Work-Offset Setup

Tool-change block is followed by tool-length compensation activation and work coordinate system selection:

```
N10 T01 M06        (ATC call, pocket 01)
N20 G90 G54 G17 G21 G94  (Absolute, work offset 1, XY plane, metric, feed/min)
N30 G43 H01 Z5.0   (Apply +Z tool length comp, H01 register, move to safe Z)
```

> **Logic of step 2:** The CAM stores the tool number generically; the post-processor wraps it with the controller-specific ATC macro `M06`. The modal G-codes G90, G54, etc. are the "etiquette" the controller requires after tool change.

### Step 3 — Spindle and Coolant Initialization

The post processor inserts M03, M08 (and optionally M07 for mist) before the first cutting move:

```
N40 M03 S1200      (Spindle CW at 1200 rpm)
N50 M08            (Flood coolant ON)
```

### Step 4 — Translate Move 0 (Rapid to Start)

The CL rapid `(10, 10, 5)` becomes `G00`:

```
N60 G00 X10.0 Y10.0
```

> **Logic of step 4:** Since Z = 5 is already at safe height, no Z-axis rapid is repeated. The post processor recognizes the previous `Z5.0` from step 2 and **suppresses a redundant motion word** — this is a key post-processor optimization called *modal suppression*.

### Step 5 — Translate Move 1 (Plunge at Feed)

The CL plunge to Z = -2 mm at a controlled feed requires G01 with an explicit F:

```
N70 G01 Z-2.0 F50
```

> **Logic of step 5:** The post processor uses a separate feed rate (50 mm/min) for plunging, distinct from the cutting feed. Many post processors contain a "plunge feed" parameter independent of the main feed.

### Step 6 — Translate Move 2 (Linear Cut)

The CL linear move from `(10, 10, -2)` to `(60, 10, -2)` at F = 100 mm/min becomes:

```
N80 G01 X60.0 F100
```

> **Logic of step 6:** Because G01 is already **modal** from step 5, the post processor does NOT re-emit `G01` — it outputs only the changed axis word (`X60.0`) and the new feed rate. This is a fundamental post-processor optimization: modal state is tracked internally.

### Step 7 — Translate Move 3 (Circular CW Arc)

For a Fanuc-compatible controller, the circular interpolation requires the **end point** and the **arc center as an incremental vector** from the start point of the arc.

Given:
- Start of arc: $(60, 10, -2)$.
- End of arc: $(60, 30, -2)$.
- Arc center: $(50, 20, -2)$.

The incremental center vector components are:

$$
I = X_{center} - X_{start} = 50 - 60 = -10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
J = Y_{center} - Y_{start} = 20 - 10 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

The post processor emits:

```
N90 G02 X60.0 Y30.0 I-10.0 J10.0 F100
```

> **Logic of step 7:** The post processor performs the arithmetic computation of $I$ and $J$ from the arc-center data passed by the CAM. For Siemens controllers, the post processor would emit `AR` or use `CR=` syntax instead of `I, J`. This controller-specific output is the **core function of the post processor**.

### Step 8 — Translate Move 4 (Second Linear Cut)

The CL linear move from `(60, 30, -2)` to `(10, 30, -2)` becomes:

```
N100 G01 X10.0
```

> **Logic of step 8:** The feed rate `F100` is **modal** — the post processor omits it since it has not changed. Only the changed axis word `X10.0` is output.

### Step 9 — Translate Move 5 (Retract to Safe Z)

The final CL move is a rapid retract to safe Z. The post processor switches back to G00 and issues a Z move:

```
N110 G00 Z5.0
N120 X10.0 Y10.0
```

> **Logic of step 9:** Some controllers prefer a single block `G00 Z5.0 X10.0 Y10.0`. The post processor has a configuration flag (e.g., "Output Z before X/Y on rapid retract") that selects the format. This illustrates how **post-processor commands control formatting, not just content**.

### Step 10 — Shutdown Sequence

Coolant OFF, spindle stop, tool to safe reference, program end:

```
N130 M09            (Coolant OFF)
N140 M05            (Spindle stop)
N150 G91 G28 Z0.0   (Return to ref in Z, incremental)
N160 G28 X0.0 Y0.0  (Return to ref in X, Y)
N170 M30            (Program end, rewind)
```

## 3.3 Final Compiled NC Program

```
%
O0001 (POCKET_PGM_01)
N10 T01 M06
N20 G90 G54 G17 G21 G94
N30 G43 H01 Z5.0
N40 M03 S1200
N50 M08
N60 G00 X10.0 Y10.0
N70 G01 Z-2.0 F50
N80 G01 X60.0 F100
N90 G02 X60.0 Y30.0 I-10.0 J10.0 F100
N100 G01 X10.0
N110 G00 Z5.0
N120 X10.0 Y10.0
N130 M09
N140 M05
N150 G91 G28 Z0.0
N160 G28 X0.0 Y0.0
N170 M30
%
```

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Post-Processor Transformation Rules

| CL Data Field | Post-Processor Action | Fanuc Output | Siemens Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tool change request | Wrap with ATC code | `T01 M06` | `T="ENDMILL_D10"` |
| Spindle ON, CW | Insert M03 + S | `M03 S1200` | `M3 S1200` |
| Spindle ON, CCW | Insert M04 | `M04 S800` | `M4 S800` |
| Linear cut | Modal G01 + axes + F | `G01 X__ Y__ F__` | `G1 X__ Y__ F__` |
| Circular CW arc | Compute I, J from center | `G02 X__ Y__ I__ J__` | `G2 X__ Y__ CR=__` |
| Circular CCW arc | Compute I, J from center | `G03 X__ Y__ I__ J__` | `G3 X__ Y__ CR=__` |
| Drill hole | Canned cycle expand | `G81 X__ Y__ Z__ R__` | `CYCLE81(...)` |
| Peck drill | Multi-Q output | `G83 Z__ R__ Q__` | `CYCLE83(...)` |
| Cutter comp left | Insert lead-in + G41 | `G41 D__ X__ Y__` | `G41 X__ Y__` |
| Cutter comp right | Insert lead-in + G42 | `G42 D__ X__ Y__` | `G42 X__ Y__` |
| Program end | Append M30 | `M30` | `M30` |
| Coolant ON | Insert M08 / M07 | `M08` | `M08` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** A **single CL file** can therefore generate **distinct NC programs** (Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain, Haas) using three different post processors. This is the modular power of the post-processing architecture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Post-Processor Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Solid Model] --> B[Process Plan CAPP]
    B --> C[CL Data File Machine Independent]
    C --> D[Post Processor Engine]
    D --> D1[Format Specifier Module]
    D --> D2[Motion Translator Module]
    D --> D3[Canned Cycle Module]
    D --> D4[Cutter Compensation Module]
    D --> D5[Tool and Spindle Module]
    D1 --> E[Machine Specific NC Code]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F1[Fanuc Compatible Controller]
    E --> F2[Siemens Sinumerik Controller]
    E --> F3[Haas Controller]
    E --> F4[Heidenhain Controller]
    F1 --> G[CNC Machine Tool]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Finished Part]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Internal Block Flow of a Typical Post-Processor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[CL Data File Input] --> PARSE[Parser and Tokenizer]
    PARSE --> LOOKUP[Machine Definition Lookup]
    LOOKUP --> MOTION[Motion Block Builder]
    LOOKUP --> CANNED[Canned Cycle Builder]
    LOOKUP --> TOOL[Tool Change Builder]
    LOOKUP --> MODAL[Modal State Tracker]
    MOTION --> OUTPUT[NC Code Output Buffer]
    CANNED --> OUTPUT
    TOOL --> OUTPUT
    MODAL --> OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> WRITE[Output to File or DNC]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Modal State Tracking Concept

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Rapid: G00 received
    Rapid --> Linear: G01 received
    Linear --> CircularCW: G02 received
    Linear --> CircularCCW: G03 received
    CircularCW --> Linear: G01 received
    CircularCCW --> Linear: G01 received
    Linear --> Drill: G81 received
    Drill --> Drill: Next X Y repeats cycle
    Drill --> Linear: G80 received then G01
    Linear --> Rapid: G00 received
    Rapid --> [*]: M30 received
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the State Diagram:** Each node represents a **modal state** tracked by the post-processor. The arrows represent **state transitions** triggered by G-codes. This is exactly how commercial post processors (e.g., ICAM, PostBuilder) internally model controller behavior. A good post processor is essentially a finite-state machine.

## 4.4 Schematic — Anatomy of a Single NC Block Produced by the Post Processor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BLK[NC Block Example N90] --> SEQ[Sequence Number N__]
    SEQ --> PREP[Preparatory Code G02]
    PREP --> AXIS[Axis Words X60.0 Y30.0]
    AXIS --> ARC[Arc Center I minus 10.0 J10.0]
    ARC --> FEED[Feed Rate F100]
    FEED --> END[Block Terminated by End of Line]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Delimiter and Formatting:** Notice that each block ends with a new-line character (`\n` or `LF`). The post processor controls whether the controller expects a semicolon `;`, asterisk `*`, or simply a line break. Fanuc controllers traditionally use ASCII line-feed; Siemens uses `LF` and accepts `;` for block-skip identifiers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a post processor. Why is it necessary in a CAM system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **post processor** is a software module that converts the machine-independent **Cutter Location (CL) data** generated by a CAM system into a specific, machine-dependent **Numerical Control (NC) part program** for a particular CNC controller.

It is necessary because:
1. **Different CNC controllers** (Fanuc, Siemens, Haas) use different code syntax, modal behaviors, and canned cycle formats — the CL data is generic and cannot be executed directly. **(1 mark)**
2. **Machine-specific kinematics**, work-offset schemes, and tool-change protocols must be embedded into the code. **(1 mark)**
3. It **bridges the gap** between the virtual CAM environment and the real shop-floor CNC, ensuring correct syntax, decimal format, and safe Z-retracts. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any six commonly used M-codes and state their functions in a CNC program.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| M-code | Function |
|---|---|
| M03 | Spindle ON — clockwise |
| M04 | Spindle ON — counter-clockwise |
| M05 | Spindle stop |
| M06 | Automatic tool change |
| M08 | Flood coolant ON |
| M09 | Coolant OFF |
| M30 | Program end and rewind |

*(Any six correctly listed with their function earn 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) Explain the role of preparatory G-codes in CNC programming with suitable examples. Discuss the difference between modal and non-modal codes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Preparatory G-codes, also called **preparatory functions**, instruct the CNC controller on the *type of motion* or *state* to be executed for the blocks that follow. The "G" stands for "General" (per ISO 6983).

**Examples and Roles:**

1. **G00 — Rapid Positioning:** Moves the tool at the maximum traverse rate to a new location. Used for non-cutting moves. Example: `G00 X100.0 Y50.0`.
2. **G01 — Linear Interpolation:** Moves the tool in a straight line at a programmed feed rate. Example: `G01 X80.0 Y40.0 F150`.
3. **G02 / G03 — Circular Interpolation:** Move the tool along a CW (G02) or CCW (G03) arc. Example: `G02 X60.0 Y20.0 I-10.0 J0.0 F100`.
4. **G17 / G18 / G19 — Plane Selection:** Define the active plane for circular interpolation and cutter compensation.
5. **G41 / G42 — Cutter Compensation:** Offset the programmed path to the left or right of the tool by the cutter-diameter register value.
6. **G81 — Drilling Canned Cycle:** Automatically executes the rapid approach, plunge, and retract sequence.

**Modal vs Non-Modal (4 marks of the 7):**

| Property | Modal | Non-Modal |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Active in all subsequent blocks until canceled or replaced | Active only in the block where it appears |
| Default state | Default-modal on most controllers | Default-non-modal |
| Example | G01, G02, G17, G54 | G04 (Dwell), G28 (Return to reference) |
| Benefit | Reduces program size | Used for one-shot actions |

**Conclusion:** Modal codes allow the post processor to **omit repetitive G-code words** in subsequent blocks — a key optimization that shrinks the NC file size by 40–60% in typical milling programs.

---

**(b) A CL data file for a milling operation contains the following moves: (i) rapid to (10, 10, 5), (ii) plunge to Z = -3 at feed 60, (iii) linear cut to (50, 10, -3) at feed 120, (iv) circular CW arc to (50, 40, -3) with center (40, 20, -3), (v) rapid retract to safe Z. Write the complete post-processed NC program for a Fanuc controller. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Computation of Arc Center Vector for move (iv):**

The Fanuc controller requires the arc center as an incremental vector from the start point of the arc.

$$
I = X_{center} - X_{start} = 40 - 50 = -10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
J = Y_{center} - Y_{start} = 20 - 10 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Post-Processed NC Program:**

```
%
O0002 (MILL_POCKET_01)
N10 T01 M06
N20 G90 G54 G17 G21 G94
N30 G43 H01 Z5.0
N40 M03 S1200
N50 M08
N60 G00 X10.0 Y10.0
N70 G01 Z-3.0 F60
N80 G01 X50.0 F120
N90 G02 X50.0 Y40.0 I-10.0 J10.0 F120
N100 G00 Z5.0
N110 M09
N120 M05
N130 G91 G28 Z0.0
N140 G28 X0.0 Y0.0
N150 M30
%
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Header and tool setup: 2 marks
- Modal state and feed-rate management: 1 mark
- Arc-center vector computation (I, J): 2 marks
- Complete shutdown sequence: 1 mark
- Overall NC code correctness: 1 mark

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) Explain the concept of canned cycles in CNC programming. With neat examples, describe the G81 (drilling) and G83 (peck drilling) cycles. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **canned cycle** is a single G-code that the CNC controller internally expands into a **predefined multi-block sequence** (rapid to R-plane, plunge at feed, retract, dwell, etc.). Canned cycles drastically reduce the size of NC programs for repetitive hole patterns.

**G81 — Drilling Cycle (Simple):**

```
G81 X10.0 Y20.0 Z-10.0 R2.0 F80
```

Internally, the controller executes:
1. Rapid to (X, Y) = (10, 20).
2. Rapid down to R-plane = Z + 2.0.
3. Feed down to Z = -10.0 at F80.
4. Rapid retract (controlled by G98 / G99).

**G83 — Peck Drilling Cycle (Deep Hole):**

```
G83 X10.0 Y20.0 Z-30.0 R2.0 Q5.0 F80
```

The Q5.0 specifies a **peck depth** of 5 mm. The controller performs:
1. Rapid to (X, Y).
2. Rapid down to R-plane.
3. Feed down 5 mm (first peck).
4. Rapid retract to R-plane to clear chips.
5. Rapid back down to previous depth - 0.5 mm (peck approach).
6. Repeat steps 3–5 until total Z-depth of -30.0 is reached.
7. Rapid retract to R-plane.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | G81 | G83 |
|---|---|---|
| Use | Shallow holes (depth < 3 × diameter) | Deep holes |
| Chip evacuation | None (continuous plunge) | Yes (retracts fully between pecks) |
| Q parameter | Not used | Q = peck increment |
| Risk | Chip packing, tool breakage | Longer cycle time, safe for deep holes |

---

**(b) Explain the role of cutter compensation commands (G40, G41, G42) in a post processor. How does the post processor handle lead-in and lead-out moves for compensation? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Cutter Compensation Commands (3 marks):**

- **G40** cancels cutter compensation. The programmed path is the exact center of the tool.
- **G41** offsets the tool to the **left** of the programmed path (as viewed from the tool's direction of travel).
- **G42** offsets the tool to the **right** of the programmed path.

The compensation distance is read from the **D-offset register**, which stores the cutter radius (or diameter depending on controller convention):

$$
\text{Offset perpendicular distance} = D \div 2 \text{ (or } D \text{ for diameter comp)}
$$

**Why Compensation is Needed:**

The CAM programmer writes the tool path along the **actual part geometry**. Without compensation, the programmer would have to manually offset every path by the tool radius. Compensation allows the same part program to be re-used when the cutter diameter changes.

**Lead-In and Lead-Out Handling by the Post Processor (4 marks):**

Modern CAM systems include **automatic lead-in / lead-out** strategies:
- **Tangent lead-in:** The post processor inserts a short linear or arc segment tangent to the part contour, allowing the controller to ramp up the compensation smoothly.
- **Normal lead-in:** The lead-in is normal (perpendicular) to the contour.
- **Lead-out:** Mirror image of lead-in, used before the compensation is canceled with G40.

A typical post-processed sequence for entering and exiting compensation:

```
N50 G01 X30.0 Y10.0 F100  (Lead-in move to engage point)
N60 G41 D01 X50.0 Y10.0   (Activate left comp, D01 = 10 mm dia)
N70 G01 X50.0 Y40.0       (Contour cut with comp active)
N80 G01 X30.0 Y40.0
N90 G40 G01 X10.0 Y40.0   (Cancel comp, lead-out)
```

**Key Post-Processor Settings:**
- Minimum lead length (avoids engagement on sharp corners).
- Compensation type (computer / control).
- Approach distance for ramp-on.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams (Post-Processor Questions):**
> 1. **Forgetting the I, J computation:** In circular interpolation, students often write the absolute arc center instead of the incremental vector from the start point. For Fanuc, the correct form is `I = X_center - X_start` and `J = Y_center - Y_start`. Marks are deducted heavily.
> 2. **Confusing G41 vs G42 direction:** Many students apply the rule "left of travel direction" inconsistently. Use the rule: stand at the start of the move, face the direction of travel, then G41 puts the tool on your **left hand**.
> 3. **Omitting modal suppression logic:** Writing `G01` in every block, even when it is already active. Examiners expect you to demonstrate that you understand **modal behavior**.
> 4. **Forgetting the safe-Z retract before tool change:** Tool change (M06) must occur with the tool at a safe Z height, away from the part, or the controller may alarm.
> 5. **Mixing G90 and G91 inconsistently:** Once absolute mode (G90) is set, all coordinates must be absolute. Mixing G91 (incremental) values into a G90 program is a fatal error.
> 6. **Not specifying a plane for circular interpolation:** G02 / G03 requires G17 / G18 / G19 to be set, or the controller will alarm with "no plane defined."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Post Processor** = software that converts machine-independent **CL data** into machine-specific **NC code**. It is the **final translator** in the CAD/CAM chain.
- **G-codes** are *preparatory* (define motion type and modal state); **M-codes** are *miscellaneous* (define spindle, coolant, ATC, program end).
- **Modal codes** persist until replaced; **non-modal codes** apply only to the current block. The post processor uses modal logic to **omit redundant G-words**, reducing program size by 40–60%.
- **Linear interpolation (G01)** requires end point + feed rate; **circular interpolation (G02 CW / G03 CCW)** requires end point + **incremental center vector I, J** (Fanuc) or **CR= radius** (Siemens).
- **Canned cycles (G81–G89)** internally expand into multi-block motion. **G81** is for shallow drilling; **G83** is for deep-hole peck drilling with a Q-peck parameter.
- **Cutter compensation (G40 / G41 / G42)** dynamically offsets the programmed path by the value in the D-offset register. The post processor inserts **lead-in and lead-out** moves to engage and disengage compensation smoothly.
- **Tool change (M06)** is always accompanied by a tool number `T__`, and must be preceded by a safe Z-retract.
- **Spindle commands** are M03 (CW), M04 (CCW), M05 (stop). They are **modal** and persist until replaced.
- **Coolant commands** are M07 (mist), M08 (flood), M09 (off).
- **The shutdown sequence** of a well-post-processed program is always: coolant OFF → spindle stop → return-to-reference → M30.
- **The post processor is a finite-state machine** that tracks modal state internally — this is the core computational model of all commercial post processors.
- **One CL file ≠ one NC program** — different controllers require different post processors, which is the **modular power** of CAM architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compilation Control Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT504 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compilation Control Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Computer Aided Manufacturing (CAM)** and **Numerical Control (NC) Part Programming**, **Compilation Control Commands** are a specialized set of preparatory and miscellaneous functions (G-codes and M-codes) that govern the *logical flow of program execution* during the translation (compilation) and runtime of an NC program. Unlike motion commands (G00, G01, G02, G03) that dictate *tool path geometry*, compilation control commands dictate *program structure*, enabling loops, conditional branching, subroutine nesting, optional block skipping, and macro variable manipulation.

In modern CNC controllers (Fanuc, Siemens Sinumerik, Heidenhain), these commands form the backbone of **macro programming** and **parametric programming**, allowing a single part program to machine a family of components by varying input parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** The compilation control commands are studied under "Advanced NC Part Programming" and form the foundation for **Computerized Numerical Control (CNC)** and **Adaptive Control Machining** systems. Mastery of these commands is mandatory for Part-B (14-mark) questions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are writing a **recipe book for a smart robotic chef** in your kitchen.

- **Motion commands (G01, G02)** = the actual actions: "chop onions", "stir the curry".
- **Compilation control commands** = the *decision-making instructions* in the recipe: "If the curry is too salty, add 1 spoon of sugar", "Repeat steps 4–7 for 10 minutes", "Skip step 12 if the guest is vegetarian".

Just as a smart recipe uses `IF`, `WHILE`, `GOTO`, and `SUBROUTINES` to handle variations, an NC part program uses **Compilation Control Commands** to:
1. Reuse subprograms (subroutines) → saving memory.
2. Branch based on tool wear, dimensional checks, or input parameters.
3. Loop over repetitive machining patterns (e.g., a grid of holes).
4. Enable/disable blocks via optional skip.

The "**compiler**" in this analogy is the CNC controller's interpreter that reads the text file (NC code) line-by-line and decides *which line to execute next*.

---

### 1.3 Categories of Compilation Control Commands

> [!NOTE]
> The three primary categories are:
>
> 1. **Sequence Control Commands** — Alter program sequence (Subroutines, Branching, Looping).
> 2. **Conditional Commands** — IF-THEN, WHILE-DO, logical comparators.
> 3. **Operator/Variable Commands** — Macro variables (#), arithmetic, and trigonometric functions.

The standard **Fanuc-compatible** controller vocabulary (most widely tested in KTU exams) is summarized below:

| Category | Standard Command | Purpose |
|----------|------------------|---------|
| Subroutine Call | `M98 P__ L__` | Calls subroutine O-number P, repeats L times |
| Subroutine End | `M99` | Returns from subroutine |
| Unconditional Branch | `GOTO n` | Jumps to sequence number n |
| Conditional Branch | `IF [condition] GOTO n` | Jumps to n if condition is TRUE |
| Loop Begin | `WHILE [condition] DO n` | Starts loop n while condition is TRUE |
| Loop End | `END n` | Ends loop n |
| Optional Skip | `/` (slash prefix) | Block skipped if Optional Stop Switch is ON |
| Macro Variable Assign | `#i = expression` | Assigns value to variable #i |
| Comment | `(...)` or `;` | Documenting block |

---

### 1.4 Macro Variables — The "Memory Cells" of the CNC

The CNC controller allocates a fixed bank of memory locations called **macro variables**, referenced by the `#` symbol. They are analogous to variables in the C language.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Bank Ranges (Fanuc Standard):**
> - **Local Variables:** `#1` to `#33` — Argument passing within subroutines.
> - **Common Variables:** `#100` to `#199` (single store), `#500` to `#999` (non-volatile/persistent).
> - **System Variables:** `#1000+` — Read tool offsets, modal info, position data, etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Subroutine Mechanism — M98 / M99

A **subroutine** is a reusable, self-contained block of NC code stored as a separate program (numbered Oxxxx). It is invoked by the main program using `M98`, and execution returns to the main program upon encountering `M99`.

**Syntax:**
```text
M98 P_xxxx_ L_yy      ; Calls subroutine Oxxxx, repeats 'yy' times
...
Oxxxx (subroutine body)
...
M99                    ; Returns control to the line after M98 call
M99 P_nnn              ; Returns AND branches to sequence 'nnn' (used with repeat)
```

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Main program issues `M98`.
2. Controller pushes the current **return address** onto an internal **call stack**.
3. Controller transfers execution to subroutine `Oxxxx`.
4. Subroutine executes until `M99` is encountered.
5. Controller pops the return address from the stack and resumes the main program.

> **Nesting Depth (Stack Limit):** Typically **4 to 10 levels** depending on the controller. Exceeding this causes an **alarm** (e.g., Fanuc Alarm #071).

---

### 2.2 Branching — GOTO and IF-GOTO

**Unconditional Branch:**
$$GOTO \; n$$

**Conditional Branch:**
$$IF \; [\#i \; \text{relational\_op} \; \#j] \; GOTO \; n$$

**The Six Relational Operators:**

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `EQ` | Equal to | `IF [#1 EQ 10] GOTO 50` |
| `NE` | Not Equal to | `IF [#1 NE 0] GOTO 20` |
| `GT` | Greater Than | `IF [#1 GT 100] GOTO 30` |
| `LT` | Less Than | `IF [#1 LT 50] GOTO 40` |
| `GE` | Greater or Equal | `IF [#1 GE 5] GOTO 60` |
| `LE` | Less or Equal | `IF [#1 LE 0] GOTO 70` |

---

### 2.3 Looping — WHILE / DO ... END

The WHILE loop is the primary iterative construct in macro programming.

**Syntax:**
```
WHILE [condition] DO n          ; n = 1, 2, or 3 (loop label)
    ... statement(s) ...
END n                           ; Marks end of loop n
```

**Rules:**
- Loop labels `n` can only be `1`, `2`, or `3` (max 3 nested loops).
- A loop label `n` may be reused only after `END n` is executed.
- Maximum nesting depth: **3 levels**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Compile this table for any KTU Numerical/Macro question.**

| Feature | Syntax / Formula | Units / Limits |
|---------|------------------|----------------|
| Absolute Value | $\vert x \vert = \sqrt{x^{2}}$ | Real number |
| Truncation (toward 0) | $\text{FIX}[\#i]$ | Integer |
| Round-up | $\text{FUP}[\#i]$ | Integer |
| Round (nearest) | $\text{ROUND}[\#i]$ | Integer |
| Modulo | $\#i \; \text{MOD} \; \#j$ | Remainder |
| Square Root | $\text{SQRT}[\#i]$ | $\geq 0$ |
| Sine (degrees) | $\text{SIN}[\#i]$ | $-1 \leq \sin \leq 1$ |
| Cosine (degrees) | $\text{COS}[\#i]$ | $-1 \leq \cos \leq 1$ |
| Arctangent | $\text{ATAN}[\#i]$ | Output in degrees, $-180$ to $+180$ |
| Absolute value function | $\text{ABS}[\#i]$ | $\geq 0$ |
| Subroutine call limit | $L_{max} = 9999$ | Repetitions |
| Nesting depth | $D_{nest} \leq 4$ (typical) | Levels |

**Sample Arithmetic Expression:**
$$\#10 = \#5 + \text{SIN}[30] \cdot \text{FIX}[\#2 \;/\; \#3]$$

**Conditional Relational Forms:**
$$\#i \; \text{EQ} \; \#j, \quad \#i \; \text{NE} \; \#j, \quad \#i \; \text{GT} \; \#j, \quad \#i \; \text{LT} \; \#j, \quad \#i \; \text{GE} \; \#j, \quad \#i \; \text{LE} \; \#j$$

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Family of Parts (Parametric Machining):** A single program machines different bolt-circle diameters by varying macro variable `#1`.
2. **Pallet Pool Automation:** IF the part-presence sensor returns `#1 = 1`, machine the part; ELSE, jump to pallet-change sequence.
3. **Adaptive Drilling:** WHILE drill depth `#5` < required depth `#6`, peck drill `#5 = #5 + 5`.
4. **Probing Cycles:** Conditional branches based on probing touch-off (touch = GOTO next operation; miss = alarm).
5. **Canned-Cycle Internals:** All built-in cycles (G81–G89) are internally implemented as subroutines using M98/M99.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1 — Macro Expression Evaluation

**Problem:** Evaluate the value stored in macro variable `#25` after execution of the following block:
```
#10 = 7
#11 = 3
#12 = 4
#25 = [#10 * #11 - SQRT[#12] + FIX[#10 / #12]]
```

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$ \#10 = 7, \quad \#11 = 3, \quad \#12 = 4 $$

$$\#10 \cdot \#11 = 7 \times 3 = 21$$

$$\text{SQRT}[\#12] = \sqrt{4} = 2$$

$$\frac{\#10}{\#12} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75$$

$$\text{FIX}\!\left[\frac{7}{4}\right] = \text{FIX}[1.75] = 1 \quad (\text{truncation toward } 0)$$

Substituting all components:

$$\#25 = 21 - 2 + 1$$

$$\#25 = 20$$

> **Final Answer:** `#25 = 20.000`

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — IF-GOTO Branching Trace

**Problem:** Trace the values of `#1` and `#2` through the following program:
```
#1 = 0
#2 = 0
N10  IF [#1 GE 5] GOTO 50
     #2 = #2 + #1
     #1 = #1 + 1
     GOTO 10
N50  (END)
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Iteration | `#1` (start) | `#1 GE 5?` | `#2 = #2 + #1` | `#1 = #1 + 1` (end) |
|-----------|--------------|------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 0 | FALSE | `#2 = 0+0 = 0` | `#1 = 1` |
| 2 | 1 | FALSE | `#2 = 0+1 = 1` | `#1 = 2` |
| 3 | 2 | FALSE | `#2 = 1+2 = 3` | `#1 = 3` |
| 4 | 3 | FALSE | `#2 = 3+3 = 6` | `#1 = 4` |
| 5 | 4 | FALSE | `#2 = 6+4 = 10` | `#1 = 5` |
| 6 | 5 | TRUE → GOTO 50 | (skipped) | (skipped) |

> **Final Answer:** `#1 = 5`, `#2 = 10`. The loop iterated **5 times**.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — WHILE-DO Loop Execution

**Problem:** Compute `#30` after the following block:
```
#30 = 1
#31 = 1
WHILE [#31 LE 5] DO 1
     #30 = #30 * #31
     #31 = #31 + 1
END 1
```

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\#30_{\text{initial}} = 1, \quad \#31_{\text{initial}} = 1$$

$$\text{Check: } \#31 = 1 \leq 5 \; \Rightarrow \; \text{TRUE} \Rightarrow \text{Enter loop}$$

**Iteration 1:** `#30 = 1 × 1 = 1`, `#31 = 2`

**Iteration 2:** `#30 = 1 × 2 = 2`, `#31 = 3`

**Iteration 3:** `#30 = 2 × 3 = 6`, `#31 = 4`

**Iteration 4:** `#30 = 6 × 4 = 24`, `#31 = 5`

**Iteration 5:** `#30 = 24 × 5 = 120`, `#31 = 6`

$$\text{Check: } \#31 = 6 \leq 5 \; \Rightarrow \; \text{FALSE} \; \Rightarrow \; \text{Exit loop}$$

> **Final Answer:** `#30 = 120`. The program computed **5!** (factorial).

---

### 3.4 Example 4 — Subroutine with Repeat Counter

**Problem:** Write a main program and subroutine that mills 4 equally spaced slots along a 200 mm length, calling subroutine `O2000` four times. Each slot is 10 mm long, machined at feed rate F100.

**Main Program:**
```
O0001 (MAIN)
G90 G54 G00 X0 Y0
M98 P2000 L4        ; Call O2000 four times
M30                  ; End main
```

**Subroutine:**
```
O2000 (SLOT SUB)
G91 G01 Y-50 F100    ; Move to slot start (incremental)
G01 X10              ; Mill 10 mm slot
G00 X-10             ; Retract
G90                  ; Back to absolute
M99                  ; Return to main
```

**Trace Logic:**
1. Main program calls `O2000` with repeat count `L = 4`.
2. The controller's internal counter decrements `L` each time `M99` is hit.
3. When `L` reaches 0, control returns to the line after the original `M98`.
4. Each call shifts the slot 50 mm along Y (incremental moves do not require re-entry of coordinates).

---

### 3.5 Example 5 — Parametric Bolt-Circle Drilling

**Problem:** A bolt-circle of **N = 8 holes** of radius **R = 50 mm** must be drilled. Write a parametric macro program.

**Solution:**
```
O0700 (BOLT CIRCLE)
#1 = 50              ; Radius R
#2 = 8               ; Number of holes N
#3 = 0               ; Angle counter θ
#4 = 360 / #2        ; Angular step = 45°

WHILE [#2 GT 0] DO 1
     #5 = #1 * COS[#3]      ; X-coordinate
     #6 = #1 * SIN[#3]      ; Y-coordinate
     G90 G00 X#5 Y#6        ; Move to hole position
     G81 R2 Z-10 F80        ; Drill cycle
     #3 = #3 + #4           ; Increment angle
     #2 = #2 - 1            ; Decrement counter
END 1

G80                      ; Cancel canned cycle
M30
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The cosine and sine computations automatically position each hole. The same program works for **any** N and R — just change `#1` and `#2`.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Command Comparison

| Feature | GOTO | IF-GOTO | WHILE-DO | M98/M99 |
|---------|------|---------|----------|---------|
| Type | Unconditional branch | Conditional branch | Iterative loop | Subroutine call |
| Stack Required | No | No | Yes (internal) | Yes (call stack) |
| Nesting Allowed | No limit (single jumps) | No limit | Max 3 nested | Max 4 nested |
| Used for | Single jump | Decision making | Repetition | Code reuse |
| KTU Frequency | High | Very High | Very High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Compilation Control

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *logical architecture* of how a CNC controller interprets compilation control commands. Each node represents a distinct functional block in the parsing and execution pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NC Source File .MIN] --> B[Lexer Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Parser Block Builder]
    C --> D{Block Type Classifier}

    D -->|Motion Block| E[Motion Interpreter G00 G01 G02 G03]
    D -->|Control Block| F[Compilation Control Module]

    F --> F1[M98 Subroutine Call]
    F --> F2[M99 Subroutine Return]
    F --> F3[IF GOTO Conditional Branch]
    F --> F4[WHILE DO Iterative Loop]
    F --> F5[GOTO Unconditional Branch]
    F --> F6[Optional Block Skip Slash]

    F1 --> G[Call Stack Push Return Address]
    F2 --> G1[Call Stack Pop Return Address]
    F3 --> H[Relational Evaluator EQ NE GT LT GE LE]
    F4 --> I[Loop Counter Manager Labels 1 2 3]

    H --> J[Boolean Decision TRUE FALSE]
    J --> K{Jump Decision}
    K -->|TRUE| L[Update Program Counter to Target Sequence]
    K -->|FALSE| M[Continue Sequential Execution]

    E --> N[Interpolator Linear Circular]
    G --> N
    G1 --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    I --> N

    N --> O[Servo Drive Signals X Y Z Spindle]
    O --> P[Machine Tool Motion]
```

**Diagram Interpretation (Block-by-Block):**
- **Node A → B:** Raw NC code (`.MIN`/`.TXT`) is read line-by-line by the lexer.
- **Node C → D:** Each block is classified. Motion blocks bypass the compilation control module entirely.
- **Node F:** The Compilation Control Module is the heart of macro interpretation. It houses **six** sub-functions (F1–F6).
- **Node G / G1:** The call stack enables subroutine nesting (push on entry, pop on exit).
- **Node J → K → L / M:** Conditional branches resolve to either a jump or sequential flow.
- **Node I:** The loop counter manager tracks WHILE-DO loops (maximum 3 nested labels).
- **Node N:** All paths converge at the interpolator, which generates axis motion commands.

---

### 4.2 Sequence Flow Diagram — Subroutine Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Program O0001
    participant Stack as Call Stack
    participant Sub as Subroutine O2000
    participant Mac as Macro Variable Bank

    Main->>Mac: Initialize #1 = 100 #2 = 4
    Main->>Stack: PUSH return address = N50
    Main->>Sub: M98 P2000 L4
    activate Sub
    Sub->>Mac: Read #1 #2 for computation
    Sub->>Sub: Execute motion and logic
    Sub->>Stack: M99 triggers POP
    deactivate Sub
    Stack-->>Main: Return to N50
    Main->>Main: Continue sequential execution
    Main->>Mac: Cleanup local variables
```

---
