### 4.3 Decision Tree — Conditional Branch Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([IF Condition Encountered]) --> Eval[Evaluate Relational Expression]
    Eval --> Check{Result}

    Check -->|TRUE| Jump[GOTO target sequence n]
    Check -->|FALSE| Skip[Continue to next sequential block]

    Jump --> Target[Resume at block Nn]
    Skip --> Next[Execute next block Nn+1]

    Target --> End([Merge Execution Path])
    Next --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Drawing Adaptation:** Since "compilation control" is a logical/software construct (not a physical mechanical component), the **functional architecture flow** above substitutes for a traditional mechanical schematic. This is the standard approach in KTU 2024 syllabus for CAM topics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define the term **subroutine** in the context of NC part programming. State the M-code used to call a subroutine and the one used to return from it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **subroutine** is a self-contained, reusable NC program block stored under a separate program number (Oxxxx) that can be invoked from a main program or other subroutines. This avoids repetition, saves memory, and simplifies program maintenance.
- **Call Command:** `M98 Pxxxx Lyy` — calls subroutine `Oxxxx`, repeats `yy` times.
- **Return Command:** `M99` — terminates subroutine execution and returns control to the line after the calling `M98` block.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark M98 + 1 mark M99)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **conditional branching** (`IF … GOTO`) and **unconditional branching** (`GOTO`) in NC macro programming. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Unconditional GOTO | Conditional IF-GOTO |
|--------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Decision Logic | None — always jumps | Checks a relational expression first |
| Syntax | `GOTO n` | `IF [#1 GT 10] GOTO n` |
| Use Case | Skip sections, fixed jumps | Sensor-driven decisions, adaptive control |

**Example 1 (Unconditional):** `GOTO 80` — always jumps to sequence N80.
**Example 2 (Conditional):** `IF [#2 EQ 1] GOTO 30` — jumps to N30 only if variable #2 equals 1.

*(1 mark difference table + 2 marks examples)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **macro variable types** used in Fanuc-compatible CNC controllers with their number ranges. Discuss the difference between *local*, *common*, and *system* variables with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a parametric NC macro program to drill **12 holes** on a **bolt-circle of diameter 100 mm**. Use the WHILE-DO loop construct. Assume the work coordinate system is G54, drill depth 10 mm, and use G81 canned cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Variable Classification Table:**

| Type | Range | Volatility | Use |
|------|-------|------------|-----|
| Local Variables | `#1` to `#33` | Reset on power off | Argument passing to subroutines |
| Common Variables (Single Store) | `#100` to `#199` | Cleared on M30 or Reset | Intermediate global storage |
| Common Variables (Non-Volatile) | `#500` to `#999` | Retained even after power off | Persistent part count, tool life |
| System Variables | `#1000` to `#9999` | Read-only / System | Modal info, position, offsets |

**Examples:**

- **Local:** `#1 = 50` — used to pass a feed-rate argument to a subroutine.
- **Common (single):** `#100 = #100 + 1` — part counter (resets daily).
- **Common (non-volatile):** `#500 = 0` — total parts machined this month.
- **System:** `#5021` — current X-axis machine coordinate (read-only).
- **System:** `#4006` — currently active G-code group 1 modal.

**Conclusion:** Variable selection depends on *scope* (where it is used) and *persistence* (how long it survives power cycles). Local variables offer **call-stack isolation**, while non-volatile common variables enable **factory-level statistics tracking**.

*(3 marks table + 2 marks examples + 2 marks scope/persistence explanation)*

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:** N = 12 holes, Bolt-circle diameter D = 100 mm → radius R = 50 mm.

**NC Macro Program:**
```
O0900 (12-HOLE BOLT CIRCLE DRILLING)
#1 = 50                   ; Radius R
#2 = 12                   ; Number of holes N
#3 = 0                    ; Angle θ (initial)
#4 = 360 / #2             ; Angular step (Δθ = 30°)
G90 G54 G00 X0 Y0 Z50     ; Initial position
G98 G81 R2 Z-10 F80       ; Drill cycle, retract to R-plane

WHILE [#2 GT 0] DO 1
     #5 = #1 * COS[#3]    ; X-coordinate
     #6 = #1 * SIN[#3]    ; Y-coordinate
     G90 X#5 Y#6          ; Rapid to hole
     #3 = #3 + #4         ; Increment angle
     #2 = #2 - 1          ; Decrement counter
END 1

G80                       ; Cancel canned cycle
G00 Z100 M30              ; Retract and end program
```

**Sample Hole Positions (First 3):**

| Hole # | θ (degrees) | X = R cos θ | Y = R sin θ |
|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | 0 | 50.000 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 30 | 43.301 | 25.000 |
| 3 | 60 | 25.000 | 43.301 |

**Explanation:** The WHILE loop iterates 12 times, each time computing the (X, Y) coordinates via parametric equations of a circle:

$$
X_i = R \cdot \cos(\theta_i), \quad Y_i = R \cdot \sin(\theta_i), \quad \theta_{i+1} = \theta_i + \frac{360^\circ}{N}
$$

*(3 marks program structure + 2 marks parametric equations + 2 marks sample positions table)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of compilation control commands** in a CNC controller. Describe the role of the **call stack**, **relational evaluator**, and **loop counter manager**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A main program calls subroutine `O3000` to mill **6 pockets** in a row, each pocket is **20 mm × 20 mm**, separated by **30 mm** along the X-axis. Write the **main program** and **subroutine** using `M98` and `M99`. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Block Diagram (textual reproduction):**

```
[NC Source File] → [Lexer] → [Parser] → [Block Classifier]
                                              ↓
                          ┌───────────────────┴───────────────────┐
                          ↓                                       ↓
               [Motion Interpreter]                  [Compilation Control Module]
                          ↓                                       ↓
                                                       ┌───────┴────────┐
                                                       ↓                ↓
                                                  [Call Stack]    [Loop Counter]
                                                       ↓                ↓
                                                 [Return Addr]   [Labels 1,2,3]
                                                       ↓                ↓
                                              [M98 Push / M99 Pop] [DO…END Tracking]
                                                       └───────┬────────┘
                                                               ↓
                                                       [Interpolator]
                                                               ↓
                                                       [Servo Drives]
```

**Three Critical Subsystems:**

1. **Call Stack** — A Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) memory buffer that stores **return addresses** whenever a subroutine (`M98`) is invoked. When `M99` is executed, the topmost return address is popped and loaded into the program counter, resuming the calling program. Depth limit: typically 4 to 10 levels.

2. **Relational Evaluator** — The hardware/firmware module that resolves expressions like `[#1 GT #2]` into a Boolean value (TRUE/FALSE). It implements the six relational operators: `EQ, NE, GT, LT, GE, LE`. Output drives the `GOTO` decision.

3. **Loop Counter Manager** — Tracks active `WHILE … DO n` blocks. Only three labels (`1, 2, 3`) are allowed, meaning maximum 3 nested loops. Each label maintains a *condition-flag* that re-evaluates the loop predicate on each iteration.

*(3 marks block diagram + 4 marks subsystem explanation)*

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Given:** 6 pockets, 20 mm × 20 mm, pitch = 50 mm center-to-center, total travel = 6 × 50 = 300 mm.

**Main Program:**
```
O0001 (MAIN — 6 POCKET ROW)
G90 G54 G00 X0 Y0 Z50
M98 P3000 L6          ; Call pocket subroutine 6 times
G00 Z100 M30          ; Retract and end
```

**Subroutine:**
```
O3000 (POCKET 20x20)
G91 G01 X30 F200       ; Move 30 mm to pocket corner
G90 X20 Y20            ; Rapid to first corner
G01 Z-5 F80            ; Plunge
G01 X40                ; Cut along X
G01 Y40                ; Cut along Y
G01 X20                ; Return along X
G01 Y20                ; Return along Y
G00 Z5                 ; Retract
M99                    ; Return to main
```

**Trace Logic:**
1. The `L6` argument tells the controller to repeat the subroutine 6 times automatically (no explicit counter variable needed).
2. After each pocket, the subroutine's incremental `X30` move positions the start of the next pocket.
3. After the 6th call, the controller automatically resumes `M30` (program end).

**Pocket Center Positions:**

$$
X_i = X_{i-1} + 50 \; \text{mm}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, 6
$$

Pocket centers: `25, 75, 125, 175, 225, 275` mm.

*(3 marks main program + 3 marks subroutine + 1 mark trace logic)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Compiled from KTU Valuation Reports 2022–2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing M98 with M99:** Many students write `M98` to end a subroutine. **Correct:** `M98` calls; `M99` returns. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Wrong Operator:** Writing `IF [#1 > 10]` instead of `IF [#1 GT 10]`. CNC uses English operators (`GT, LT, EQ`), not mathematical symbols. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting Square Brackets:** Expressions must be enclosed in `[ ]`, e.g., `IF [#1 GT 10] GOTO 50`. Without brackets, syntax error. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Loop Label Limit:** Using `DO 4` or `DO 5`. Only labels `1, 2, 3` are valid. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Missing FIX / FUP Distinction:** `FIX[1.5] = 1`, `FUP[1.5] = 2`. Confusing these gives wrong integer values. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Modulo Sign Error:** `#7 MOD #8` in Fanuc returns a **positive** remainder only. Negative results must be handled manually.
> 7. **Degrees vs. Radians:** `SIN[30]` in Fanuc expects **degrees**, not radians. Marks lost if assumed radians.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Compile before exam):**
>
> - **M98 Pxxxx Lyy** = call subroutine `Oxxxx` `yy` times.
> - **M99** = return from subroutine.
> - **GOTO n** = unconditional jump to sequence Nn.
> - **IF [#i OP #j] GOTO n** = conditional jump. Operators: `EQ, NE, GT, LT, GE, LE`.
> - **WHILE [condition] DO n** = start loop. `n ∈ {1, 2, 3}`. Max nesting = 3.
> - **END n** = terminate loop `n`.
> - **/ (slash prefix)** = optional block skip.
> - **Local variables:** `#1` to `#33` (volatile, argument-passing).
> - **Common variables:** `#100–#199` (single store), `#500–#999` (non-volatile).
> - **System variables:** `#1000+` (modal, position, offsets — mostly read-only).
> - **Functions to memorize:** `FIX, FUP, ROUND, ABS, SQRT, SIN, COS, TAN, ATAN, MOD`.
> - **Default angle unit:** degrees (not radians).
> - **Nesting depth:** Subroutines ≤ 4; Loops ≤ 3.
> - **Square brackets `[ ]` are mandatory** around every expression.
> - **Call Stack:** LIFO; `M98` pushes; `M99` pops.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Module 2 Notes — Compilation Control Commands.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Exercise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT504 - COMPUTER AIDER DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Programming Exercise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Computer Aided Design and Manufacturing (CAD/CAM)**, Module 2 deals with **Numerical Control (NC) and Computer Numerical Control (CNC) Part Programming**. A *Programming Exercise* refers to the structured, written activity of creating an alphanumeric code sequence — using standardized preparatory codes (**G-codes**), miscellaneous codes (**M-codes**), and coordinate data — that instructs a CNC machine tool to perform a specific sequence of machining operations on a workpiece to produce a finished component.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBAUT504), the *Programming Exercise* module mandates that students gain hands-on competency in:

- **Manual part programming** (also called *hand programming* or *word-address format programming*).
- Writing programs in the **ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267** standard format.
- Developing logic for **APT (Automatically Programmed Tools) language** statements.
- Computing **cutter path coordinates**, **tool offsets**, and **canned cycle parameters**.
- Generating **tool path** for turning, milling, drilling, and profile cutting operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition (Verbatim Style):**  
> *Part Programming is the process of preparing a detailed sequence of machining instructions, in a form acceptable to the Numerical Control system, based on the engineering drawing, tooling selection, and process plan of the component.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a **robot chef** in your kitchen how to make a square roti (flatbread) from a ball of dough:

1. You cannot just hand the dough to the robot and say "make a roti." You must **break down** every tiny action — pick up dough, place on board, roll forward 10 cm, lift, rotate 90°, roll forward 10 cm, rotate 90°, roll forward 10 cm, rotate 90°, roll forward 10 cm, lift off.
2. Each instruction is written in a **rigid, unambiguous language** the robot understands.
3. The robot **cannot infer** anything — if you forget a step, the roti is ruined.

A **CNC Programming Exercise** is exactly the same idea, except:
- The "robot chef" is a **CNC milling or turning machine**.
- The "dough" is the **raw workpiece (blank)**.
- The "rolling pin" is the **cutting tool (end mill, drill, lathe tool)**.
- The "language" is the **NC part program** consisting of **G-codes, M-codes, and coordinate words**.
- The "recipe" is the **engineering drawing + process sheet**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** A CNC program is a *deterministic recipe* — every line (called a *block*) tells the machine exactly one thing to do, and the machine executes them in strict sequential order with **zero interpretation or guesswork**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standards are mandatory references for any KTU programming exercise:

| Standard / Constant | Value / Description | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **G-code standard** | **ISO 6983** (also **EIA RS-267**) | Universal preparatory function language |
| **Coordinate axes** | **X, Y, Z** (linear) + **A, B, C** (rotary) | Standard 3-axis Cartesian system |
| **Feed unit** | **mm/min** (or **mm/rev** for turning) | Default in metric ISO programs |
| **Spindle speed unit** | **rev/min (RPM)** | Designated by **S-word** |
| **Block format** | Word-address format (e.g., `N10 G01 X25.0 Y30.0 F150`) | Modern ISO standard |
| **Decimal point programming** | **Implicit (calculator-style)** — `X250` = `X25.0` | Default in most CNC controllers |
| **Modal vs Non-modal codes** | Modal = stay active; Non-modal = one block only | Affects program length |
| **Origin of part** | Designated by **G54–G59** (work coordinate system) | Critical for setup |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** Always explicitly state the **G-code standard** (ISO 6983) and the **work coordinate system** (e.g., G54) at the start of any programming exercise answer to earn the first easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Workflow of a Programming Exercise

Every KTU programming exercise problem follows a **rigorous five-stage methodology**. Skipping any stage will result in lost marks.

#### **Stage 1 — Engineering Drawing Interpretation**
- Identify the **geometry** of the final part (rectangles, slots, holes, pockets, profiles, tapers).
- Read all **dimensions and tolerances**.
- Identify the **datum surfaces** and establish the **origin (0,0,0)** location on the drawing.
- Identify **surface finish requirements** (which dictates feed and speed).

#### **Stage 2 — Process Planning**
- Select the **machine tool** (vertical milling center, lathe, etc.).
- Select the **cutting tools** (end mill Ø10 mm HSS, drill Ø6 mm, etc.).
- Decide the **work-holding device** (vise, chuck, fixture).
- Determine the **sequence of operations** (e.g., Face → Drill → Pocket → Profile).
- Calculate **cutting parameters**:
  - Spindle speed $N$ in RPM
  - Feed rate $F$ in mm/min
  - Depth of cut $d$ in mm

#### **Stage 3 — Tool Path Planning**
- Plot the **cutter path** on the engineering drawing (or in your head).
- Decide **approach** and **retract** moves.
- Plan **entry/exit strategies** for tools (ramp, plunge, helical).
- Mark all **interpolation moves** (linear G01, circular G02/G03).

#### **Stage 4 — Coordinate Calculation**
- Compute every **target coordinate** (X, Y, Z) in the part program.
- For arcs/circles: compute **arc start point, end point, center (I, J, K), and radius R**.
- Apply **cutter radius compensation** if needed (G41/G42).
- Apply **tool length compensation** (G43, H-offset).

#### **Stage 5 — Code Writing (Part Program Generation)**
- Write the program block-by-block using **word-address format**.
- Add **safety blocks**, **tool changes**, and **spindle start/stop** sequences.
- End with **M30** (program stop and rewind).

---

### 2.2 The Word-Address Format (Block Format)

Each **block** (one line of program) consists of **words** in the form:

$$\text{Block Number} \; \text{Preparatory Function} \; \text{Coordinate Word} \; \text{Feed} \; \text{Spindle} \; \text{Misc.}$$

**General syntax:**
$$\texttt{N}\text{(block)} \; \; \texttt{G}\text{(prep)} \; \; \texttt{X\_} \; \texttt{Y\_} \; \texttt{Z\_} \; \; \texttt{F}\text{(feed)} \; \; \texttt{S}\text{(speed)} \; \; \texttt{T}\text{(tool)} \; \; \texttt{M}\text{(misc)}$$

**Example block:**
$$\texttt{N40 \; G01 \; X30.0 \; Y20.0 \; F150}$$

This means: *"At block 40, perform linear interpolation to coordinate (30, 20) at a feed rate of 150 mm/min."*

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **mandatory** for any KTU programming exercise numerical.

#### **(a) Spindle Speed (N) Calculation**
$$\boxed{N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}}$$

Where:
- $N$ = Spindle speed in **rev/min (RPM)**
- $V_c$ = Cutting speed in **m/min**
- $D$ = Tool diameter in **mm**

#### **(b) Feed Rate (F) Calculation**
$$\boxed{F = f_z \cdot z \cdot N}$$

Where:
- $F$ = Feed rate in **mm/min**
- $f_z$ = Feed per tooth in **mm/tooth**
- $z$ = Number of teeth (flutes) on the tool
- $N$ = Spindle speed in **rev/min**

#### **(c) Material Removal Rate (MRR)**
$$\boxed{MRR = d \cdot w \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $d$ = Depth of cut in **mm**
- $w$ = Width of cut in **mm**
- $F$ = Feed rate in **mm/min**

#### **(d) Cutter Path Compensation (Cutter Radius Offset)**
$$\boxed{X_{offset} = X_{program} \pm r}$$

Where:
- $r$ = Cutter radius
- $+$ sign for **G42** (right-side compensation, conventional milling)
- $-$ sign for **G41** (left-side compensation, climb milling)

#### **(e) Arc Center Offset Coordinates (I, J, K)**
$$\boxed{I = X_{center} - X_{start}, \quad J = Y_{center} - Y_{start}}$$

Where $I$ is the incremental distance in X from arc start to arc center, and $J$ is the incremental distance in Y.

#### **(f) Number of Flutes Determination (Standard End Mills)**
$$\boxed{\text{2-flute} \to \text{aluminum/plastics}, \; \text{4-flute} \to \text{steel}, \; \text{6-flute} \to \text{hardened steel}}$$

#### **(g) Programming Length Formulas**
$$\boxed{L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{(x_{i+1}-x_i)^2 + (y_{i+1}-y_i)^2}}$$

This gives the **total linear tool travel** for a multi-segment path.

---

### 2.4 High-Frequency G-Codes (Must Memorize for KTU)

| G-Code | Function | Type | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **G00** | Rapid traverse (point-to-point) | Modal | Non-cutting moves |
| **G01** | Linear interpolation (cutting) | Modal | Straight line cutting |
| **G02** | Circular interpolation CW | Modal | Clockwise arcs |
| **G03** | Circular interpolation CCW | Modal | Counter-clockwise arcs |
| **G17 / G18 / G19** | Plane selection (XY / XZ / YZ) | Modal | Defines arc plane |
| **G20 / G21** | Inch / Metric programming | Modal | Units selection |
| **G40 / G41 / G42** | Cutter compensation OFF / LEFT / RIGHT | Modal | Tool offset |
| **G43** | Tool length compensation (+) | Modal | Z-axis offset |
| **G54–G59** | Work coordinate system selection | Modal | Datum shift |
| **G80–G89** | Canned cycles (drilling, tapping, boring) | Modal | Hole-making |
| **G90 / G91** | Absolute / Incremental programming | Modal | Coordinate mode |
| **G94 / G95** | Feed/min or Feed/rev | Modal | Feed mode |

---

### 2.5 High-Frequency M-Codes (Must Memorize for KTU)

| M-Code | Function |
|---|---|
| **M00** | Program stop (operator intervention) |
| **M01** | Optional stop |
| **M03** | Spindle ON clockwise |
| **M04** | Spindle ON counter-clockwise |
| **M05** | Spindle stop |
| **M06** | Automatic tool change (ATC) |
| **M08** | Coolant ON (flood) |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF |
| **M30** | Program end and rewind |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

NC part programming is the **backbone of modern manufacturing**:

- **Aerospace industry** uses 5-axis CNC programs for turbine blades and structural components.
- **Automotive industry** mass-produces engine blocks, cylinder heads, and gearbox housings via CNC programs.
- **Medical implant manufacturing** uses micro-CNC programs for orthopedic and dental parts.
- **Mold and die industry** uses complex APT-style programs for 3D cavity machining.
- **Rapid prototyping and 3D printing** services depend on G-code interpretation.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world insight:** Modern CNC controllers (e.g., **Fanuc, Siemens Sinumerik, Heidenhain, Mazak Mazatrol**) are all based on ISO 6983 G/M-code fundamentals. Mastering the manual programming in KTU exercises directly translates to industrial competency.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

### 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE WORKED EXAMPLE — KTU Standard Question

> **Problem Statement:**  
> Write a manual part program (word-address format) for milling the **rectangular pocket** shown below on a CNC vertical machining center using a **Ø10 mm HSS end mill (4-flute)**. The pocket dimensions are **60 mm × 40 mm**, depth is **5 mm**, machined in **mild steel**. The corner of the pocket is at coordinates **(20, 20)** with the top-left corner of the workpiece as origin. Use climb milling, climb direction = **G02**, and canned cycle is **NOT** required. Apply **G42 cutter compensation**.

**Given data:**
- Tool diameter $D = 10$ mm ⇒ cutter radius $r = 5$ mm
- Material: Mild steel
- Recommended cutting speed $V_c = 25$ m/min
- Feed per tooth $f_z = 0.04$ mm/tooth
- Number of flutes $z = 4$
- Depth of cut $d = 5$ mm

---

### 3.2 STEP-BY-STEP CALCULATION (Stage 4 of workflow)

#### **Step 1 — Calculate Spindle Speed (N)**

Using the formula:
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

Substitute the values:
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 10}$$

$$N = \frac{25000}{31.416}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 796 \; \text{RPM}}$$

**Explanation:** We multiply $V_c$ by 1000 to convert m/min → mm/min, then divide by the circumference of the tool ($\pi D$) to get revolutions per minute.

---

#### **Step 2 — Calculate Feed Rate (F)**

Using the formula:
$$F = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$$

Substitute the values:
$$F = 0.04 \cdot 4 \cdot 796$$

$$F = 0.16 \cdot 796$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 127 \; \text{mm/min}}$$

**Explanation:** Each of the 4 flutes cuts 0.04 mm per revolution. With 796 rev/min, total chip-load per minute is $0.04 \times 4 \times 796$.

---

#### **Step 3 — Compute Pocket Corner Coordinates (Original Geometry)**

Pocket corner points (without cutter compensation):
- **P1** (entry start) = (20, 20)
- **P2** = (80, 20)
- **P3** = (80, 60)
- **P4** = (20, 60)

---

#### **Step 4 — Apply G42 Cutter Compensation (Right-Side, Conventional Milling)**

With **G42 (right-side compensation)**, the tool path is offset **outward** by the cutter radius $r = 5$ mm.

Compensation equations:
$$X_{offset} = X_{program} + r$$
$$Y_{offset} = Y_{program} + r$$

Computing each compensated point:

- **P1' (approach)** = (15, 15)
- **P2'** = (85, 15)
- **P3'** = (85, 65)
- **P4'** = (15, 65)

**Detailed calculation for P1':**
$$X_{P1'} = 20 + 5 = 25 \;\; \text{(for right-edge in conventional milling on a left-side approach)}$$

*(Note: Conventionally, depending on entry direction, the offset is +r on the **outside** perimeter when milling externally; for internal pocket milling with G42, the path is shifted into the workpiece by +r on the right side. The student must identify the entry direction from the drawing.)*

For our case, assuming entry from the **lower-left** corner along the **X+ direction** with G42 right-side compensation:

- **Approach point** = (15, 15)
- **P1** (start of cut) = (20, 20)
- **P2** = (80, 20)
- **P3** = (80, 60)
- **P4** = (20, 60)
- **Retract point** = (15, 65)

---

#### **Step 5 — Calculate Arc Center Offsets for Corner Radii**

If we round the pocket corners with **R5 mm fillets** (optional, for advanced programming), the arc center offset coordinates are computed using:

$$I = X_{center} - X_{start}$$
$$J = Y_{center} - Y_{start}$$

For corner at P2 with fillet radius 5 mm, arc center is at (75, 25), and arc start is at (75, 20):
$$I = 75 - 75 = 0$$
$$J = 25 - 20 = 5$$

---

### 3.3 COMPLETE PART PROGRAM (Word-Address Format)

Below is the **full executable part program**. Each block is annotated with its engineering meaning.

```
%
O1001 (POCKET MILLING PROGRAM - 60x40x5 MM POCKET)  (Program number)
N05 G21 G90 G94 G17 G54                          (Metric, Absolute, Feed/min, XY plane, Work Coord)
N10 G00 Z25.0 M08                                (Rapid to safe Z=25, coolant ON)
N15 T01 M06                                      (Tool 1: Ø10 end mill, Tool Change)
N20 G00 X15.0 Y15.0                              (Rapid to approach point above pocket)
N25 G00 Z5.0                                     (Rapid to Z=5 above workpiece)
N30 G01 Z-5.0 F50                                (Plunge into pocket at controlled feed F=50)
N35 G42 D01 X20.0 Y20.0 F127                     (Engage cutter compensation, line to P1)
N40 G01 X80.0 Y20.0                              (Linear cut to P2 along +X)
N45 G01 X80.0 Y60.0                              (Linear cut to P3 along +Y)
N50 G01 X20.0 Y60.0                              (Linear cut to P4 along -X)
N55 G01 X20.0 Y20.0                              (Linear cut back to P1 along -Y)
N60 G40 X15.0 Y15.0                              (Cancel cutter compensation, retract)
N65 G00 Z25.0                                    (Rapid retract to safe Z)
N70 M09                                          (Coolant OFF)
N75 G00 X0 Y0                                    (Rapid to home position)
N80 M05                                          (Spindle stop)
N85 M30                                          (Program end and rewind)
%
```

---

### 3.4 EXHAUSTIVE APT-LANGUAGE VERSION (For KTU Theory Questions)

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the *Programming Exercise* module also includes the **APT (Automatically Programmed Tools)** symbolic language. The same pocket example in APT syntax:

```
PARTNO POCKET_60x40x5
MACHINE VMC,3AXIS
CLPRNT
CUTTER/ 10.0          (Tool diameter declaration)
INTOL/ 0.05           (Inner tolerance)
OUTTOL/ 0.05          (Outer tolerance)
FROM/ 0, 0, 25
GO/ 1                  (Call tool 1)
RAPID
GOTO/ 20, 20, 5
SPINDL/ 796, CLW
FEDRAT/ 127
CUTTER/ 10.0
POCKET
  TLLFT, 5             (Tolerance left = cutter radius)
  BOTLFT, 5            (Bottom left corner of pocket)
  TOPRGT, 60, 40       (Top right corner relative)
  DEPTH/ 5
  ON/ 20, 20
FINI
```

**Explanation of APT statements:**
- `PARTNO` — assigns a part name.
- `MACHINE` — declares machine type and number of axes.
- `CUTTER/` — defines tool diameter (the APT processor calculates paths).
- `FROM/` — defines the starting point of the tool.
- `GOTO/` — directs the tool to a coordinate.
- `SPINDL/` — sets spindle speed and direction (CLW = clockwise).
- `FEDRAT/` — sets feed rate.
- `POCKET` — a built-in APT verb that automatically generates the pocket milling cycle.
- `TLLFT, BOTLFT` — left and bottom tolerances for cutter radius compensation.
- `FINI` — ends the program.

---

### 3.5 Tool Path Length Verification (Numerical Check)

To verify the program, compute the **total linear tool travel** using:

$$L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{(x_{i+1}-x_i)^2 + (y_{i+1}-y_i)^2}$$

For the rectangular pocket (perimeter only):

- Segment P1 → P2 (along X): $\sqrt{(80-20)^2 + (20-20)^2} = 60$ mm
- Segment P2 → P3 (along Y): $\sqrt{(80-80)^2 + (60-20)^2} = 40$ mm
- Segment P3 → P4 (along -X): $\sqrt{(20-80)^2 + (60-60)^2} = 60$ mm
- Segment P4 → P1 (along -Y): $\sqrt{(20-20)^2 + (20-60)^2} = 40$ mm

$$L_{total} = 60 + 40 + 60 + 40 = 200 \; \text{mm}$$

**Machining time at F = 127 mm/min:**

$$T_{machining} = \frac{L_{total}}{F} = \frac{200}{127} \approx 1.575 \; \text{minutes}$$

$$\boxed{T_{machining} \approx 1 \; \text{minute} \; 35 \; \text{seconds}}$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Code

| Scenario | Recommended Code | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Tool moves from home to workpiece | **G00** | Rapid, non-cutting, save time |
| Tool performs straight cut | **G01** | Linear interpolation at controlled feed |
| Tool performs a CW arc | **G02** | Circular interpolation CW |
| Tool performs a CCW arc | **G03** | Circular interpolation CCW |
| Need to make a hole | **G81** (drilling canned cycle) | Avoids manual repetitive coding |
| Tool profile must match part profile | **G41/G42** | Cutter radius compensation |
| Switching to a new tool | **M06** | Automatic tool change |
| Program end | **M30** | Resets program to beginning |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Five-Stage Programming Exercise Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Engineering Drawing] --> B[Stage 1: Drawing Interpretation]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Process Planning]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Tool Path Planning]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Coordinate Calculation]
    E --> F[Stage 5: Code Generation G and M]
    F --> G{Verification with Simulation}
    G -->|Pass| H[Program Ready for CNC]
    G -->|Fail| D
    H --> I[End: Executable Program]
```

**Interpretation of nodes:**
- `A` = Entry point with raw engineering drawing input.
- `B` = First cognitive stage — student must identify all geometric features, datums, and tolerances.
- `C` = Machine, tool, and parameter selection.
- `D` = Plotting the cutter's physical trajectory on the drawing.
- `E` = Numerical computation of all coordinates using formulas from SECTION 2.
- `F` = Final code writing in word-address or APT format.
- `G` = Verification gate (either by simulation software or manual dry-run).
- `H` = Approved program ready for production.
- `I` = Output — executable NC program.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block Structure of a CNC Program

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Program Start O1001] --> P2[Safety Block G21 G90 G54]
    P2 --> P3[Tool Change M06 T01]
    P3 --> P4[Rapid to Approach G00]
    P4 --> P5[Plunge Move G01 Z]
    P5 --> P6[Cutting Moves G01 G02 G03]
    P6 --> P7[Compensation Engage G41 G42]
    P7 --> P8[Canned Cycles G81 G83]
    P8 --> P9[Retract G00 Z]
    P9 --> P10[Compensation Cancel G40]
    P10 --> P11[Coolant OFF M09]
    P11 --> P12[Spindle Stop M05]
    P12 --> P13[Program End M30]
```

**Interpretation of the linear flow:**
- This represents the **canonical structure** of every CNC part program.
- `P1` (program number) → `P13` (program end) is the linear execution order.
- `P7` and `P10` form a **compensation pair** — every G41/G42 engagement MUST have a corresponding G40 cancellation.
- `P8` (canned cycles) is optional and used only for hole-making operations.

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Tool Path Visualization for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Approach Point: 15 15] --> Q1[Plunge to Z minus 5]
    Q1 --> Q2[Line to P1: 20 20]
    Q2 --> Q3[Line to P2: 80 20]
    Q3 --> Q4[Line to P3: 80 60]
    Q4 --> Q5[Line to P4: 20 60]
    Q5 --> Q6[Line back to P1: 20 20]
    Q6 --> Q7[Cancel Compensation G40]
    Q7 --> Q8[Rapid Retract Z plus 25]
```

**Interpretation:**
- `Q0` is the safe approach point (outside the pocket).
- `Q1` is the controlled plunge into the material.
- `Q2` to `Q6` form the **closed rectangular loop** — the actual pocket boundary.
- `Q7` and `Q8` are the retract and cancellation sequence.

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — APT vs Manual Programming Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Programming Task] --> R2{Part Geometry}
    R2 -->|Simple 2D Profile| R3[Manual G-code Programming]
    R2 -->|Complex 3D Surface| R4[APT Language Programming]
    R2 -->|Free-form Curves| R5[CAM Software CAD-CAM]
    R3 --> R6[Word-address format]
    R4 --> R7[Symbolic APT format]
    R5 --> R8[MasterCAM, CATIA, NX]
    R6 --> R9[Output: NC file]
    R7 --> R9
    R8 --> R9
```

**Interpretation:**
- For **2D profiles** (lines, arcs, simple pockets), manual G-code is sufficient and is the focus of KTU 2024 exams.
- For **3D sculptured surfaces**, APT or CAM software is required.
- All three paths converge to the same output: an **NC file** consumable by a CNC controller.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**  
**Define the term "Word-Address Format" as used in CNC part programming. Explain with a suitable example the structure of a typical block.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Word-Address Format** is a method of writing NC part programs where each piece of information is written as a separate "word" consisting of an **address letter** (X, Y, Z, F, S, T, etc.) followed by a numerical value. This format allows words to appear in any order within a block and is the most widely used standard under **ISO 6983**.

**Example block:**
```
N40 G01 X30.0 Y20.0 F150 S800
```

**Decoded meaning:**
- `N40` — Block (sequence) number 40
- `G01` — Linear interpolation command
- `X30.0` — Move to X-coordinate 30 mm
- `Y20.0` — Move to Y-coordinate 20 mm
- `F150` — Feed rate of 150 mm/min
- `S800` — Spindle speed 800 RPM

The **word-address format** provides flexibility, readability, and error-detection capability through its modular structure. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**  
**List any six commonly used G-codes with their functions. Why is G90 called a modal code?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No | G-Code | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | G00 | Rapid traverse (point-to-point) |
| 2 | G01 | Linear interpolation (controlled feed) |
| 3 | G02 | Circular interpolation, clockwise |
| 4 | G03 | Circular interpolation, counter-clockwise |
| 5 | G17 | XY plane selection |
| 6 | G90 | Absolute coordinate programming |

**G90 is called a *modal code*** because once it is activated in a block, it remains effective for all subsequent blocks until it is explicitly cancelled by another code from the same modal group (in this case, **G91** for incremental programming). Non-modal codes, in contrast, are effective only in the block where they appear. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU Internal Choice Pattern):** In KTU 2024 ESE, Part B questions carry 14 marks and provide an internal choice between two alternatives on the same module. Both options are given below with full model solutions.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2, Programming Exercise]**  
A CNC vertical machining center is to be used to machine the **rectangular plate** shown in Figure (assumed): dimensions **80 mm × 60 mm × 10 mm** (L × W × H) from a mild steel blank of size **100 mm × 80 mm × 12 mm**. The profile must be machined on the perimeter to final dimensions, with **R5 mm fillets** at all four corners. Use a **Ø10 mm HSS end mill (4-flute)**, climb milling, and ISO 6983 word-address format.

**Given:**
- Cutting speed $V_c$ = 25 m/min
- Feed per tooth $f_z$ = 0.05 mm/tooth
- Depth of cut $d$ = 2 mm (per pass, multiple passes)
- Cutter radius $r$ = 5 mm

**Required:**

**(a)** Calculate the spindle speed (N), feed rate (F), and total tool path length. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete manual part program in word-address format with proper cutter compensation (G42) and all safety blocks. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution A**

#### **Part (a) — Numerical Calculations (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Spindle Speed (N) (2 marks)**
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 10} = \frac{25000}{31.416}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 796 \; \text{RPM} \; \approx 800 \; \text{RPM (rounded to controller-safe value)}}$$

**Step 2: Feed Rate (F) (2 marks)**
$$F = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$$

$$F = 0.05 \cdot 4 \cdot 800 = 1.6 \cdot 800$$

$$\boxed{F = 1280 \; \text{mm/min}}$$

**Step 3: Total Tool Path Length (3 marks)**

For a rectangle with **R5 mm corner fillets**, the path consists of:
- 4 straight segments
- 4 arc segments

Straight segment length calculation:
$$L_{straight,total} = 2 \cdot (L - 2r) + 2 \cdot (W - 2r)$$

$$L_{straight,total} = 2 \cdot (80 - 10) + 2 \cdot (60 - 10)$$

$$L_{straight,total} = 2 \cdot 70 + 2 \cdot 50 = 140 + 100$$

$$L_{straight,total} = 240 \; \text{mm}$$

Arc segment length (4 quarter-circles = 1 full circle circumference):
$$L_{arc,total} = 2 \pi r = 2 \pi \cdot 5 = 31.416 \; \text{mm}$$

Total path length:
$$L_{total} = L_{straight,total} + L_{arc,total} = 240 + 31.416$$

$$\boxed{L_{total} \approx 271.4 \; \text{mm}}$$

**Machining time:**
$$T = \frac{L_{total}}{F} = \frac{271.4}{1280} \approx 0.212 \; \text{min} \approx 12.7 \; \text{seconds}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Complete Part Program (7 marks)**

**Work coordinate origin:** Bottom-left corner of the final part at the **top surface of the workpiece**.

```
%
O2001 (PROFILE MILLING - 80x60 mm RECTANGLE WITH R5 FILLETS)
N05 G21 G90 G94 G17 G54                       (1 mark - Initialization)
N10 G00 Z50.0 M08                             (1 mark - Safe Z, coolant ON)
N15 T01 M06                                   (1 mark - Tool change to Ø10 end mill)
N20 G00 X-5.0 Y-5.0                           (Approach point outside part)
N25 G00 Z2.0                                  (Position above workpiece)
N30 G01 Z-2.0 F80                             (Plunge first pass)
N35 G42 D01 X0 Y0 F1280                       (1.5 marks - Engage G42 compensation)
N40 G01 X80.0 Y0                              (Line along +X)
N45 G02 X75.0 Y5.0 R5.0                       (1.5 marks - CW arc, corner 1)
N50 G01 X75.0 Y55.0                           (Line along +Y)
N55 G02 X70.0 Y60.0 R5.0                      (CW arc, corner 2)
N60 G01 X5.0 Y60.0                            (Line along -X)
N65 G02 X0.0 Y55.0 R5.0                       (CW arc, corner 3)
N70 G01 X0.0 Y5.0                             (Line along -Y)
N75 G02 X5.0 Y0.0 R5.0                        (1 mark - CW arc, corner 4)
N80 G01 X0.0 Y0.0                             (Close loop)
N85 G40 X-5.0 Y-5.0                           (0.5 marks - Cancel G42, retract)
N90 G00 Z50.0                                 (Rapid to safe Z)
N95 M09                                       (Coolant OFF)
N100 G00 X0 Y0                                (Return home)
N105 M05                                      (Spindle stop)
N110 M30                                      (0.5 marks - Program end)
%
```

**Mark distribution rationale (7 marks):**
- Safety/initialization blocks: **1 mark**
- Tool change block: **1 mark**
- Compensation engagement: **1.5 marks**
- Arc programming with G02: **2 marks** (multiple occurrences)
- Compensation cancellation: **0.5 marks**
- End sequence (M09, M05, M30): **1 mark**

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Option 2)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, APT Exercise]**  
**(a)** Explain the basic structure of an APT part program with the help of a suitable example. List and briefly explain any **five APT statements** used for defining geometry and motion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an APT program to machine the **outline of a triangular profile** with vertices at **(10, 10)**, **(80, 10)**, and **(45, 70)** on a CNC milling machine using a **Ø8 mm end mill**. Use a depth of cut of 3 mm and climb milling. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution B**

#### **Part (a) — APT Structure and Statements (7 marks)**

**Basic Structure of an APT Program (3 marks):**

An APT (Automatically Programmed Tools) program consists of four logical sections:

1. **Initialization / Setup Statements** — define part name, machine, tolerances.
2. **Geometry Definition Statements** — define points, lines, circles, planes.
3. **Motion Definition Statements** — define initial position, approach, cutting, retract.
4. **Termination Statement** — `FINI`.

**Example skeleton:**
```
PARTNO/TRIANGLE_PART
MACHINE/VMC,3AXIS
CLPRNT
CUTTER/ 8.0
FROM/ 0, 0, 25
GO/ TO, PT1
GOLFT/ L1, PAST, L2
GORGT/ L2, PAST, L3
GOFWD/ L3, PAST, L1
RAPID
GOTO/ 0, 0, 25
FINI
```

**Five APT Statements (4 marks):**

| S.No | Statement | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PARTNO** | `PARTNO/name` | Assigns a part program name for identification |
| 2 | **CUTTER** | `CUTTER/ d` | Defines tool diameter (Cutter radius is automatically d/2) |
| 3 | **FROM** | `FROM/ x, y, z` | Defines the starting point of the tool before cutting begins |
| 4 | **GOTO** | `GOTO/ x, y, z` | Moves the tool to a specified point along a straight line |
| 5 | **FINI** | `FINI` | Marks the end of the APT program |

**Bonus statements (for higher marks):**

- `SPINDL/` — sets spindle speed and direction.
- `FEDRAT/` — sets feed rate.
- `COOLNT/` — turns coolant ON/OFF.
- `INTOL/` and `OUTTOL/` — define inside/outside tolerances for cutter compensation.

---

#### **Part (b) — APT Program for Triangle Profile (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define Geometry (2 marks):**

Vertices:
- PT1 = (10, 10)
- PT2 = (80, 10)
- PT3 = (45, 70)

Edges:
- L1 = line through PT1 and PT2
- L2 = line through PT2 and PT3
- L3 = line through PT3 and PT1

**Step 2 — Define Tool and Parameters (1 mark):**

- Tool diameter: 8 mm
- Spindle speed: assume $V_c$ = 30 m/min ⇒ $N = \frac{1000 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot 8} \approx 1194$ RPM
- Feed rate: assume $f_z = 0.04$ mm/tooth, $z = 2$ flutes ⇒ $F = 0.04 \cdot 2 \cdot 1194 \approx 95$ mm/min

**Step 3 — Write the APT Program (4 marks):**

```
PARTNO TRIANGLE_PROFILE_80x70
MACHINE VMC, 3AXIS
CLPRNT
CUTTER/ 8.0
INTOL/ 0.02
OUTTOL/ 0.02
FROM/ 0, 0, 25
GO/ TO, PT1
SPINDL/ 1194, CLW
FEDRAT/ 95
COOLNT/ ON
RAPID
GOTO/ 10, 10, 3
GODLTA/ 0, 0, -3               (Plunge to depth Z = -3)
TLLFT, 4                       (Left tolerance = tool radius)
GOLFT/ L1, PAST, L2            (Cut along L1, past L2)
GORGT/ L2, PAST, L3            (Cut along L2, past L3)
GOFWD/ L3, PAST, L1            (Cut along L3, past L1)
RAPID
GOTO/ 0, 0, 25                 (Retract to safe Z)
COOLNT/ OFF
SPINDL/ OFF
FINI
```

**Mark distribution rationale (7 marks):**
- Geometry definition (vertices and lines): **2 marks**
- Tool and parameter setup: **1 mark**
- Motion commands and cutting sequence: **3 marks**
- Termination and coolant logic: **1 mark**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Programming Exercises:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the safety block** — A program that starts directly with `G00 X_Y_` without initial `G21 G90 G17 G54` loses **1–2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Wrong feed/spindle units** — Using feed/rev (`G95`) with mm/min numerical value OR not matching units (imperial vs metric). Always explicitly write **G21** (metric) and **G94** (feed/min).
> 3. **Not cancelling cutter compensation** — Every `G41/G42` MUST have a matching `G40`. Missing this is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Arc direction error** — Confusing G02 (CW) and G03 (CCW). Always draw the arc on paper and check the rotation direction relative to the **positive Z-axis looking down**.
> 5. **Forgetting tool change block** — Using a tool without `M06 T01` declaration loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Wrong arc center (I, J, K) calculation** — I, J, K are **incremental from arc start point**, not absolute coordinates. This is the #1 calculation error.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** — For APT problems, students lose marks by not stating tool diameter, tolerances, and origin explicitly at the start.
> 8. **Mixing modal codes incorrectly** — Putting `G01` and `G00` in the same block (only one motion code per block). Use multiple blocks.
> 9. **Decimal point errors** — Writing `X250` when meaning `X25.0`. Always use explicit decimal points.
> 10. **Missing M30 termination** — Program without `M30` is considered incomplete and loses **0.5–1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Programming Exercise):**

- ⭐ **Word-address format** is the universally accepted ISO 6983 standard.
- ⭐ Every program must begin with: **G21 G90 G94 G17 G54** (metric, absolute, feed/min, XY-plane, work coordinate).
- ⭐ **Modal codes** stay active; **non-modal codes** work only for one block.
- ⭐ **G00** = rapid (no cutting); **G01** = straight-line cutting; **G02** = CW arc; **G03** = CCW arc.
- ⭐ **G41** = cutter compensation left; **G42** = cutter compensation right; **G40** = cancel compensation.
- ⭐ **Arc center offsets** are computed as: $I = X_{center} - X_{start}$, $J = Y_{center} - Y_{start}$.
- ⭐ **Spindle speed formula**: $N = \frac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$.
- ⭐ **Feed rate formula**: $F = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$.
- ⭐ **Tool path length**: $L_{total} = \sum \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$.
- ⭐ **APT language** uses verbs like `GOTO/`, `GOLFT/`, `GORGT/`, `GOFWD/`, `FROM/`, `SPINDL/`, `FEDRAT/`, `FINI`.
- ⭐ **Canned cycles** (G81 drilling, G83 peck drilling) are used for repetitive hole-making operations.
- ⭐ Always end with **M30** (program end + rewind).
- ⭐ **Compensation pairs must always close** — every G41/G42 needs a G40.
- ⭐ **Decimals** should always be explicit (`X25.0` not `X250`) to avoid ambiguity.
- ⭐ **Work coordinate system** (G54–G59) is critical for setting up the part origin.
- ⭐ **Climb milling** is preferred for finish cuts (better surface finish, longer tool life).
- ⭐ **Cutter path length** determines **machining time**: $T = L_{total} / F$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Board Tip:** In the 14-mark programming exercise, allocate your answer time as follows — **3 minutes** for setup calculations (spindle speed, feed rate), **5 minutes** for coordinate computation, and **12 minutes** for writing the program with proper annotations. Always annotate each block with a brief comment explaining its purpose — this earns you **easy readability marks** from the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAUT504 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Introduction # 📘 Module 3 — Introduction to Numerical Control (NC) and Computer Numerical Control (CNC) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Numerical Control (NC)** is a form of programmable automation in which the processing equipment (machine tool, robot, inspection device, etc.) is controlled by a set of numerical data (instructions) inserted into the system in the form of alphanumeric characters. The instructions are coded and arranged in a logical sequence to direct the machine tool to perform the desired machining operations automatically, with little or no human intervention. **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** is the evolution of NC in which an on-board computer (typically a microprocessor) is used as the controller, replacing the hard-wired logic controllers of classical NC. The part program is stored in the computer's memory, enabling editing, looping, subroutines, and macro programming. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** > *Numerical Control is the operation of machine tools by means of specifically coded instructions prepared in a format that can be interpreted by the machine controller. When the controller is a dedicated computer, the system is termed Computer Numerical Control (CNC).* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" Analogy 🍰 Imagine you are teaching your grandmother (the machine) to bake a cake (the part): - The **machine tool** is the baker — it can knead, mix, and bake, but needs to be told *exactly* what to do. - The **NC tape / part program** is the **recipe card** — a list of coded instructions ("Add 200 g flour, mix for 3 min, bake at 180°C for 40 min"). - The **MCU (Machine Control Unit)** is the **person reading the recipe aloud** — interpreting each line and telling the baker which action to perform next. A **CNC machine** is like giving the baker an **iPad with the recipe** — the baker (machine) can now search, loop (repeat a step), and even adjust the recipe (parametric programming) using a built-in computer. > [!NOTE] > **Key Milestones in NC History (Frequently Asked in KTU):** > - **1947** — John T. Parsons (Father of NC) and Frank L. Stulen developed the concept at Wright-Patterson Air Force Base. > - **1952** — First NC machine installed at MIT (a Cincinnati Hydrotel milling machine). > - **1955** — NC becomes commercially available. > - **1970s** — CNC emerges with microprocessor-based controllers. ### 1.3 NC vs CNC — A Side-by-Side Snapshot | Parameter | NC (Hard-wired) | CNC (Computer-based) | |---|---|---| | **Controller** | Logic gates, relay panels | Microprocessor / PC | | **Program storage** | Punched tape / cards | RAM, hard disk, USB | | **Editing** | Requires new tape | On-screen editing | | **Looping** | Limited | Full subroutine support | | **Cost** | Lower (vintage) | Higher (modern) | | **Flexibility** | Low | High | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why NC? — The Driving Forces The transition from manual machining to NC was driven by **four engineering imperatives** that the KTU examiner frequently tests: 1. **Higher accuracy and repeatability** — NC machines routinely achieve positioning accuracies of **±0.0025 mm**, far exceeding manual capability. 2. **Reduced lead time** — A complex aerospace part that took weeks manually can be produced in hours with NC. 3. **Lower operator skill requirement** — A trained NC operator is cheaper to hire than a master toolmaker. 4. **Easier part-to-part repeatability** — Critical for mass production (e.g., 10,000 identical engine blocks). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Favourite:** "Differentiate between NC and CNC" is asked almost every semester. Memorize the table above. ## 2.2 Coordinate System Conventions (NC Axes) Every NC machine uses a **Cartesian coordinate system** (right-hand rule). KTU expects students to draw the 3-axis and 5-axis configurations. | Axis | Convention (Right-Hand Rule) | Typical Machine Movement | |---|---|---| | **X** | Longest travel, parallel to spindle face | Left ↔ Right | | **Y** | 90° to X | In ↔ Out | | **Z** | Parallel to spindle axis (rotation) | Up ↕ Down | | **A** | Rotation about X | Tilt around X | | **B** | Rotation about Y | Tilt around Y | | **C** | Rotation about Z | Spindle rotation | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Feed rate** | $f_r = N \times z \times f_z$ | mm/min | Turning, milling | | 2 | **Spindle speed** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \times v_c}{\pi \times D}$ | rev/min (rpm) | Cutting velocity conversion | | 3 | **MRR (Metal Removal Rate)** | $\text{MRR} = f_r \times a_p \times a_e$ | mm³/min | Productivity | | 4 | **Programming time** | $t_p = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ | min | Estimate cycle time | | 5 | **NC positioning accuracy** | $\text{TP} = 0.05\% \times L + 0.01$ | mm | ISO 230/2 standard | | 6 | **Block execution time** | $t_b = \dfrac{\Delta L}{f_r}$ | min/block | Verification | > [!WARNING] > **DO NOT use the `|` symbol inside any table cell** in your answer script — it breaks the markdown table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. In manual KTU scripts, just use plain text descriptions. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility of NC/CNC - **Aerospace:** Machining monolithic titanium wing spars (5-axis CNC). - **Automotive:** Crankcase, cylinder head, and gearbox production lines. - **Medical:** Patient-specific orthopedic implants (knee, hip). - **Die & Mould:** Complex injection mould cavities with sub-micron finish. - **Electronics:** PCB drilling with positioning accuracy of **0.01 mm**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Spindle Speed and Feed Rate This is the most frequently asked numerical in KTU Module 3. The derivation below is presented exactly as a topper would write it on the answer script. ### 3.1.1 Derivation of Spindle Speed (N) **Given:** Cutting speed $v_c$ (m/min) and tool/workpiece diameter $D$ (mm). **Step 1 — Starting point: Definition of cutting speed.** Cutting speed is defined as the tangential surface speed of the workpiece (or tool) at the cutting interface. $$ v_c = \pi \times D \times N $$ **Step 2 — Unit alignment.** The diameter $D$ is conventionally measured in **mm**, while $v_c$ is given in **m/min**. To reconcile units, we convert $D$ from mm to m by dividing by 1000. $$ v_c = \pi \times \dfrac{D}{1000} \times N $$ **Step 3 — Solving for N.** Rearranging the equation to isolate the unknown $N$: $$ N = \dfrac{1000 \times v_c}{\pi \times D} $$ **Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.** > **Problem:** A turning operation is performed on a workpiece of diameter $D = 80\ \text{mm}$ with a cutting speed of $v_c = 200\ \text{m/min}$. Calculate the spindle speed $N$. Substituting into the derived formula: $$ N = \dfrac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 80} $$ $$ N = \dfrac{200000}{251.327} $$ $$ N = 795.77\ \text{rpm} \approx 796\ \text{rpm} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** Always round to the nearest standard spindle speed available on the machine (e.g., 800 rpm if available, else 750 rpm). In KTU scripts, the examiner awards **half a mark** for explicitly stating the final unit. ### 3.1.2 Derivation of Feed Rate ($f_r$) **Step 1 — Definition.** Feed per revolution ($f$) is the linear distance the tool advances per spindle revolution. For a multi-tooth milling cutter with $z$ teeth, the feed per tooth is $f_z$, so: $$ f = z \times f_z $$ **Step 2 — Feed rate calculation.** Feed rate is the linear distance traversed per minute: $$ f_r = N \times f = N \times z \times f_z $$ **Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example.** > **Problem:** A 4-tooth end mill ($z = 4$) operates at $N = 1200\ \text{rpm}$ with a feed per tooth $f_z = 0.05\ \text{mm/tooth}$. Calculate the feed rate $f_r$. $$ f_r = 1200 \times 4 \times 0.05 $$ $$ f_r = 240\ \text{mm/min} $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Classical NC vs CNC vs DNC vs MMC This comparison matrix is **high-yield** and almost always appears in Part B (14 marks). | Feature | NC (Hard-wired) | CNC | DNC (Direct NC) | MMC (Mfg. Msg Cell) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Control** | Hard-wired logic | On-board computer | Mainframe → multiple machines | Computer-integrated cell | | **Program storage** | Punched tape/card | Internal memory | Mainframe host | Shared network database | | **No. of machines** | 1 | 1 | Several (5–100+) | 5–20 with robots | | **Editing** | Impossible | On-line | From mainframe | Real-time scheduling | | **Flexibility** | Very low | High | Medium | Very high | | **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | High | Highest | | **Application era** | 1955–1970 | 1970–today | 1970s–1990s | 1990s–today | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pattern (Dec 2022, June 2023, Dec 2023):** "Compare NC, CNC, and DNC systems" has appeared **3 times in 2 years**. Memorize this matrix verbatim. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The NC System Architecture The following Mermaid diagram captures the canonical NC system architecture, which is the most-tested diagram in KTU Module 3. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Part Program
Punched Tape or Memory] --> B[Machine Control Unit
MCU] B --> C[Servo Drive
Amplifier] C --> D[Servo Motor
X, Y, Z axes] D --> E[Machine Tool
Slide and Spindle] E --> F[Feedback
Transducer] F --> B G[Operator Console
Keyboard and Display] --> B H[Coolant and Lubrication
Subsystem] --> E styleA fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000000 styleB fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000 styleC fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000000 styleD fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000000 styleE fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000000 styleF fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c,color:#000000 styleG fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,color:#000000 styleH fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Closed-Loop vs Open-Loop Control Architecture A **very common 14-mark question** asks students to draw and explain the difference between open-loop and closed-loop NC systems. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OPEN[Open Loop NC System] A1[MCU] --> A2[Stepper Motor Driver] A2 --> A3[Stepper Motor] A3 --> A4[Machine Slide] end subgraph CLOSED[Closed Loop NC System] B1[MCU] --> B2[Servo Amplifier] B2 --> B3[Servo Motor] B3 --> B4[Machine Slide] B4 --> B5[Linear Encoder or Resolver] B5 --> B1 end styleA1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000 styleA2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000000 styleA3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000000 styleA4 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000000 styleB1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000 styleB2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000000 styleB3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000000 styleB4 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000000 styleB5 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Open loop** uses stepper motors — no feedback; cheaper but less accurate. > **Closed loop** uses servo motors with feedback — more accurate, more expensive. Most industrial CNC machines use closed-loop control. ## 4.3 Evolution Timeline (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[1952 - MIT First NC] --> T2[1955 - Commercial NC] T2 --> T3[1970 - Microprocessor CNC] T3 --> T4[1980s - DNC Networks] T4 --> T5[1990s - FMS and CIM] T5 --> T6[2000s - Open Architecture CNC] T6 --> T7[2020s - AI Assisted Machining] styleT1 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000000 styleT2 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000000 styleT3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000000 styleT4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000 styleT5 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000000 styleT6 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000000 styleT7 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c,color:#000000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]` **Define Numerical Control (NC). List any four advantages of NC over manual machining.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Numerical Control is a method of controlling the movements of machine tool slides and other functions (spindle on/off, coolant on/off) by means of coded numerical instructions fed into the system, where these instructions are processed by the Machine Control Unit (MCU) to drive the motors. **Advantages (2 marks — any four of the following):** 1. **Higher accuracy and repeatability** — typically ±0.0025 mm. 2. **Lower production lead time** — direct part production from CAD data. 3. **Reduced human error** — minimal operator intervention. 4. **Easier storage and retrieval** — programs can be stored digitally. 5. **Lower scrap rate** — proven programs yield predictable results. 6. **Complex geometry capability** — 3D contours, splines, free-form surfaces. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems in NC machines.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Open Loop | Closed Loop | |---|---|---| | **Feedback** | Absent | Present (encoder/resolver) | | **Motor** | Stepper motor | Servo motor (DC/AC) | | **Accuracy** | Lower (cumulative error) | Higher (corrected continuously) | | **Cost** | Low | High | | **Application** | Light-duty (PCB drills, small routers) | Heavy-duty (milling, turning) | A **closed-loop system** continuously compares the actual slide position (from feedback) with the commanded position, computes the error, and drives the motor to minimize it — yielding higher accuracy. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the components and working of a Numerical Control (NC) system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A milling operation uses a 4-tooth end mill cutter of diameter $D = 50\ \text{mm}$ on a workpiece with cutting speed $v_c = 150\ \text{m/min}$ and feed per tooth $f_z = 0.08\ \text{mm/tooth}$. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed $N$ in rpm. **(3 marks)** (ii) Feed rate $f_r$ in mm/min. **(2 marks)** (iii) Metal removal rate (MRR) if depth of cut $a_p = 3\ \text{mm}$ and width of cut $a_e = 20\ \text{mm}$. **(2 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks The NC system consists of the following **four major components**: 1. **Part Program (1 mark):** A sequential set of coded instructions stored on a punched tape, punched card, magnetic tape, or computer memory. Each line is called a *block* and contains words like N, G, X, Y, Z, F, S, T, M. 2. **Machine Control Unit — MCU (2 marks):** The "brain" of the NC system. It: - Reads the part program (block by block). - Decodes each instruction. - Performs interpolation (linear, circular, helical). - Sends pulse signals to the servo drives. - Receives feedback from transducers and computes position error. 3. **Drive System (1 mark):** Converts electrical pulses from the MCU into mechanical motion. Includes: - **Servo amplifier** (power booster) - **Servo motor / Stepper motor** (actuator) - **Ball screw** (converts rotary to linear motion) - **Slide** (the moving element of the machine) 4. **Feedback System (1 mark):** Closed-loop NC machines include **transducers** (linear encoders, resolvers, or inductosyns) that measure the actual slide position and feed this back to the MCU for error correction. 5. **Machine Tool (1 mark):** The mechanical structure (bed, column, spindle, slides) that physically performs the cutting operation. **Working Principle (1 mark):** The MCU reads the program → decodes the target coordinates → compares with actual position (from feedback) → drives the motor until the error is zero → moves to the next block. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $D = 50\ \text{mm}$, $v_c = 150\ \text{m/min}$, $z = 4$ teeth, $f_z = 0.08\ \text{mm/tooth}$, $a_p = 3\ \text{mm}$, $a_e = 20\ \text{mm}$. **(i) Spindle speed (3 marks):** $$ N = \dfrac{1000 \times v_c}{\pi \times D} $$ $$ N = \dfrac{1000 \times 150}{\pi \times 50} $$ $$ N = \dfrac{150000}{157.08} = 954.93\ \text{rpm} \approx 955\ \text{rpm} $$ **(ii) Feed rate (2 marks):** $$ f_r = N \times z \times f_z = 954.93 \times 4 \times 0.08 $$ $$ f_r = 305.58\ \text{mm/min} \approx 306\ \text{mm/min} $$ **(iii) Metal Removal Rate (2 marks):** $$ \text{MRR} = f_r \times a_p \times a_e $$ $$ \text{MRR} = 305.58 \times 3 \times 20 = 18334.8\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min} $$ $$ \text{MRR} \approx 18335\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 18.335\ \text{cm}^3/\text{min} $$ --- ### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** Compare and contrast NC, CNC, and DNC systems based on **at least 8 parameters**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A turning operation is performed on a 100 mm diameter workpiece with cutting speed $v_c = 250\ \text{m/min}$, feed $f = 0.25\ \text{mm/rev}$, and depth of cut $a_p = 2.5\ \text{mm}$. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed $N$ **(2 marks)** (ii) Feed rate $f_r$ **(2 marks)** (iii) Machining time for a cut length of $L = 200\ \text{mm}$ **(3 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks Refer to the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.2** of these notes. The student must present a comparison table covering: | # | Parameter | NC | CNC | DNC | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Controller type | Hard-wired | Computer | Mainframe | | 2 | Storage medium | Punched tape | Memory | Mainframe | | 3 | Editing | Not possible | Possible | From host | | 4 | Subroutines | Limited | Full | Full | | 5 | No. of machines | 1 | 1 | Many | | 6 | Cost | Low | Medium | High | | 7 | Reliability | High (no software bug) | High | High | | 8 | Operator skill | High | Low | Low | > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Pen):** Students often **confuse CNC with DNC**. CNC has **one computer per machine**; DNC has **one mainframe serving many machines**. Drawing this distinction clearly is worth **2 marks** in KTU scripts. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $D = 100\ \text{mm}$, $v_c = 250\ \text{m/min}$, $f = 0.25\ \text{mm/rev}$, $a_p = 2.5\ \text{mm}$, $L = 200\ \text{mm}$. **(i) Spindle speed (2 marks):** $$ N = \dfrac{1000 \times 250}{\pi \times 100} $$ $$ N = \dfrac{250000}{314.16} = 795.77\ \text{rpm} \approx 796\ \text{rpm} $$ **(ii) Feed rate (2 marks):** $$ f_r = N \times f = 795.77 \times 0.25 = 198.94\ \text{mm/min} \approx 199\ \text{mm/min} $$ **(iii) Machining time (3 marks):** $$ t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r} = \dfrac{200}{198.94} = 1.005\ \text{min} \approx 1\ \text{min} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Penalty Trap:** Students often forget to convert $D$ from **mm to m** in the spindle speed formula. This **silently** leads to an answer 1000× too large. Always write down the unit conversion step explicitly: "$D = 100\ \text{mm} = 0.1\ \text{m}$". --- ## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize Before KTU Exam:** - ✅ **Definition of NC & CNC** — write verbatim from the formal definition in Section 1.1. - ✅ **John T. Parsons** — Father of NC (1947). **1952** — MIT first NC. - ✅ **Right-hand rule** for axis convention — X, Y, Z, then A, B, C as rotations. - ✅ **Spindle speed formula:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \times v_c}{\pi \times D}$ — unit conversion is the most common error. - ✅ **Feed rate formula:** $f_r = N \times z \times f_z$ — used in milling. - ✅ **MRR formula:** $\text{MRR} = f_r \times a_p \times a_e$. - ✅ **NC = hard-wired**, **CNC = on-board computer**, **DNC = mainframe network** — memorize the 8-parameter table. - ✅ **Open loop = stepper motor (no feedback)**; **Closed loop = servo motor (with encoder)**. - ✅ **Positioning accuracy** of NC: typically **±0.0025 mm** or **±2.5 microns**. - ✅ **Feedback devices:** linear encoder, resolver, inductosyn, glass scale. - ✅ **Interpolation methods:** linear (G01), circular (G02/G03), helical, parabolic, cubic. - ✅ Draw the **block diagram of NC system** from memory — it's worth 3–5 marks in Part B. - ✅ **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** is the heart of every NC system — know its 5 functions. - ✅ **Advantages of NC:** accuracy, repeatability, low lead time, low skill, complex geometry. - ✅ **Limitations of NC:** high initial cost, skilled part programmer required, idle time for small batches. - ✅ **Difference between absolute (G90) and incremental (G91) coordinates** — coming up in next topic. - ✅ **APT language** — the original NC programming language (1956, MIT). Modern equivalent: G-code (ISO 6983). > [!NOTE] > **📌 Examiner's Last-Minute Tip:** For 14-mark Part B questions on this topic, allocate your time as: > - **4 minutes** — drawing the block diagram. > - **4 minutes** — explaining MCU + drive + feedback. > - **6 minutes** — solving the numerical. > - **3 minutes** — reviewing units and rounding. **End of Module 3 — Introduction Notes**

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT504 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to "Types" in CNC — The Engineering Taxonomy

In **Computer Aided Design and Manufacturing (CAD/CAM)**, the term **"Types"** within **Module 3 (CNC Technology & Part Programming)** refers to the systematic classification of **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) machines** and **CNC control systems** based on their motion characteristics, axis configurations, control philosophy, and intended manufacturing applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **CNC (Computer Numerical Control)** is a manufacturing method in which a computer decodes and executes a set of programmed instructions to control the motion and operation of machine tools, replacing the manual hand-wheel operation traditionally performed by a human operator.

Classification is necessary because **no single CNC machine can economically and efficiently manufacture every part**. A turning operation on a cylindrical shaft requires a different kinematic structure than drilling holes on a flat plate. Therefore, understanding the **types** allows the manufacturing engineer to select the **right tool for the right job**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot Family" Intuition

Imagine a **family of robots** living in a factory. Each robot has a different set of skills:

- **Robot A (CNC Lathe)** — Can only spin an object and cut into it while it spins. Perfect for making **pencils, shafts, and bolts**.
- **Robot B (CNC Milling Machine)** — Holds a spinning cutter and moves it around a stationary block. Perfect for making **gears, slots, and complex 3D shapes**.
- **Robot C (CNC Drilling Machine)** — A specialist that just pushes a spinning drill into the workpiece. Perfect for making **holes**.
- **Robot D (Machining Center)** — A super-robot that can do all of the above by automatically changing its own tools.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Picture:**
> Just as you would not use a screwdriver to hammer a nail, you must understand the **types** of CNC machines to choose the correct one for each manufacturing task. KTU examiners **frequently ask 3-mark questions** directly testing this classification.

---

## 1.3 Primary Classification Axes

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures the "Types" topic along **three orthogonal classification dimensions**:

| # | Classification Basis | Sub-Types (the actual "Types") |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Based on Motion / Control Loop** | Point-to-Point (PTP), Straight-Cut (Line), Contouring (Continuous Path) |
| **2** | **Based on Machining Process** | CNC Lathe (Turning), CNC Milling, CNC Drilling, CNC Grinding, CNC EDM, CNC Wire-cut |
| **3** | **Based on Axis Configuration** | 2-Axis, 3-Axis, 4-Axis, 5-Axis (Multi-axis machines) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** When a KTU question says *"Classify CNC machines"* without specifying the basis, the **safest and highest-scoring answer** is to present all **three classification schemes** in a single tabulated answer.

---

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary Anchors

- **NC (Numerical Control):** Hardwired control using punched tape; no general-purpose computer.
- **CNC (Computer Numerical Control):** Uses a microcomputer / microcontroller as the heart of the controller; the standard in modern industry.
- **DNC (Direct Numerical Control):** A central mainframe computer directly controls a network of several CNC machines.
- **Axis:** A direction in which the cutting tool or workpiece can be moved (X, Y, Z linear; A, B, C rotary).
- **Slide / Table:** The physical mechanical component that moves along an axis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Based on Motion / Control Loop (KTU High-Yield Topic)

This is the **most important classification** in the KTU Module 3 syllabus because it determines the **mathematical complexity** of the part program.

### 2.1.1 Type 1 — Point-to-Point (PTP) Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The cutting tool moves from one programmed point to the next at **maximum (rapid) feed rate**, with **no controlled path** between the points. The tool is **withdrawn (retracted)** to a safe height during transit so it cannot accidentally gouge the workpiece.

**Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic):**

1. The controller reads the **target coordinates** (X, Y) of the next hole.
2. The tool is **rapidly retracted to a safe Z-clearance plane** to clear any clamps, fixtures, or surface features.
3. The tool travels in **X and Y simultaneously at rapid traverse** to the new position. *Path is irrelevant.*
4. Once positioned, the tool **plunges rapidly (G0)** or **feeds rapidly (G0 / G99)** to the drilling depth.
5. The machining operation (drilling, tapping, boring) executes.
6. Tool retracts and the cycle repeats for the next point.

**Engineering Applications:**

- **Drilling, tapping, reaming, boring** of discrete holes.
- **PCB drilling, sheet-metal punching, spot welding.**
- Any operation where **only the start and end coordinates matter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why no path control?** The cost of accurately tracking a curved path between two points is high. For hole-making, the path is irrelevant — only the **final position** matters. PTP machines are therefore the **cheapest** CNC machines.

---

### 2.1.2 Type 2 — Straight-Cut Control (Line / Parallel Axis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The cutting tool moves parallel to **only one of the machine axes at a time**. The machine can cut along the X-axis OR the Y-axis in a single move, but **not simultaneously on both axes** (and not on diagonal lines).

**Working Principle:**

1. The controller receives a command to cut a straight line.
2. Motion is generated along **one axis only** (X **or** Y) at a controlled feed rate.
3. The slide is locked on the other axis to prevent drift.
4. The cutter moves to the end of the line, then the second axis activates to reposition.
5. The next straight cut begins.

**Engineering Applications:**

- **Milling of rectangular pockets** and slots aligned to machine axes.
- **Simple face milling** of rectangular surfaces.
- Older-generation machines; largely **obsolete** in modern industry but historically important.

---

### 2.1.3 Type 3 — Contouring (Continuous Path / Multi-Axis Simultaneous)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The cutting tool moves in a **precisely controlled continuous path** in 2D or 3D space, with **simultaneous coordinated motion** along two or more axes. This enables machining of **curves, circles, tapers, and sculptured surfaces**.

**Working Principle (The Heart of Modern CNC):**

1. The part program (G-code) specifies a sequence of target coordinates that define a continuous curve.
2. The CNC controller uses a technique called **Interpolation** (see Section 2.3) to break the curve into microscopic **tool-position increments (blending)**.
3. The X and Y slides (and Z, if 3D) move **simultaneously in synchrony**, each with its own precisely calculated velocity at every instant, so the resultant tool path is the desired curve.
4. The cutting tool stays engaged throughout the entire motion, **sculpting** the desired profile.

**Engineering Applications:**

- **Turning of tapers, threads, and contours on a lathe** (X-Z simultaneous motion).
- **Milling of circular pockets, gears, molds, dies, aerospace components, and 3D sculptured surfaces.**
- **Any 2D / 3D profile that is not aligned with a single machine axis.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Many KTU students write *"CNC machines that cut curves use continuous path control"* but forget to mention that **multi-axis simultaneous interpolation** is the underlying mechanism. Always mention **interpolation** explicitly.

---

### 2.1.4 Comparison Table — The Big Three Control Types

| Feature | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Straight-Cut | Contouring (Continuous Path) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Path between points** | Not controlled | Parallel to one axis only | Fully controlled continuous path |
| **Tool during transit** | Retracted to safe Z | Engaged (limited) | Engaged throughout |
| **Simultaneous axes** | One at a time (rapid) | One at a time (controlled) | 2 or more simultaneously |
| **Typical process** | Drilling, tapping | Slot milling, face milling | Turning tapers, profile milling, 3D surfaces |
| **Programming complexity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Hardware cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **KTU mark weight** | 3 marks | 2-3 marks | 5-7 marks (favourite topic) |

---

## 2.2 Classification Based on Machining Process

### 2.2.1 CNC Turning Machines (CNC Lathes)

- **Spins the workpiece** at a controlled RPM about the Z-axis.
- A single-point cutting tool removes material as the workpiece rotates.
- **Standard 2-axis configuration:** X (cross-slide, radial) and Z (longitudinal, along spindle axis).
- Used for **shafts, axles, threaded fasteners, hubs, bushings**.
- **Part programming coordinate origin** is typically set at the **center of the spindle nose on the face of the workpiece**.

### 2.2.2 CNC Milling Machines

- **Spins the cutting tool** (end mill, face mill, ball nose mill) about a vertical (or sometimes horizontal) axis.
- The **workpiece is stationary**; the tool moves in X, Y, Z to remove material.
- **Standard 3-axis configuration:** X (table left-right), Y (table in-out), Z (spindle up-down).
- Used for **slots, pockets, contours, 3D cavities, prismatic parts, molds**.

### 2.2.3 CNC Drilling Machines

- Specialized for **hole-making** operations: drilling, reaming, tapping, boring, counter-boring, counter-sinking.
- Usually PTP control.
- Often integrated as a **drilling head** within a larger machining center.

### 2.2.4 Multi-Tasking Machines (Machining Centers & Turning Centers)

> [!NOTE]
> **Machining Center** = CNC milling machine with an **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)**. Can perform milling, drilling, tapping, and boring in a single setup.
>
> **Turning Center** = CNC lathe with **live tooling and a C-axis / Y-axis**. Can perform turning AND milling/drilling off-center on the same machine.

---

## 2.3 Classification Based on Axis Configuration

The **number of simultaneous axes** is the most commercially important classification because it directly determines the **geometric complexity of parts** that can be machined.

### 2.3.1 Standard Axis Naming Convention (ISO 841)

| Axis | Type | Direction |
|---|---|---|
