For the ideal case with cells of sizes $3\times 3$ and $3\times 2$, the desired exceptions $e_d$ = (total cells) − (ones in cells) = $30 - 11 = 19$ zeros, but arranged so that each cell is dense.

A standard simplified formula for the desired exceptions is:

$$
e_d \;=\; (m - f) \times n \;+\; (f - 1) \times \text{shortest row}
$$

For this $6 \times 5$ matrix with $f = 2$ families:

$$
e_d \;=\; (5 - 2) \times 6 \;+\; (2 - 1) \times 2 \;=\; 18 + 2 \;=\; 20
$$

**Step 5: Compute grouping efficiency.**

$$
\eta_g \;=\; \frac{e_d - e_o}{e_d} \;=\; \frac{20 - 15}{20} \;=\; \frac{5}{20} \;=\; 0.25 \;=\; 25\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A grouping efficiency of 25% means the clustering is not perfectly tight; there are still inter-cell movements. KTU expects students to comment on whether a re-clustering is needed.

---

### Problem 4 — Opitz Code Interpretation

**Question:**
> *A rotational part has the following features: Length-to-diameter ratio = long (2), has a stepped bore (1), has no plane surfaces (0), has external thread (1), material is steel (1), length is 100 mm (3), tolerance is medium (2), surface finish is ground (1). Write the Opitz code.*

**Step-by-step Construction:**

Digit 1 = 0 (rotational)
Digit 2 = 2 (long L/D)
Digit 3 = 1 (stepped bore)
Digit 4 = 0 (no plane surfaces)
Digit 5 = 1 (external thread)
Digit 6 = 1 (steel)
Digit 7 = 3 (length class 100 mm)
Digit 8 = 2 (medium tolerance)
Digit 9 = 1 (ground surface)

$$
\text{Opitz Code} \;=\; 0\,2\,1\,0\,1\;-\;1\,3\,2\,1 \;=\; \boxed{0210\,1\,-\,1321}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse digits 2–5 with secondary features; remember that digits 6–9 are **auxiliary** and digits 2–5 are **form features** for the chosen family in digit 1.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — JIT vs. Traditional vs. GT

| Parameter | Traditional (Push) | JIT (Pull) | GT-Based Cellular |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inventory | High | Very Low | Moderate (cell buffer) |
| Lead Time | Long | Very Short | Short |
| Batch Size | Large | One-piece flow | Small (family batch) |
| Quality Control | Inspection at end | Jidoka — built-in | Standardized per family |
| Setup Time | Long | SMED (single-digit min) | Reduced (similar parts) |
| Workforce | Specialized | Multi-skilled | Multi-skilled cell operators |
| Information Flow | MRP / Schedule | Kanban signal | Cell-level Kanban |
| Bottleneck Handling | Inventory hides it | Exposed and solved | Reduced by cell design |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 JIT Pull System with Two Kanban Loops

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    supA[Supplier] --Delivery--> warehouseA[Raw Material Store]
    warehouseA -- Withdrawal Kanban --> pressOp[Press Operation]
    pressOp -- Production Kanban --> pressOp
    pressOp -- Withdrawal Kanban --> weldOp[Welding Operation]
    weldOp -- Production Kanban --> weldOp
    weldOp -- Withdrawal Kanban --> assyOp[Assembly Operation]
    assyOp -- Production Kanban --> assyOp
    assyOp -- Withdrawal Kanban --> ship[Customer Shipping Dock]
    ship -. demand signal .-> assyOp
    pressOp -. WIP Store B .-> weldOp
    weldOp -. WIP Store C .-> assyOp
```

**Interpretation:**
- Solid arrows: Material/conveyance Kanban flow (downstream demand pulls upstream production).
- Dotted arrows: In-process stores placed between operations as buffer (controlled by Kanban count).
- Each WIP store is small — the cell cannot overproduce because the next downstream withdrawal Kanban must come back before new production begins.

---

## 4.2 Kanban Card Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Downstream process needs parts] --> checkA{Empty container in WIP store?}
    checkA -- Yes --> detA[Detach Withdrawal Kanban from container]
    detA --> moveA[Send empty container upstream with Withdrawal Kanban]
    moveA --> upPick[Upstream process picks Withdrawal Kanban]
    upPick --> prodA[Upstream produces quantity on Production Kanban]
    prodA --> fillA[Fill container with new parts]
    fillA --> moveDown[Move full container downstream]
    moveDown --> storeA[Place in WIP store with Production Kanban attached]
    storeA --> nextWait[Downstream waits for its turn to withdraw]
    nextWait --> startA
    checkA -- No --> idleA[Downstream continues working on current container]
```

---

## 4.3 Group Technology — Classification and Coding Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA[Engineering Drawings Database] --> extA[Extract Design Features]
    extA --> matA[Material, Tolerance, Finish]
    extA --> geomA[Geometry: L/D, bore, slots, threads]
    geomA --> codeA[Apply Opitz 9-digit code]
    matA --> codeA
    codeA --> familyA[Group codes by similarity in first 5 digits]
    familyA --> cellA[Form Manufacturing Cell]
    familyA --> retA[Standard Process Plan Retrieval]
    cellA --> camA[NC Program Library per Family]
    retA --> camA
    camA --> outputA[CAD/CAM Production Output]
```

---

## 4.4 Production Flow Analysis — Matrix Clustering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawM[Raw Part-Machine Matrix] --> sortR[Sort rows to group identical patterns]
    sortR --> sortC[Sort columns to align with row clusters]
    sortC --> diagA[Diagonal blocks emerge]
    diagA --> famA[Part Family Identification]
    diagA --> cellA[Machine Cell Identification]
    famA --> effA[Compute Grouping Efficiency eta_g]
    cellA --> effA
    effA --> decideA{eta_g acceptable?}
    decideA -- Yes --> deployA[Deploy Cellular Layout]
    decideA -- No --> reSort[Recluster and re-evaluate]
    reSort --> sortR
```

---

## 4.5 Integrated JIT + GT — Cellular Manufacturing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    custA[Customer Order] --> mrA[Level Production Schedule]
    mrA --> cell1A[Cell 1: Shaft Family]
    mrA --> cell2A[Cell 2: Housing Family]
    mrA --> cell3A[Cell 3: Bracket Family]
    cell1A --> kanban1A[Kanban Control]
    cell2A --> kanban1A
    cell3A --> kanban1A
    kanban1A --> suppA[Supplier JIT Delivery]
    suppA --> cell1A
    suppA --> cell2A
    suppA --> cell3A
    cell1A --> shipA[Finished Goods]
    cell2A --> shipA
    cell3A --> shipA
    shipA --> custA
```

**Caption:** This block-level architecture shows how GT (forming the cells by part family) and JIT (controlling flow with Kanban) operate together — the foundation of **Lean Cellular Manufacturing**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define Just-In-Time (JIT) production. List the seven wastes (muda) that JIT aims to eliminate.**

**Model Answer:**

Just-In-Time (JIT) is a Japanese production philosophy originating from the Toyota Production System, in which each operation produces **only the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time**. It is fundamentally a **pull-based system** driven by actual customer demand rather than forecasts.

The **seven wastes (muda)** that JIT targets are:
1. Overproduction
2. Waiting
3. Transportation
4. Over-processing
5. Inventory
6. Motion
7. Defects (rework/scrap)

> [!NOTE]
> Sometimes the 8th waste — **underutilized talent (human potential)** — is also included in modern lean literature.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is Group Technology (GT)? Explain the concept of a part family with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

Group Technology (GT) is a manufacturing strategy in which parts are classified into **part families** based on similarities in design features (shape, size, material) and/or manufacturing processes (machines, tooling, sequence). By identifying these families, similar parts can be processed together, leading to reduced setup time, lower WIP, and easier process planning.

**Example:** In an automotive factory, all **shafts** (drive shaft, camshaft, countershaft) may be grouped into one family because they:
- Share rotational geometry (L/D > 1).
- Use similar machines (lathe, grinder, heat treatment).
- Use similar tooling and fixtures.

These similarities allow the factory to dedicate a **manufacturing cell** with a lathe, a cylindrical grinder, and a heat treatment furnace specifically for the shaft family.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Define Kanban. Explain the different types of Kanban and list the five rules of Kanban operation.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] A production cell requires 600 components per day. The lead time for replenishment is 1.2 days, the container size is 40 components, and the safety stock factor is 0.20. Calculate the number of Kanban cards. If the safety factor is reduced to 0.10 (under SMED improvements), how many cards are now needed?

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a):

**Definition:**
A **Kanban** (Japanese for "signal card" or "visible record") is a control device used in a pull-based production system to authorize the production or movement of parts between processes. It is the operational heartbeat of the JIT system and ensures that no upstream process produces anything without a downstream demand signal.

**Types of Kanban:**

| Type | Alternative Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Production Kanban (P-kanban)** | Make-Kanban, In-process Kanban | Authorizes the upstream (preceding) process to produce a fixed quantity of parts |
| **Withdrawal Kanban (W-kanban)** | Conveyance Kanban, Move-Kanban | Authorizes the downstream (succeeding) process to withdraw a container of parts from the upstream process |
| **Supplier Kanban** | External Kanban | Authorizes supplier delivery of parts to the receiving dock |
| **Signal Kanban** | Production-Ordering Kanban | Used in single-piece flow to signal production start (no card attached to container) |

**Five Rules of Kanban Operation (Toyota's rules):**

1. **Rule 1 — Withdrawal by Need:** The downstream process must withdraw only the quantity it needs from the upstream process.
2. **Rule 2 — Production to Withdrawal:** The upstream process must produce only the quantity withdrawn by the downstream process.
3. **Rule 3 — Zero Defects:** Defective items must never be passed to the next process; they trigger immediate quality control (Jidoka).
4. **Rule 4 — Card Discipline:** A Kanban must always accompany the parts, and the number of cards must be strictly controlled.
5. **Rule 5 — Pull Only by Kanban:** No production or withdrawal is allowed without a corresponding Kanban signal.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b):

**Given Data:**
- Demand rate $D = 600$ components/day
- Lead time $L = 1.2$ days
- Container size $S = 40$ components/container
- Case 1: Safety factor $C_1 = 0.20$
- Case 2: Safety factor $C_2 = 0.10$

**Formula:**

$$
N \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{D \times L \times (1 + C)}{S} \right\rceil
$$

**Case 1 Calculation ($C_1 = 0.20$):**

$$
N_1 \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{600 \times 1.2 \times (1 + 0.20)}{40} \right\rceil
$$

Step by step:

$$
600 \times 1.2 \;=\; 720
$$

$$
720 \times 1.20 \;=\; 864
$$

$$
\frac{864}{40} \;=\; 21.6
$$

$$
N_1 \;=\; \lceil 21.6 \rceil \;=\; 22 \text{ Kanban cards}
$$

**Case 2 Calculation ($C_2 = 0.10$):**

$$
N_2 \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{600 \times 1.2 \times (1 + 0.10)}{40} \right\rceil
$$

Step by step:

$$
720 \times 1.10 \;=\; 792
$$

$$
\frac{792}{40} \;=\; 19.8
$$

$$
N_2 \;=\; \lceil 19.8 \rceil \;=\; 20 \text{ Kanban cards}
$$

**Conclusion:**
- With $C = 0.20$, **22 Kanban cards** are required.
- With $C = 0.10$ (after SMED/reliability improvements), only **20 Kanban cards** are needed.
- This represents a **9.1% reduction** in the number of Kanbans — directly lowering WIP inventory and improving the pull system efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> The reduction in safety factor reflects a **more reliable, less variable** production system — the JIT ideal of approaching zero safety stock.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the Opitz classification and coding system for Group Technology. Briefly describe the meaning of the first five digits.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] The following part-machine incidence matrix is given for 7 parts and 6 machines. Perform Production Flow Analysis (PFA) by reordering the matrix to identify part families and machine cells, and compute the grouping efficiency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \begin{array}{c|cccccc}
   & M1 & M2 & M3 & M4 & M5 & M6 \\
\hline
P1 & 1  & 0  & 0  & 0  & 1  & 0 \\
P2 & 0  & 1  & 1  & 0  & 0  & 0 \\
P3 & 1  & 0  & 0  & 1  & 0  & 0 \\
P4 & 0  & 1  & 0  & 0  & 0  & 1 \\
P5 & 0  & 0  & 1  & 0  & 0  & 0 \\
P6 & 0  & 1  & 0  & 0  & 0  & 1 \\
P7 & 1  & 0  & 0  & 0  & 1  & 0 \\
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a):

**Opitz Coding System — Overview:**

The **Opitz system**, developed by Prof. H. Opitz of Germany in the 1960s, is the most widely used GT classification system globally. It assigns each part a **9-digit numeric code** divided into two logical halves: the first five digits describe the **design (form) features** of the part, while the last four digits describe **auxiliary manufacturing information** (material, size, tolerance, finish).

**Structure of the 9-Digit Opitz Code:**

| Digit(s) | Category | Information Conveyed |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Family class | Rotational (0), Flat (1), Cast (2), Special (3) |
| 2 | External shape | Length-to-diameter ratio (rotational parts) or length-to-width ratio (flat parts) |
| 3 | Internal shape / bore | Presence and type of bore, slots, cavities |
| 4 | Plane / auxiliary surfaces | Number and orientation of flat surfaces, grooves |
| 5 | Threads / toothing | External/internal thread, gear teeth, splines |
| 6 | Material | Cast iron, steel, non-ferrous, plastic, etc. |
| 7 | Largest dimension | Length / diameter size class (1–9) |
| 8 | Tolerance class | IT grade (1–9) |
| 9 | Surface finish | Rough, turned, ground, polished, etc. |

**Detailed Meaning of Digits 1–5:**

- **Digit 1 (Form class):** Categorizes the part as rotational (e.g., shafts, gears), flat (e.g., plates, brackets), or cast/irregular (e.g., housing castings).
- **Digit 2 (External form):** For rotational parts, classifies by length-to-diameter (L/D) ratio: short, medium, long, very long. For flat parts, by length-to-width.
- **Digit 3 (Internal form):** Identifies whether the part has internal features such as bores, holes, slots, or stepped features.
- **Digit 4 (Plane/auxiliary surfaces):** Counts and qualifies the presence of planar surfaces, grooves, or slots.
- **Digit 5 (Threads/toothing):** Specifies whether the part has external threads, internal threads, gear teeth, splines, or none.

**Significance:**
- The first 5 digits define the **part family** for grouping purposes.
- The last 4 digits refine the family for process planning and tooling decisions.
- Two parts with the same Opitz code can be processed in the same manufacturing cell with minimal setup change.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b):

**Step 1: Count the 1s in each row and column to identify patterns.**

Row sums: P1=2, P2=2, P3=2, P4=2, P5=1, P6=2, P7=2
Column sums: M1=3, M2=3, M3=2, M4=1, M5=2, M6=2

**Step 2: Reorder by sorting rows.**

Identify parts with the same machine pattern:
- P1 and P7: {M1, M5} — identical.
- P4 and P6: {M2, M6} — identical.
- P2: {M2, M3}
- P3: {M1, M4}
- P5: {M3} — singleton.

**Step 3: Reorder columns to align with the new row order.**

Group M1, M5, M4 (for P1, P7, P3) and M2, M6, M3 (for P4, P6, P2, P5). The reordered matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{reordered}} &= \begin{array}{c|cccccc}
   & M1 & M5 & M4 & M2 & M6 & M3 \\
\hline
P1 & 1  & 1  & 0  & 0  & 0  & 0 \\
P7 & 1  & 1  & 0  & 0  & 0  & 0 \\
P3 & 1  & 0  & 1  & 0  & 0  & 0 \\
\hline
P4 & 0  & 0  & 0  & 1  & 1  & 0 \\
P6 & 0  & 0  & 0  & 1  & 1  & 0 \\
P2 & 0  & 0  & 0  & 1  & 0  & 1 \\
P5 & 0  & 0  & 0  & 0  & 0  & 1 \\
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Identify part families and machine cells.**

- **Family 1:** Parts {P1, P7, P3} processed on machines {M1, M5, M4}.
- **Family 2:** Parts {P4, P6, P2, P5} processed on machines {M2, M6, M3}.

**Step 5: Count exceptions.**

Total 1s in the matrix: $2+2+2+2+1+2+2 = 13$.
Total cells in $7\times 6$ matrix: 42.
Zeros in cell blocks: Family 1 block has one 0 (P3-M5); both other cell blocks are fully 1s.
Off-diagonal block exceptions: Family 1 vs {M2, M6, M3} = $3 \times 3 = 9$ zeros; Family 2 vs {M1, M5, M4} = $4 \times 3 = 12$ zeros.

Total observed exceptions: $e_o = 1 + 9 + 12 = 22$.

**Step 6: Compute desired exceptions.**

For an ideal grouping with $f=2$ families in a $7\times 6$ matrix:

$$
e_d \;=\; (m - f) \times n \;+\; (f - 1) \times \text{min(row lengths)}
$$

$$
e_d \;=\; (6 - 2) \times 7 \;+\; (2 - 1) \times 3 \;=\; 28 + 3 \;=\; 31
$$

**Step 7: Grouping efficiency.**

$$
\eta_g \;=\; \frac{e_d - e_o}{e_d} \;=\; \frac{31 - 22}{31} \;=\; \frac{9}{31} \;\approx\; 0.290 \;=\; 29.0\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Two part families and two machine cells are identified. A grouping efficiency of ~29% indicates that the clustering is reasonable but not perfect (the P3-M5 zero is a bottleneck operation that may require inter-cell movement).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Kanban Calculation:** Students forget to apply the **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ and write fractional Kanbans (e.g., "8.8 Kanban cards" — this loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Formula Units:** Mixing minutes and days in $L$ is a common error. Ensure $D$, $L$, and $S$ are dimensionally consistent.
> 3. **Opitz Code:** Confusing the first 5 digits (form) with the last 4 (auxiliary) is a recurring mistake. KTU specifically tests digit-by-digit meaning.
> 4. **PFA Matrix:** Students often fail to **reorder both rows and columns**. A diagonal-block structure must be obtained before counting exceptions.
> 5. **Grouping Efficiency Formula:** Mixing up $e_d$ and $e_o$ — remember: $e_d$ is the *desired* (ideal) exceptions, $e_o$ is the *observed* exceptions in the actual reordered matrix.
> 6. **JIT vs. Push:** Confusing pull (downstream triggers upstream) with push (forecast triggers upstream). This is a conceptual loss of 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — KTU Module 4: JIT and GT**

**JIT — Core Recall:**
- ☐ JIT = *Pull-based, zero-inventory, demand-driven* production.
- ☐ Origin: Toyota Production System (Taiichi Ohno).
- ☐ Two pillars: **JIT (flow)** + **Jidoka (built-in quality)**.
- ☐ **Kanban** = signal card (Production + Withdrawal types).
- ☐ **5 Kanban rules:** withdraw by need, produce to withdrawal, no defects pass, card discipline, no production without Kanban.
- ☐ **Kanban formula:** $N = \lceil (D \times L \times (1+C)) / S \rceil$ — **always round up**.
- ☐ **Takt time:** $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D_{demand}$ — sets the heartbeat of production.
- ☐ **7 Wastes (Muda):** Overproduction, Waiting, Transport, Over-processing, Inventory, Motion, Defects.
- ☐ **SMED** (Single-Minute Exchange of Die) is the JIT tool for reducing setup time.

**GT — Core Recall:**
- ☐ GT = classification & coding of parts into **part families**.
- ☐ Three pillars: **Classification, Coding, Cellular Manufacturing**.
- ☐ **Opitz code = 9 digits** (5 form + 4 auxiliary).
- ☐ Digit 1: Family (0=Rotational, 1=Flat, 2=Cast, 3=Special).
- ☐ Digits 2–5: Form features (L/D, bore, planes, threads).
- ☐ Digits 6–9: Material, size, tolerance, finish.
- ☐ **PFA** uses part-machine matrix; reorder rows + columns → diagonal blocks = cells.
- ☐ **Grouping efficiency:** $\eta_g = (e_d - e_o) / e_d$.
- ☐ **80/20 Pareto rule:** 80% volume comes from 20% of parts.
- ☐ GT benefits: standardized design, faster process planning, NC code reuse, reduced setup, foundation for FMS and Lean Cells.

**Integration Concept:**
- ☐ **JIT + GT = Cellular / Lean Manufacturing.**
- ☐ GT forms the cells (by family), JIT controls the flow (by Kanban).
- ☐ This combination is the **modern lean production backbone** of CAM-enabled factories.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Reality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBAUT504 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Virtual Reality # Module 4 — Virtual Reality (VR) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Virtual Reality (VR) is a computer-generated, three-dimensional, interactive environment that simulates a realistic or non-realistic experience, in which the user is immersed using specialized hardware devices (such as Head-Mounted Displays, data gloves, and motion trackers) and software systems that synchronize sensory inputs (visual, auditory, haptic, and olfactory) in real-time to create the perception of being physically present within a simulated world.* ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you put on a special pair of goggles while sitting in your drawing room. Suddenly, the floor beneath your feet is gone — you are now standing inside the engine bay of a car that has not yet been built. You can walk around the crankshaft, lean down to inspect a piston, push a bolt with your finger, and hear the click of the starter motor. None of this physically exists; the goggle lenses are showing you two slightly different pictures (one per eye), your position tracker is updating as you tilt your head, and a software engine is rendering the geometry of a CAD model in real time. This is **Virtual Reality** — a believable, immersive simulation generated entirely by a computer. In the context of **CAD/CAM**, VR is the natural progression after 3D modeling (CAD), numerical simulation (CAE), and CNC programming (CAM). Instead of staring at a flat monitor showing a 3D model, an engineer **steps inside** the model — to review designs, plan manufacturing layouts, train operators on robotic cells, or visualize factory floor ergonomics *before* any metal is cut. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from Related Technologies** > > | Acronym | Full Form | What the User Sees | > |---|---|---| > | **VR** | Virtual Reality | A fully computer-generated world; the real world is hidden | > | **AR** | Augmented Reality | Computer graphics overlaid on the real world | > | **MR** | Mixed Reality | Real and virtual objects interact in real time | > | **XR** | Extended Reality | Umbrella term covering VR, AR, and MR | ### Standard VR Metrics (KTU Board Favourite) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical VR Performance Metrics — Memorize These** > > - **Frame Rate (Refresh Rate):** **90 Hz minimum**, ideally **120 Hz**, to prevent motion sickness. > - **Field of View (FOV):** **90°–120°** horizontal per eye for immersion (human binocular FOV ≈ 200°). > - **Motion-to-Photon Latency:** **≤ 20 milliseconds** (ideally **≤ 11 ms**) — the delay between user head movement and the new image appearing on the display. > - **Inter-Pupillary Distance (IPD):** **54–74 mm** (average human ≈ 63 mm) — must match the user's eye spacing for stereo comfort. > - **Degrees of Freedom (DoF):** > - **3 DoF** — only rotational tracking (yaw, pitch, roll). e.g., Google Cardboard. > - **6 DoF** — rotational + translational tracking (X, Y, Z + yaw, pitch, roll). e.g., Meta Quest, HTC Vive. > - **Pixel Density (PPD):** **Pixels Per Degree** ≥ 15 for "Retina-level" clarity in VR HMDs. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Architecture of a VR System ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of a VR System A Virtual Reality system is built from **four tightly-coupled subsystems**. The KTU board frequently asks students to "list and explain the components of a VR system" — this is the canonical answer. ### Pillar 1 — **Computer / Rendering Engine (The Brain)** This is the high-performance workstation or gaming PC/console that: - Stores the 3D CAD model (typically in formats like **STEP, IGES, JT, or glTF**). - Runs the **VR engine software** (Unity, Unreal Engine, OpenXR, WebXR). - Performs **stereoscopic rendering** — rendering the scene **twice per frame**, once for each eye with a slight horizontal offset equal to the IPD. - Calculates new viewpoints every frame (typically 90–120 times per second). ### Pillar 2 — **Display Hardware (The Eyes)** The visual output devices: - **Head-Mounted Display (HMD):** Two micro-displays (one per eye), with **Fresnel** or **pancake** lenses to focus the image and provide a wide FOV. - **Cave Automatic Virtual Environment (CAVE):** A room-sized cube where projections are cast on the walls and floor. - **Stereo Glasses** + **Workbench** (lower-cost CAD-review setup). - **Autostereoscopic Displays:** "Glasses-free" 3D screens using lenticular lenses. ### Pillar 3 — **Tracking & Input Devices (The Nervous System)** Sensors that capture user motion and convert it into digital input: - **Outside-In Tracking:** External cameras/lighthouses detect markers on the HMD/controllers (e.g., HTC Vive base stations). - **Inside-Out Tracking:** Cameras on the HMD itself observe the environment (e.g., Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro). - **Inertial Measurement Units (IMU):** Combine a 3-axis **gyroscope**, **accelerometer**, and **magnetometer** to estimate orientation at 1000 Hz. - **Data Gloves:** Measure finger flexion, hand position, and gesture recognition. - **Haptic Devices:** Force-feedback joysticks, exoskeletons, or ultrasonic mid-air haptics. - **Eye Tracking:** Cameras that detect pupil direction for **foveated rendering**. ### Pillar 4 — **Software / VR Engine (The Soul)** The software stack: - **Modeling software:** CATIA, NX, SolidWorks, Blender (import CAD geometry). - **VR engine:** Unity / Unreal Engine (real-time rendering, physics, scripting). - **Middleware:** SteamVR, OpenXR (hardware abstraction layer). - **Networking:** Multi-user VR collaboration (e.g., NVIDIA Omniverse). ## 2.2 The VR Pipeline — How a Frame Reaches Your Eye Every **11 to 16.67 milliseconds**, the following sequence executes: 1. **Sensor Read:** IMU + trackers sample the user's head position/orientation at 1000 Hz. 2. **Pose Prediction:** Algorithm extrapolates the head pose to the exact moment the pixels will be displayed (compensating for tracking & rendering delay). 3. **View Matrix Update:** A new camera transform matrix is computed for **each eye** (left and right viewpoints separated by IPD). 4. **Scene Rendering:** GPU renders the scene twice (once per eye) using the updated view matrices — this is **stereoscopic rendering**. 5. **Distortion Correction:** The rendered image is pre-distorted (barrel distortion) so that the **Fresnel lenses** optically correct it back into a rectilinear image. 6. **Time-Warp / Asynchronous Reprojection:** If the GPU misses a frame, the previous frame is re-projected using the *latest* head pose, hiding judder. 7. **Display Refresh:** The HMD panels scan out the final image to the user's retina. ## 2.3 The Taxonomy of Virtual Reality > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "classify VR systems," use the standard immersion-based taxonomy below. | Type | Immersion Level | Typical Hardware | Cost Range (INR) | CAD/CAM Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Non-Immersive** | Low | Desktop monitor, mouse | ₹ 0 – 50 K | Standard CAD review on a 3D monitor | | **Fish Tank VR** | Low-Medium | Stereo monitor + shutter glasses | ₹ 50 K – 2 L | Solo engineering design review | | **Semi-Immersive** | Medium | Large projection screen / curved monitor | ₹ 2 L – 10 L | Team design reviews, digital mock-up (DMU) | | **Fully Immersive** | High | HMD + motion trackers + haptics | ₹ 1 L – 20 L+ | Factory layout planning, operator training | | **CAVE** | Highest | Room-sized multi-projection cube | ₹ 50 L – 5 Cr | Automotive/aerospace full-scale mock-ups | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — VR Mathematics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear regularly in Part B numerical problems.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Where Used | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Stereoscopic Parallax Angle** | $\theta = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2D}\right)$ | Stereo depth perception; $d$ = IPD, $D$ = object distance | | 2 | **Field of View (FOV)** | $\text{FOV} = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right)$ | Lens & display design; $w$ = screen width, $f$ = focal length | | 3 | **Required Frame Time** | $T_f = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{refresh}}}$ | Latency budget (90 Hz → $T_f \approx 11.11$ ms) | | 4 | **Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget** | $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}}$ | Head-tracking pipeline design | | 5 | **Refresh Rate from Frame Time** | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_f}$ | Reverse calculation for "find Hz" questions | | 6 | **Angular Resolution (PPD)** | $\text{PPD} = \dfrac{\text{horizontal pixels}}{\text{horizontal FOV (deg)}}$ | Display quality assessment | | 7 | **3-DoF vs 6-DoF Pose Vector** | $\mathbf{p}_{6\text{DoF}} = [x, y, z, \psi, \theta, \phi]^T$ | State vector in tracker fusion | | 8 | **Tracking Update Rate (HMD)** | $f_{\text{IMU}} \approx 1000\,\text{Hz}$ | Pose prediction frequency | ### Real-World Engineering Utility In modern automotive companies (e.g., Tata Motors, Mahindra, Mercedes-Benz), entire factories are first built in **VR** before any concrete is poured. Engineers wearing HMDs walk through the digital twin of the assembly line, check for ergonomic issues, verify that robots have adequate reach, and validate that a human worker can safely access every maintenance point. This **Virtual Commissioning** reduces plant commissioning time by **30–60 %** and saves crores of rupees in rework. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The derivations below are **board-exam favourites**. Practice writing each line on paper until you can reproduce them in under 4 minutes. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View (FOV) of a VR Lens **Problem Setup:** A VR headset uses a display panel of horizontal width $w$ placed at a focal distance $f$ behind a thin convex lens. Derive the horizontal Field of View captured by one eye. **Step 1 — Geometry of the Imaging Triangle** The lens forms a virtual image. The eye is positioned at the focal point. Light from the extreme left edge of the panel enters the eye at half-angle $\alpha$ measured from the optical axis. $$ \tan(\alpha) \;=\; \dfrac{w/2}{f} $$ **Step 2 — Total Field of View** The total horizontal FOV spans from $-\alpha$ on the left to $+\alpha$ on the right of the optical axis. $$ \text{FOV} \;=\; 2\alpha \;=\; 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation Example** > *Given:* Panel width $w = 64\,\text{mm}$, focal length $f = 35\,\text{mm}$. > > *Find:* Horizontal FOV in degrees. $$ \text{FOV} \;=\; 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{64}{2 \times 35}\right) \;=\; 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{64}{70}\right) $$ $$ \text{FOV} \;=\; 2\arctan(0.9143) \;=\; 2 \times 42.43^\circ \;=\; \mathbf{84.86^\circ} $$ **Interpretation:** A 64 mm display with a 35 mm lens yields roughly 85° horizontal FOV — typical of older-generation VR headsets like the Oculus Rift CV1. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Refresh Rate (Hz) from Frame Time **Problem Setup:** A VR engine is set to render the scene twice per frame (one image per eye). If each rendered frame takes **5.5 ms** to complete, calculate the effective per-eye refresh rate in Hz. **Step 1 — Single Stereo Frame Time** $$ T_{\text{stereo}} \;=\; 2 \times T_{\text{per-eye}} \;=\; 2 \times 5.5\,\text{ms} \;=\; 11.0\,\text{ms} $$ **Step 2 — Convert to Frequency** $$ f_{\text{refresh}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{stereo}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{11.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}} $$ $$ f_{\text{refresh}} \;=\; \dfrac{1000}{11.0} \;\approx\; \mathbf{90.91\,\text{Hz}} $$ **Interpretation:** This satisfies the **> 90 Hz** VR comfort threshold — the headset will not induce simulator sickness under normal head motion. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget **Problem Setup:** A VR pipeline has the following delays: IMU sensor read = **2 ms**, pose prediction = **1 ms**, GPU rendering = **6 ms**, display scan-out = **3 ms**. Compute the total motion-to-photon latency and verify whether it meets the comfort threshold. **Step 1 — Sum All Stage Delays** $$ L_{\text{total}} \;=\; L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{predict}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}} $$ $$ L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 2 + 1 + 6 + 3 \;=\; \mathbf{12\,\text{ms}} $$ **Step 2 — Compare Against Threshold** $$ 12\,\text{ms} \;<\; 20\,\text{ms (VR comfort threshold)} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 3 — Safety Margin Calculation** $$ \text{Margin} \;=\; \dfrac{20 - 12}{20} \times 100\% \;=\; 40\% $$ **Interpretation:** With 8 ms of headroom, the headset can absorb sudden GPU load spikes (e.g., complex scene changes) without breaking the immersion comfort budget. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stereoscopic Parallax & Apparent Depth **Problem Setup:** Two cameras (left and right eye) are separated by the average human IPD, $d = 63\,\text{mm}$. An object is located $D = 2\,\text{m}$ in front of the user. Compute the **binocular disparity angle** $\theta$. **Step 1 — Half-Angle Construction** The object appears at angle $\theta/2$ from each eye relative to the straight-ahead direction. $$ \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{d/2}{D} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{2D} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for Total Disparity** $$ \theta \;=\; 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{2D}\right) \;=\; 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.063}{2 \times 2}\right) $$ $$ \theta \;=\; 2\arctan(0.01575) \;=\; 2 \times 0.9023^\circ \;=\; \mathbf{1.8046^\circ} $$ **Step 3 — Apparent Depth at Near Distance** > *Compare:* Object moved to $D = 0.5\,\text{m}$. $$ \theta_{\text{near}} \;=\; 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.063}{1.0}\right) \;=\; 2\arctan(0.063) \;\approx\; \mathbf{7.21^\circ} $$ **Interpretation:** As an object comes closer, the disparity angle increases, and the brain interprets it as "nearer." When $\theta$ exceeds about **12°**, the human visual system can no longer fuse the two images → double vision (diplopia). This sets the **near clipping plane** in VR (typically 0.3 – 0.5 m). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tracking Technologies > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 board exams often include a "Compare the following VR tracking methods" sub-question (7 marks).** Memorize this table. | Parameter | **Outside-In (Lighthouse)** | **Inside-Out (SLAM)** | **Electromagnetic** | **Optical Marker (Vicon)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Reference Frame** | External base stations | World anchors on headset | Magnetic field generator | External IR cameras | | **Workspace** | Bounded by base-station FOV | Unlimited (room-scale) | Distortion near metal | Large, requires clear sightlines | | **Accuracy** | Sub-millimetre | 2–5 mm | ~1 mm; degrades near steel | Sub-millimetre | | **Latency** | ~2–4 ms | ~5–10 ms | ~5–8 ms | ~3–5 ms | | **Cost (INR)** | Medium (₹ 50 K – 3 L) | Low (built into HMD) | Medium (₹ 1 – 5 L) | Very High (₹ 50 L+) | | **Metal Interference** | None | None | **Severe** | None | | **CAD/CAM Use** | Precision assembly review | Factory walkthroughs | Surgical & robotic training | Motion-capture for ergonomic studies | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 VR System — Block-Level Functional Architecture The Mermaid diagram below maps every hardware and software component of a generic VR system used in a CAD/CAM engineering workflow, and shows how information flows between them. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CAD Model
STEP or IGES] --> B[VR Engine
Unity or Unreal] B --> C[Scene Graph
Geometry plus Materials] C --> D[Stereoscopic Renderer
90 to 120 Hz] G[Tracking Sensors
IMU 1000 Hz] --> H[Pose Predictor] H --> D D --> E[Lens Distortion
Correction] E --> F[HMD Display
Left and Right Panels] F --> I[User Eyes
Retinal Fusion] J[Data Glove] --> K[Haptic Processor] K --> B L[Force Feedback
Controller] --> K I --> M[User Brain
Perceived 3D World] K --> N[User Hand
Tactile Sensation] ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **left column** (A → B → C → D) is the software pipeline that builds and renders the synthetic world. - The **middle column** (G → H → D) shows the real-time tracking loop that constantly updates the user's viewpoint. - The **bottom row** (J → K → L) is the input/haptic loop — where the user reaches into and touches the virtual world. - The **right column** (F → I → M) is where pixels become perception. --- ## 4.2 The VR Frame-Render Loop — Timing Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Frame N S1[Sensor Read
2 ms] --> S2[Pose Predict
1 ms] S2 --> S3[GPU Render
6 ms] S3 --> S4[Lens Distort
1 ms] S4 --> S5[Display Scan
3 ms] end S5 --> S6[User Sees Frame N
at t = 13 ms] subgraph Frame N plus 1 T1[Sensor Read
2 ms] --> T2[Pose Predict
1 ms] T2 --> T3[GPU Render
6 ms] T3 --> T4[Lens Distort
1 ms] T4 --> T5[Display Scan
3 ms] end T5 --> S7[User Sees Frame N plus 1
at t = 26 ms] S6 --> S7 ``` **Reading the Timing Diagram:** - Each **frame** is a self-contained pipeline that must complete within **~13 ms** for a 75 Hz refresh, or **~11 ms** for 90 Hz. - The next frame (N+1) starts reading sensors **before** frame N finishes displaying — this is **pipelined parallelism**. - If frame N+1's render takes longer than 11 ms, the system triggers **Asynchronous Spacewarp (ASW)** or **Motion Smoothing** to interpolate and avoid judder. --- ## 4.3 Immersion Pyramid — Types of VR Systems ```mermaid flowchart TB A0[Desktop Monitor
Non Immersive VR] --> A1[Fish Tank VR
Shutter Glasses] A1 --> A2[Powerwall or Workbench
Semi Immersive] A2 --> A3[HMD plus Trackers
Fully Immersive] A3 --> A4[CAVE Room
Highest Immersion] style A0 fill:#ffe0e0 style A1 fill:#ffd0b0 style A2 fill:#ffffb0 style A3 fill:#b0ffc0 style A4 fill:#b0d0ff ``` **Reading the Pyramid:** As we move **upwards**, immersion increases (and so does the capital cost). KTU questions on "classify VR" usually expect this 5-level hierarchy. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define Virtual Reality. List any four applications of VR in manufacturing.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Virtual Reality (VR) is a computer-generated, three-dimensional, interactive simulation in which the user is immersed via specialized hardware (HMD, gloves, trackers) and perceives the synthetic environment as if physically present. **Four Applications in Manufacturing:** 1. **Digital Mock-Up (DMU) Review** — Engineers inspect full-scale 3D CAD assemblies (e.g., a complete car) before physical prototyping. 2. **Virtual Commissioning** — Simulating and validating PLC / robotic programs on a digital twin of the factory before real installation. 3. **Operator Training** — Safe training on hazardous equipment (welding, forging, CNC tending) without material wastage or injury risk. 4. **Ergonomic Analysis** — Validating that human operators can reach every control/bolt on the production line. 5. *(Any other valid application — layout planning, maintenance training, sales visualization — also accepted.)* --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR).** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **Virtual Reality (VR)** | **Augmented Reality (AR)** | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Fully computer-generated 3D environment | Computer graphics overlaid on the real world | | **Real World Visibility** | Completely hidden (occluded by HMD) | Visible through camera or transparent display | | **Hardware** | HMD (opaque lenses), trackers, gloves | Smartphone, AR glasses (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens) | | **User Goal** | Immersion in a synthetic world | Enhance perception of the real world | | **Manufacturing Use** | Factory walkthroughs, VR training | Assembly guidance overlays on real parts | | **Example Device** | Meta Quest 3, HTC Vive Pro | Microsoft HoloLens 2, Apple Vision Pro (passthrough) | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### ❖ Question A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four main components** of a Virtual Reality system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each component in creating the immersive experience. **(b) [7 Marks]** A VR headset uses a display of horizontal width **70 mm** and a Fresnel lens of focal length **38 mm**. Calculate: 1. The horizontal Field of View (FOV) in degrees. 2. The required frame time if the headset must operate at **90 Hz** refresh rate. 3. Comment on whether this FOV is suitable for **fully immersive** VR. #### Model Solution — Part (a) The four components of a VR system are: 1. **Rendering Computer / VR Engine:** The high-performance workstation that loads the CAD model, maintains the scene graph, runs the physics simulator, and renders the scene at 90–120 Hz. It uses a multi-core CPU and a high-end GPU (e.g., NVIDIA RTX series) for **stereoscopic rendering** — generating two slightly offset images per frame. 2. **Display Hardware:** The output devices — primarily the **Head-Mounted Display (HMD)** containing two micro-displays (LCD/OLED) and Fresnel/pancake lenses. Each lens creates a virtual image at optical infinity, allowing the eye to focus comfortably while the brain perceives depth from binocular disparity. 3. **Tracking & Input Devices:** Sensors that capture user motion in real time. - **IMU (gyro + accelerometer + magnetometer)** at 1000 Hz gives orientation. - **Outside-In or Inside-Out cameras** track position in 6 DoF. - **Data gloves, hand controllers, and force-feedback devices** capture hand gestures and return haptic sensations. 4. **VR Software Stack:** The application software — typically **Unity** or **Unreal Engine** — that integrates CAD geometry, runs interaction scripts, applies physics, and communicates with hardware via **OpenXR / SteamVR** middleware. **Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1):** *(Student must draw the flow: CAD Model → VR Engine → Stereoscopic Renderer → Lens Correction → HMD → User Eyes, plus the tracking loop and haptic feedback loop.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Calculate the horizontal FOV:** $$ \text{FOV} = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2f}\right) = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{70}{2 \times 38}\right) = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{70}{76}\right) $$ $$ \text{FOV} = 2\arctan(0.9211) = 2 \times 42.62^\circ = \mathbf{85.24^\circ} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the required frame time at 90 Hz:** $$ T_f = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{90}\,\text{s} = 0.01111\,\text{s} = \mathbf{11.11\,\text{ms}} $$ **Step 3 — Suitability comment:** A horizontal FOV of **85.24°** falls in the **mid-range** of VR headsets. While it is sufficient for **semi-immersive** and most **fully-immersive** applications, it is below the **100°–120°** range preferred for **best-in-class immersion**. To achieve a true 110° FOV with the same focal length, the display width must increase to: $$ w = 2f \tan(55^\circ) = 2 \times 38 \times 1.4281 \approx \mathbf{108.5\,\text{mm}} $$ Hence, this headset is **acceptable for general VR** but **not optimal** for premium fully-immersive use. --- ### ❖ Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a flowchart, describe the **VR rendering pipeline** for a single frame. Explain the importance of **motion-to-photon latency** and state the threshold value required for comfortable VR. **(b) [7 Marks]** A factory is implementing a **VR-based operator training** system for a robotic welding cell. Discuss: 1. **Hardware selection** (HMD type, tracking method, haptic devices). 2. **Software stack** (CAD format, engine, middleware). 3. **Two specific training scenarios** the system should support. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **VR Rendering Pipeline (Single Frame):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sensor Read at 1000 Hz] --> B[Pose Prediction] B --> C[Stereo View Matrix per Eye] C --> D[GPU Scene Render at 90 Hz] D --> E[Lens Distortion Correction] E --> F[Display Scan-Out] F --> G[User Retina Fusion] ``` **Step-by-Step Explanation:** 1. **Sensor Read:** The IMU inside the HMD samples linear acceleration, angular velocity, and magnetic heading at **1000 Hz**. 2. **Pose Prediction:** A Kalman filter fuses IMU + camera data to estimate the head's pose at the *exact* time the next image will appear on the display, compensating for future delay. 3. **Stereo View Matrix:** Two camera transforms are computed — one for each eye — separated by the user's **IPD** along the x-axis. 4. **GPU Render:** The VR engine renders the scene twice (once per eye) using the new view matrices, applying lighting, shadows, and physics. 5. **Lens Distortion Correction:** Because the display is viewed through a magnifying lens, the image is pre-distorted (pincushion) so that after passing through the lens it appears rectilinear. 6. **Display Scan-Out:** The corrected stereo pair is sent to the HMD's micro-displays. 7. **User Retina Fusion:** The brain fuses the two slightly offset images into a single percept with depth. **Importance of Motion-to-Photon Latency:** - **Definition:** Time elapsed between the user's physical head motion and the corresponding updated image appearing on the retina. - **Why Critical:** If latency exceeds ~20 ms, the visual scene lags behind the vestibular system (inner ear), causing **simulator sickness / VR sickness** with symptoms of nausea, eye strain, and disorientation. - **Threshold:** **≤ 20 ms** (industry standard); **≤ 11 ms** preferred for high-end VR. $$ L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{predict}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}} \leq 20\,\text{ms} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **1. Hardware Selection:** | Component | Recommended Choice | Justification | |---|---|---| | **HMD** | Meta Quest 3 (standalone) or HTC Vive Pro 2 (PC-tethered) | 6-DoF inside-out tracking, 90–120 Hz refresh, ≤ 11 ms latency | | **Tracking Method** | Inside-Out (SLAM) — no base stations | Easier setup in factory floor; no occlusion from metal structures | | **Haptic Devices** | Force-feedback controllers (e.g., Meta Touch) + vibration motors | Simulate torch weight and weld bead contact | | **Auxiliary** | Wireless adapter, anti-fatigue mat, hygienic face covers | Operator comfort for 8-hour training sessions | **2. Software Stack:** | Layer | Tool | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **CAD Format** | STEP AP-242 or JT | Vendor-neutral, preserves PMI and assembly structure | | **Real-Time Engine** | Unity 2023 LTS + XR Interaction Toolkit, or Unreal Engine 5 | High-fidelity rendering, C# / Blueprint scripting | | **Middleware** | OpenXR | Hardware abstraction across multiple HMD vendors | | **Collaboration** | NVIDIA Omniverse or Mozilla Hubs | Multi-user VR for trainer + trainee sessions | | **LMS Integration** | SCORM 2004 packaging | Track trainee progress and certification | **3. Two Training Scenarios:** 1. **First-Time Weld Scenario:** Trainee wears HMD, sees the robotic welding cell in 3D. Virtual instructor guides them through safety checks (PPE, gas flow, earth clamp). Trainee practices striking an arc on a virtual plate — wrong angle triggers a haptic vibration + visual fault indicator (spatter, porosity). 2. **Fault-Recovery Scenario:** A simulated fault occurs mid-weld (wire feed jam, gas starvation). Trainee must safely stop the robot, identify the fault using the in-HMD diagnostic overlay, perform a virtual reset, and resume — all without risk to real equipment. Performance is logged for assessment. --- ## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks in Virtual Reality Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing VR with AR/MR** — Mixing definitions costs 2–3 marks immediately. Always begin with the *full* form and *one-line formal definition*. > 2. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Writing "FOV = 85" instead of "FOV = 85°" loses the unit mark. > 3. **Forgetting the latency budget equation** — Many students write the components but forget the summation formula $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}}$. Examiners expect this exact form. > 4. **No diagram in 7-mark questions** — A labelled block diagram (even hand-drawn) is **mandatory** for any "explain components" question. Omitting it loses at least 2 marks. > 5. **Wrong inverse-trigonometric calculation** — Always use `arctan`, not `tan⁻¹` followed by arithmetic errors. Show each step explicitly. > 6. **Missing the "Comment" part** — In FOV calculations, examiners often ask "comment on suitability." Students compute the number and stop — losing 1–2 easy marks. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Virtual Reality (Module 4)** - ✅ **Definition:** VR = immersive, interactive, computer-generated 3D environment perceived via specialized hardware. - ✅ **Four Components:** (1) Computer/Engine, (2) Display (HMD/CAVE), (3) Tracking & Input, (4) Software Stack. - ✅ **5-Tier Immersion Hierarchy:** Non-immersive → Fish Tank → Semi-immersive → Fully Immersive → CAVE. - ✅ **3-DoF vs 6-DoF:** 3-DoF = rotation only; 6-DoF = rotation + translation. Industrial VR needs **6-DoF**. - ✅ **Critical Metrics:** ≥ 90 Hz refresh, ≤ 20 ms latency, 90°–120° FOV, 54–74 mm IPD. - ✅ **FOV Formula:** $\text{FOV} = 2\arctan(w/2f)$ — practice with at least two numerical values. - ✅ **Frame Time:** $T_f = 1/f$ — 90 Hz → 11.11 ms. - ✅ **Latency Budget:** $L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{render}} + L_{\text{display}} \leq 20\,\text{ms}$. - ✅ **Stereo Disparity:** $\theta = 2\arctan(d/2D)$ — disparity grows as object comes nearer. - ✅ **Pipeline Order:** Sensor → Predict → Stereo Render → Distort → Display → Fusion. - ✅ **VR vs AR vs MR vs XR:** Be ready to tabulate this in one line each. - ✅ **CAD/CAM Applications:** DMU review, virtual commissioning, operator training, ergonomic analysis, factory layout planning. - ✅ **Tracking Technologies:** Outside-In (lighthouse), Inside-Out (SLAM), Electromagnetic, Optical — know the comparison table. - ✅ **Manufacturing Examples:** Virtual commissioning saves 30–60 % plant commissioning time; remember to cite one example in long answers. - ✅ **Always draw the block diagram** in 7-mark "explain" questions — labelled, with arrows showing data flow. - ✅ **Always include units** in numerical answers (Hz, ms, mm, degrees). > **End of Module 4 — Virtual Reality Notes** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — PBAUT504 Computer Aided Design and Manufacturing*
